context.offerings: AI/GPU compute (SageMaker, Bedrock, EC2 GPU, EKS), general compute + storage (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, EBS), broad first-party managed services, and serverless/edge (Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFront, WAF).
context.use_cases: Model training & inference endpoints, batch jobs, general application workloads, dev/prod environments across many teams.
context.assets_at_stake: The organization's own proprietary IP — source code, model weights, internal documents (sensitive). Blast radius is IP theft / exfiltration.
context.trust_boundary: Multi-team organization — many accounts under a single AWS Organization. The AWS account is the strong isolation unit.
context.comparison_baseline: Well-architected security best practice (AWS Well-Architected Security Pillar / CIS AWS Foundations). AWS is evaluated on its own merits as an absolute deep-dive — not against another provider. AWS is normally THIS framework's hyperscaler baseline; here it is the subject.

visibility.probe: Control-plane vs application-log separation (CloudTrail management events vs CloudWatch Logs app stdout)
visibility.finding: AWS structurally separates admin/control-plane activity from application output. CloudTrail records management events (control-plane API calls like AttachRolePolicy, CreateTrail, plus the non-API ConsoleLogin sign-in event) in a dedicated, structured JSON audit stream; application stdout/stderr lands in CloudWatch Logs log groups (e.g. /aws/lambda/{function-name}). These are different services with different schemas, retention models, and IAM controls, so an app cannot pollute or evade the audit trail by writing to stdout. Management events are logged by default on every trail and event data store; data events (data-plane operations) are explicitly NOT logged by default.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-concepts.html ("By default, trails and event data stores log management events, but not data or Insights events"; management events = control plane, e.g. AttachRolePolicy, plus the non-API ConsoleLogin event)
visibility.gap: The two streams are not unified by default. There is no single default-on pane that joins 'who did this admin action' (CloudTrail) with 'what the app then logged' (CloudWatch Logs); correlation requires deliberate pipeline work (CloudTrail to CloudWatch Logs, or both into Security Lake/CloudTrail Lake). For a multi-team org chasing an IP-exfil incident, stitching control-plane and app-plane timelines is an opt-in engineering task, not a turnkey capability.
visibility.risk: Incident responders must pre-build correlation infrastructure; absent it, joining an attacker's IAM/API actions to the resulting application behavior across many member accounts is slow and manual during an active proprietary-IP incident.

visibility.probe: CloudTrail default-on: Event history (90-day console view) vs a configured Trail (durable export)
visibility.finding: Two distinct things share the 'CloudTrail' name. (1) Event history is on automatically for every account: a viewable, searchable, downloadable, immutable record of the past 90 days of MANAGEMENT events per Region, free, no setup. (2) A Trail is a separate, opt-in configuration that delivers events to an S3 bucket (optionally CloudWatch Logs / EventBridge) for retention beyond 90 days, cross-account aggregation, and export. Without a configured trail you have only the 90-day rolling console/LookupEvents window, and Event history shows management events only (no data events, ever).
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-concepts.html ("CloudTrail event history provides a viewable, searchable, downloadable, and immutable record of the past 90 days of CloudTrail management events in an AWS Region"; "A trail is a configuration that enables delivery of CloudTrail events to an S3 bucket")
visibility.gap: The default-on layer is a 90-day rolling buffer of management events only. Long-term retention, export to SIEM, and any data-event capture all require explicitly creating a trail (or event data store). A brand-new account or a freshly-vended member account that nobody configured retains nothing past 90 days and captures no object-level access. This is the canonical 'familiar term implies more than it delivers' footgun: people say 'CloudTrail is on by default' and assume durable, exportable, complete logging.
visibility.risk: A newly created member account in the org without org-trail coverage silently loses all audit history after 90 days and never records S3/Bedrock/SageMaker object access. An attacker operating in such an account leaves no durable trace.

visibility.probe: Management vs DATA events: defaults, cost, and blast radius of data events OFF by default
visibility.finding: Management events are on by default for trails/event data stores. Data events (the data plane: S3 GetObject/PutObject/DeleteObject, Lambda Invoke, DynamoDB item ops, and notably AI/ML resource access such as SageMaker InvokeEndpoint) are NOT logged by default and incur additional per-event charges due to volume. (Corrected by fact-check: the common Bedrock invocation APIs — InvokeModel, InvokeModelWithResponseStream, Converse, ConverseStream — are MANAGEMENT events and ARE logged by default at no data-event charge; only certain runtime ops are data events: InvokeModelWithBidirectionalStream, Start/GetAsyncInvoke, the Agents-for-Bedrock runtime, Retrieve/RetrieveAndGenerate, InvokeFlow.) Network activity events (VPC-endpoint API calls) are likewise off by default and billed separately. So by default AWS records who changed configuration AND the fact of a common Bedrock model invocation (principal, source IP, modelId, via the management-event stream), but NOT who read or moved the actual proprietary data or model weights — S3 object-level access is a data event — and not the prompt/response content of a model call (Bedrock model-invocation logging is the separate control for content).
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-concepts.html ("Data events are not logged by default when you create a trail or event data store. To record CloudTrail data events, you must explicitly add each resource type... Additional charges apply for logging data events."; S3 AWS::S3::Object, Lambda AWS::Lambda::Function, Bedrock and SageMaker resource types listed)
visibility.gap: The default audit posture is blind to data exfiltration. For an org whose crown jewels are source code and model weights in S3, the single most security-relevant log (object-level GetObject) is OFF by default and costs money to turn on. SageMaker InvokeEndpoint is a data event (off by default); common Bedrock invocations are management events (on by default), but even then CloudTrail records only the API call, not prompt/response content (Bedrock model-invocation logging is the separate control for content). The cost coupling means cost-conscious teams may deliberately leave the most important read-access logging disabled.
visibility.risk: Mass download of model weights or source from S3, or abuse of a model endpoint, produces zero CloudTrail evidence by default. Detection and forensics of IP theft depend entirely on someone having opted into (and paid for) data events on the right buckets/resources beforehand.

visibility.probe: Audit log schema documentation (record body + userIdentity)
visibility.finding: The CloudTrail record schema is fully and precisely documented and versioned. Mandatory top-level fields (eventVersion, eventTime, eventSource, eventName, awsRegion, sourceIPAddress, userIdentity, requestParameters, responseElements, eventID, eventType, eventCategory, recipientAccountId) are specified with 'Since' version and Optional flags; sourceIPAddress and userIdentity are marked Optional:False (always present). eventVersion (currently 1.11) follows documented major/minor compatibility rules. The userIdentity block is exhaustively documented: type, principalId, arn, accountId, accessKeyId, sessionContext (sessionIssuer for the assumed-role's parent role, mfaAuthenticated, sourceIdentity), invokedBy, onBehalfOf/credentialId for IAM Identity Center, inScopeOf for service-to-service, and tlsDetails (TLS version/cipher/host).
visibility.gap: Schema completeness is a genuine strength; the weaknesses are content-level, not documentation-level. requestParameters/responseElements are service-defined and truncated/omitted above 100 KB (1 MB only for extended-size event data stores), so large payloads are silently dropped. sourceIdentity (the original human behind a chain of assumed roles) only appears if an admin pre-configured STS to require it; without it, role-chaining can obscure the true actor. console sign-in failures hide userName as HIDDEN_DUE_TO_SECURITY_REASONS.
visibility.risk: Well-documented schema lets the org build reliable parsers, but in a heavy assume-role/role-chaining environment the true originating human may be unattributable unless sts:SourceIdentity enforcement was set up in advance.

visibility.probe: Identity capture in events (IAM principal, assumed-role session, source IP)
visibility.finding: Every event carries a userIdentity block plus a top-level sourceIPAddress (Optional:False). For temporary credentials, sessionContext.sessionIssuer records the parent role's ARN/principalId, and attributes.mfaAuthenticated records whether MFA was used. AssumedRole principalId encodes the role session name. IAM Identity Center events carry onBehalfOf (userId + identityStoreArn) and credentialId to tie back to the SSO human. invokedBy identifies AWS-service-initiated calls; inScopeOf ties service-to-service calls to the originating resource (e.g. a specific Lambda function ARN). A new invokedByDelegate field flags actions taken by product providers via delegated access.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-event-reference-user-identity.html (sessionContext, sessionIssuer, mfaAuthenticated, onBehalfOf, inScopeOf examples) and sourceIPAddress documented Optional:False in record-contents reference
visibility.gap: Identity capture is strong, with two caveats. (1) sourceIPAddress for console-originated and AWS-internal calls is the customer resource or 'AWS Internal/{#}', not always an external attacker IP. (2) Mapping an AssumedRole session back to the originating human requires either the SSO onBehalfOf data or pre-configured STS sourceIdentity; a long-lived shared role assumed by many people yields only the role and session name, not the person, unless those controls were set up.
visibility.risk: In a multi-team org where teams share roles, attribution to an individual engineer for a suspicious data access can be impossible after the fact unless sourceIdentity / Identity Center attribution was enforced beforehand.

visibility.probe: Retention & configurability (Trail-to-S3, CloudWatch Logs, CloudTrail Lake)
visibility.finding: Multiple configurable retention tiers exist. Trail-to-S3: retention governed by S3 lifecycle policy (effectively unlimited/customer-controlled). CloudWatch Logs: per-log-group retention configurable from 1 day to 10 years (3653 days) or 'Never Expire'. CloudTrail Lake event data stores are immutable collections with retention up to 3653 days (~10 years) under EXTENDABLE_RETENTION_PRICING, or up to 2557 days (~7 years) under FIXED_RETENTION_PRICING. So years-long, queryable, immutable audit retention is achievable.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-lake.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/Working-with-log-groups-and-streams.html (retention 1 day to 10 years or never expire) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/query-event-data-store-cloudtrail.html (retention up to 3653 days)
visibility.gap: Retention is capable but defaults cut the wrong way for cost/safety. CloudWatch Logs default is 'Never Expire' (unbounded cost, including for sensitive app data that may have been captured), while CloudTrail Event history default is only 90 days (too short for forensics). Neither default matches a sensible security baseline; both must be deliberately overridden per-log-group and per-trail.
visibility.risk: Without governance, the org simultaneously over-retains potentially-sensitive app logs forever (cost + data-at-rest exposure) and under-retains audit history (only 90 days where no trail/Lake is configured).

visibility.probe: Export to external SIEM (S3, EventBridge, Kinesis Data Firehose, CloudTrail Lake, Security Lake / OCSF)
visibility.finding: Rich, well-documented export paths exist. Trails deliver to S3 (batched, for SIEM pull or Firehose) and can stream to CloudWatch Logs and Amazon EventBridge for routing. CloudTrail Lake offers SQL-queryable storage. Amazon Security Lake centralizes CloudTrail management events, S3/Lambda data events, VPC Flow Logs, and more, automatically normalizing them to the open OCSF schema in the customer's S3, with subscriber access for third-party SIEMs.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-lake/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-event-logs.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-concepts.html (trail delivery to S3, CloudWatch Logs, EventBridge)
visibility.gap: All export is opt-in and assembled from multiple services. Security Lake is not enabled by default and, to collect CloudTrail management events, requires a pre-existing multi-Region organization trail; data events must be separately enabled on that trail to reach Security Lake. The OCSF normalization covers native AWS sources, but custom S3 server access logs and arbitrary app logs need separate conversion. There is no single default-on 'everything to OCSF' switch.
visibility.risk: A SIEM-centric security team inherits real integration work (trail + data events + Security Lake + subscribers) before centralized cross-account visibility exists; until then, member-account activity is siloed.

visibility.probe: Log immutability and attacker-resistance (org trail, log file validation, S3 Object Lock, Lake)
visibility.finding: Several immutability mechanisms exist. CloudTrail log file integrity validation creates hourly digest files using SHA-256 hashing and SHA-256-with-RSA signing, each digest chaining the previous one, making undetected modification/deletion computationally infeasible. The destination S3 bucket can add Object Lock (WORM) and MFA Delete. Crucially, an ORGANIZATION trail created from the management/delegated-admin account is visible to member accounts but cannot be modified, disabled, have its event selectors changed, or deleted by them, so an attacker who compromises a single member account cannot turn off their own logging. CloudTrail Lake event data stores are immutable.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-log-file-validation-intro.html (SHA-256, digest chaining) and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/services-that-can-integrate-cloudtrail.html ("users in member accounts do not have sufficient permissions to delete organization trails, turn logging on or off, or otherwise change an organization trail")
visibility.gap: These are all opt-in and correctly-architected only if set up that way. Log file validation is not on by default; Object Lock must be enabled on the bucket. The member-account-can't-disable property holds ONLY for organization trails created centrally; a trail created locally in a member account CAN be disabled by a sufficiently-privileged principal in that account. The org-trail protection also presumes the management/delegated-admin account itself is not compromised.
visibility.risk: If the org relies on per-account trails rather than a centrally-owned org trail, an attacker with admin in a member account can stop logging and cover their tracks. Immutability is only as strong as the (opt-in) configuration choices made up front.

visibility.probe: Real-time streaming and alerting (EventBridge, CloudWatch metric filters/alarms, GuardDuty)
visibility.finding: Near-real-time alerting is supported. CloudTrail management events flow to Amazon EventBridge and (if a trail streams to CloudWatch Logs) to metric filters + CloudWatch Alarms, enabling reactions within seconds of an event. AWS documents canned CloudWatch alarm examples for CloudTrail (e.g. security group / IAM policy changes). Amazon GuardDuty consumes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to produce threat findings without the customer building detections.
visibility.gap: All of this is opt-in and per-account/per-Region. GuardDuty is NOT enabled by default; each account gets a 30-day free trial then bills per-Region, and Runtime Monitoring/EKS coverage is a further explicit opt-in. EventBridge delivers CloudTrail events into each Region's own event bus (not one global bus), so org-wide real-time detection requires fan-in plumbing across Regions and accounts. Data-event-based alerts (e.g. anomalous S3 reads) only work if data events were enabled first.
visibility.risk: Out of the box there is no real-time alert on the actions that matter most for IP theft. A multi-team org must explicitly enable GuardDuty everywhere and build EventBridge/alarm fan-in before it has live detection across the estate.

visibility.probe: Container/serverless application log retention and sensitive-data capture (Lambda, EKS/Fargate, CloudWatch Logs default)
visibility.finding: Lambda auto-creates a /aws/lambda/{function-name} log group and ships stdout there; EKS/Fargate/ECS can ship container logs to CloudWatch Logs. But the default retention for any newly created CloudWatch Logs log group, including auto-created Lambda groups, is 'Never Expire' (represented as a missing retentionInDays). Retention is configurable (1 day to 10 years) but must be set explicitly per log group. The account owner pays storage indefinitely. Application logs may contain sensitive data (secrets, IP, PII) and CloudWatch Logs does not redact by default.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/Working-with-log-groups-and-streams.html (new log groups default to Never Expire) and Lambda auto-creates /aws/lambda/{function-name} log groups
visibility.gap: Two coupled footguns. (1) Unbounded default retention means proprietary data accidentally logged by an app lives forever in CloudWatch Logs at the org's cost and expands the attack surface (anyone with logs:GetLogEvents can read it). (2) There is no default-on data-protection/redaction; CloudWatch Logs data protection (sensitive-data masking) is an explicit opt-in per log group. App logs are also governed by CloudWatch Logs IAM, separate from the CloudTrail audit IAM, so 'who can read the app logs containing IP' is its own access-control problem.
visibility.risk: Across many teams' Lambdas/containers, sensitive IP inadvertently written to stdout is retained forever, unredacted, readable by any principal with CloudWatch Logs read permission, growing the blast radius of any credential compromise and the cost base simultaneously.

visibility.probe: AI/ML application-layer content logging (Bedrock model invocation logs; prompt/response capture)
visibility.finding: For AI workloads there is a content-level log distinct from CloudTrail. Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging captures full request data, response data, and metadata (prompts and completions, input/output JSON bodies up to 100 KB) to CloudWatch Logs and/or S3 - but it is DISABLED by default and must be explicitly enabled per Region. CloudTrail records the Bedrock API call (management/data event) but not the prompt/response content. SageMaker similarly separates control-plane (CloudTrail) from endpoint data and any model-output capture.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-invocation-logging.html ("model invocation logging is disabled by default"; captures model input/output data to CloudWatch Logs and S3)
visibility.gap: The content of AI interactions, the prompts that may carry proprietary data and the model outputs, is invisible by default. Enabling Bedrock invocation logging is itself double-edged: it then writes potentially highly sensitive prompt/response content into CloudWatch Logs/S3 where (per the container-log finding) default retention is Never Expire and there is no default redaction. So the org chooses between no visibility into model usage or a long-lived, sensitive content store that itself becomes an IP-exposure target.
visibility.risk: Without invocation logging the org cannot detect prompt-based exfiltration of IP through Bedrock/SageMaker; with it enabled naively, the prompt/response store becomes a concentrated, indefinitely-retained repository of the very secrets it was meant to monitor.

visibility.probe: GuardDuty default-on baseline vs the per-plan opt-in protection plans (which feeders are actually on after a vanilla org enablement)
visibility.finding: When GuardDuty is first enabled in a Region, it auto-enables most supported protection types INCLUDING Malware Protection for EC2 (agentless EBS snapshot scanning) and S3 Protection, all inside a 30-day free trial, and you can opt out. Extended Threat Detection and the foundational data sources (CloudTrail mgmt events, VPC Flow Logs, Route 53 DNS query logs) are also on by default with no separate enablement. The single critical exception is RUNTIME MONITORING (covering EKS, ECS-Fargate, and EC2 host/container runtime), which is NOT enabled by default and is the only plan excluded from the default free-trial activation. So a vanilla 'enable GuardDuty org-wide' gives you control-plane/network/DNS detections and agentless EBS malware scanning, but ZERO runtime (in-container/in-host) visibility until you separately turn on Runtime Monitoring and deploy its agent. Getting-started doc: 'When you enable GuardDuty for the first time in any AWS Region, by default, it also enables all the available protection types that are supported in that Region, including Malware Protection for EC2 ... [protection plans] except Runtime Monitoring are automatically enabled and included in the initial 30-day free trial.'
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_settingup.html — 'When you enable GuardDuty for the first time in any AWS Region, by default, it also enables all the available protection types that are supported in that Region, including Malware Protection for EC2'; and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/runtime-monitoring.html (Runtime Monitoring requires a GuardDuty security agent and is configured separately).
visibility.gap: The control that catches the highest-value attacks for this org — runtime container escape, crypto-mining on GPU fleets, model-weight exfiltration from inside a workload — is the ONE plan that is silent by default. An org can believe it has 'GuardDuty enabled everywhere' (true) and still have no runtime detection at all. Malware Protection for S3 OBJECT scanning (distinct from agentless EBS scanning) is also a separate per-bucket opt-in, billed separately, so uploaded-object malware on IP/document buckets is unscanned unless explicitly turned on per bucket.
visibility.risk: Container escape from an EKS/ECS workload to the underlying GPU host, cryptojacking of the GPU fleet, and process-level exfiltration of model weights or source code all occur with no GuardDuty finding generated — because the runtime feeder plan was never enabled. The org's most sensitive blast radius (proprietary IP on compute) is exactly the part left dark by the defaults.

visibility.probe: Org-wide enablement ergonomics: delegated admin + auto-enable for new accounts is per-plan, per-Region — does turning GuardDuty on org-wide actually turn the plans on org-wide?
visibility.finding: GuardDuty supports a delegated administrator via AWS Organizations and an auto-enable setting with three modes per the org config API: NEW (auto-enable only for accounts that join later), ALL (back-fill every existing account), or NONE. Critically, auto-enable is configured INDEPENDENTLY for GuardDuty itself AND for each optional protection plan (S3 Protection, EKS Protection, RDS Protection, Runtime Monitoring, etc.), and GuardDuty is a Regional service — every configuration step 'must be repeated in each Region you want to monitor.' The legacy console default 'Auto-enable' tip only applies to accounts that join AFTER the toggle is set, not existing members, unless you explicitly choose ALL. So the matrix to get full coverage is [each plan] x [NEW/ALL] x [every region] x [delegated-admin enforced], and a partial setup (e.g., auto-enable GuardDuty=ALL but Runtime Monitoring auto-enable left at NONE) silently produces accounts with the core service on but the runtime feeder off.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_organizations.html and the org config API https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/APIReference/API_DescribeOrganizationConfiguration.html (AutoEnable values NEW/ALL/NONE set independently per feature); 'GuardDuty is a Regional service ... configuration procedures ... must be repeated in each Region' — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_settingup.html
visibility.gap: There is no single 'enable everything everywhere' switch. Achieving complete coverage requires N-plans x M-regions x correct auto-enable mode, plus back-filling existing accounts with ALL (the default NEW leaves the current fleet untouched). This is precisely the kind of multi-dimensional config where a multi-team org ends up with inconsistent coverage — some accounts/regions/plans on, others off — and no built-in attestation that the matrix is complete. Drift is invisible without external tooling (Security Hub controls, Config rules, or scripting ListDetectors per region).
visibility.risk: A team spins up GPU/EKS workloads in a region or account where Runtime Monitoring auto-enable was never set, and the workload is monitored only at the control-plane level. Coverage gaps track account/region sprawl, which is exactly how fast-moving teams holding proprietary IP create undetected exposure. The org may pass a 'GuardDuty is enabled' audit check while the runtime plans are off in the accounts that matter most.

visibility.probe: GuardDuty Extended Threat Detection (attack-sequence findings) — default-on but coverage is entirely gated on the feeder plans being enabled
visibility.finding: Extended Threat Detection (ETD) is enabled by default for every GuardDuty account at no extra cost and correlates multi-stage attacks across foundational data sources, AWS resources, and a 24-hour window into Critical-severity attack-sequence findings. The console even shows Status: Enabled out of the box. BUT AWS is explicit that 'its detection comprehensiveness depends on your enabled GuardDuty protection plans.' Concretely: EKS attack sequences (AttackSequence:EKS/CompromisedCluster) require EKS Protection OR Runtime Monitoring; ECS attack sequences REQUIRE Runtime Monitoring for Fargate or EC2 (no fallback); EC2 attack sequences are only enhanced beyond control-plane/network signal when Runtime Monitoring is on; S3 data-compromise sequences require S3 Protection. Doc: 'If you don't enable EKS Protection or Runtime Monitoring, GuardDuty will not be able to generate individual ... finding types. Therefore, GuardDuty will not be able to detect multi-stage attack sequences that involve associated findings.' GA timeline: foundational ETD Dec 2024, EKS Jun 2025, EC2 + ECS Dec 2025.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty-extended-threat-detection.html — 'other attack sequences require advanced signals that are only available from advanced protection plans such as Runtime monitoring, S3 protection and EKS Audit Log Monitoring' and 'Extended Threat Detection for ECS requires Runtime Monitoring for Fargate or EC2'; EC2/ECS GA announcement https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2025/12/guardduty-extended-threat-detection-ec2-ecs/ ('automatically enabled ... at no additional cost, its detection comprehensiveness depends on your enabled GuardDuty protection plans').
visibility.gap: The headline 'Extended Threat Detection: Enabled' status is true even when every advanced feeder plan is off — in which case the only attack sequence it can produce is the IAM-on-S3-control-plane scenario from foundational sources. The container/runtime attack sequences (the ones that catch the EKS/ECS/EC2-GPU compromise this org cares about) light up ONLY if Runtime Monitoring / EKS Protection / S3 Protection are individually enabled. The 'Enabled' badge implies a level of correlation that the account does not actually have, and there is no warning that ETD is running near-empty.
visibility.risk: A multi-team org with EKS/GPU workloads sees 'Extended Threat Detection: Enabled' and assumes its crown-jewel container/instance workloads are covered by attack-sequence correlation. In reality, a container-escape-to-cryptomining-to-exfiltration chain on a GPU fleet produces NO AttackSequence finding unless Runtime Monitoring was separately enabled and its agent deployed — so the single most sophisticated detection capability is silently inert exactly where the proprietary IP lives.

visibility.probe: Security Hub as the multi-account posture brain: CSPM aggregation, security standards/scores, ASFF/OCSF normalization, cross-account + cross-region aggregation, and auto-enable for new org accounts
visibility.finding: Security Hub provides genuine org-wide posture management: a delegated administrator aggregates findings across accounts, normalizes them to ASFF (and, in the new OCSF-based 'Security Hub' GA'd at re:Inforce 2025, correlates GuardDuty/Inspector/Macie/CSPM signals into 'exposure findings'), runs standards (AWS FSBP, CIS, PCI, NIST) and computes security scores. But almost nothing is default-on. Per AWS: 'By default, new accounts don't become Security Hub CSPM members when they join the organization. Their status is Not a member.' Auto-enable must be explicitly turned on (UpdateOrganizationConfiguration AutoEnable=true, or a central-configuration policy associated with the org root/OUs). Cross-Region aggregation is also opt-in: you must designate a home/aggregation Region and select linked Regions, and 'Security Hub CSPM is not automatically enabled for an account based on the cross-Region aggregation configuration' — so findings are siloed per-region until configured. Control findings additionally require AWS Config to be enabled and recording the required resource types in every account/region (a separate paid prerequisite). Cross-region replication itself is free; the underlying service, Config, and per-finding ingestion are billed.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/accounts-orgs-auto-enable.html ("By default, new accounts don't become Security Hub CSPM members when they join the organization. Their status is Not a member."); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/finding-aggregation.html ("Security Hub CSPM is not automatically enabled for an account based on the cross-Region aggregation configuration.")
visibility.gap: For a multi-team org under one AWS Organization, the only service that aggregates posture across accounts is fully opt-in and assembled from several independently-toggled switches (designate delegated admin, enable auto-enable or a central-config policy, designate a home Region + linked Regions, enable AWS Config recording everywhere). Miss any one and you get a partial picture that looks complete: a new team's account silently sits at 'Not a member', and a workload in an un-linked region (e.g. a GPU fleet stood up in us-west-2 when the home Region is us-east-1) produces findings nobody sees in the aggregated view. The 2025 repackaging adds a doc-quality hazard: 'Security Hub' now denotes both the new OCSF correlation layer and the renamed 'Security Hub CSPM' (ASFF), so a phrase like 'Security Hub is enabled' is ambiguous about which product, which schema, and which capabilities are actually on.
visibility.risk: Posture scoring and exposure correlation for proprietary-IP workloads can be silently incomplete across the org — unmonitored member accounts and unlinked regions are invisible in the central dashboard even though they hold source code, model weights, and sensitive S3 data. The naming collision makes it easy to believe full coverage exists when only the legacy CSPM checks (or only one region) are wired up.

visibility.probe: Vuln + sensitive-data discovery layer beyond GuardDuty: Inspector (continuous CVE/EOL for EC2/ECR/Lambda, vs ECR basic Clair scanning), Macie (sensitive-data/IP discovery in S3), Detective (graph investigation) — enablement, delegation, and auto-on for new accounts
visibility.finding: All three exist, are org-integrated via a delegated administrator, and can auto-enable new accounts — but each is opt-in, paid, and regional, and each carries a footgun. Inspector: once activated, all scan types (EC2, ECR, Lambda standard, Lambda code, Code Security) are on by default; it continuously rescans on new-package install, patch, and newly-published CVE. This is materially stronger than ECR's 'basic scanning,' which uses the open-source Clair DB, covers only OS packages, only scans on push/manual, and never auto-rescans against new CVEs — so an AMI/image flagged clean under basic scanning silently accrues unflagged CVEs over time. ECR 'enhanced scanning' IS Inspector (billed through Inspector, not ECR). Macie: the whole service is off until enabled; automated sensitive-data discovery is a further separate (paid) layer. In an org, the admin's autoEnableOrganizationMembers defaults to ALL once the org is enabled, but nothing runs until an admin explicitly turns Macie + automated discovery on per region. Detective: opt-in, graph-based investigation, with a hard prerequisite — 'make sure that your account has been enrolled in Amazon GuardDuty for at least 48 hours' or you cannot enable it; it can auto-enable new org accounts only after delegation.
visibility.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/getting_started_tutorial.html ("When you activate Amazon Inspector for a standalone account, all scan types are activated by default"); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/image-scanning-enhanced.html (enhanced scanning = Amazon Inspector, continuous); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-account-enable.html (automated discovery is separately enabled; autoEnableOrganizationMembers ALL/NEW/NONE); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/detective/latest/adminguide/detective-enabling.html (48-hour GuardDuty prerequisite)
visibility.gap: Versus well-architected best practice, the brain of detection/posture (the answers to 'do our AMIs/containers carry known CVEs', 'is our IP sitting in an unencrypted/public bucket', and 'show me the blast radius of this account') is four separate opt-in subscriptions, each enabled per-region, each delegated independently, and each metered. The dangerous default is ECR basic scanning: it is the cheap, obvious-looking option, but its Clair OS-only, no-continuous-rescan model gives false comfort that container images are 'scanned.' Macie's two-step enablement (service, then automated discovery) means an org can have Macie 'on' while no bucket is actually being profiled for sensitive data. Detective's 48-hour GuardDuty gate means investigation tooling cannot be stood up reactively at incident time. New regions and new accounts are not covered unless auto-enable/org policy was configured in advance for each service.
visibility.risk: An org protecting proprietary IP can believe it has CVE coverage (while relying on ECR basic scanning that never rescans), believe it has data-loss visibility (while Macie automated discovery was never switched on), and discover at incident time that Detective is unusable because the relevant account wasn't in GuardDuty 48 hours earlier. Model weights or source code in a mis-scoped or newly-created S3 bucket/account can go unscanned and unprofiled with no signal that coverage is missing.

identity_granularity.probe: Action-level granularity and explicit Deny: can a policy allow ec2:RebootInstances while denying ec2:RunInstances/ec2:TerminateInstances?
identity_granularity.finding: IAM is genuinely action-level. Each API action is an independent string in the Action element, and explicit Deny always overrides Allow in the evaluation logic. A policy with Allow on ec2:RebootInstances (scoped to a specific instance ARN) plus an explicit Deny on ec2:RunInstances and ec2:TerminateInstances is valid and enforceable. The Service Authorization Reference lists every action as a discrete entity with its supported resource types and condition keys, so the litmus test passes cleanly. RebootInstances/StartInstances/StopInstances/TerminateInstances each support the 'instance' resource type for ARN-level scoping.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/service-authorization/latest/reference/list_amazonec2.html and policy evaluation logic: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html (explicit deny overrides allow)
identity_granularity.gap: None on the core capability — action-level granularity and explicit Deny are real and expressible. The residual concern is that wildcards (ec2:*, ec2:Describe*) are equally easy to write, so granularity is available but not enforced by default.
identity_granularity.risk: For proprietary-IP workloads, the capability exists to write tightly-scoped operator roles (reboot but not destroy GPU instances). The risk is organizational discipline, not a platform limitation.

identity_granularity.probe: Service Authorization Reference + resource-level permission coverage: is least privilege actually WRITABLE per-action, or do resource-level gaps undermine it?
identity_granularity.finding: AWS publishes a per-service Service Authorization Reference enumerating every action, the resource types it supports, condition keys, and dependent actions — this is best-in-class documentation for writing least-privilege policies. However, resource-level permission support is uneven: many actions (notably Describe*/List* across EC2, S3 ListAllMyBuckets, and numerous service actions) do NOT support resource-level permissions and must be granted on Resource: '*'. The reference itself marks these gaps, but writing least privilege requires splitting policies into resource-scoped and wildcard-only statements, which is error-prone.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/service-authorization/latest/reference/reference_policies_actions-resources-contextkeys.html and EC2 list showing actions without resource types: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/service-authorization/latest/reference/list_amazonec2.html
identity_granularity.gap: Least privilege is writable in principle but ergonomically hard: every action's resource-level support must be checked individually, Describe/List actions force '*', and there is no single 'deny all describe except X' primitive. A multi-team org must invest real engineering to author scoped policies per service.
identity_granularity.risk: Teams default to broad managed policies (e.g. AmazonEC2FullAccess) or '*' Describe grants because precise authoring is laborious, widening the blast radius for any compromised role with access to model weights or source in S3.

identity_granularity.probe: ec2:RunInstances least-privilege ergonomics: the multi-resource-type footgun
identity_granularity.finding: ec2:RunInstances is a single action that requires the caller to hold permissions across up to twelve distinct resource types simultaneously — instance, image (AMI), subnet, network-interface, security-group, and volume (required), plus key-pair, elastic-gpu, capacity-reservation, placement-group, launch-template, and license-configuration (optional); spot-instances-request belongs to other Spot actions, not RunInstances. To write a least-privilege launch policy you must enumerate ARNs (or wildcards) for ALL of them in the Resource element; omitting one breaks the launch. AWS's own 'Demystifying EC2 Resource-Level Permissions' blog resorts to wildcards for key pair, security group, volume, and AMI to keep the example tractable.
identity_granularity.gap: Constraining which AMI, subnet, or security group GPU instances may launch with requires a sprawling multi-statement policy; the path of least resistance (AWS's own example) wildcards most resources, defeating the point. ABAC via condition keys (aws:RequestTag, ec2:Subnet) helps but adds further complexity.
identity_granularity.risk: Compute teams provisioning GPU/SageMaker capacity commonly get over-broad RunInstances grants, enabling launch of instances in unintended subnets or with unintended IAM instance profiles — a path to lateral movement toward model-weight stores.

identity_granularity.probe: Credential inventory: distinct credential types, plane, transmission, expiration
identity_granularity.finding: AWS supports a broad, well-differentiated credential taxonomy: (1) root user (full control + billing, cannot be permission-bounded); (2) IAM user long-lived access keys (no built-in expiry, manual rotation); (3) IAM roles via STS temporary credentials (AccessKeyId+SecretAccessKey+SessionToken, time-boxed); (4) EC2 instance profile creds (auto-delivered via IMDS); (5) IRSA and EKS Pod Identity for Kubernetes; (6) IAM Roles Anywhere X.509; (7) federated SAML/OIDC sessions; (8) Cognito identity pool creds. All data-plane API calls are authenticated with SigV4-signed request headers (not URL query params for normal use), so credentials are not exposed in URLs. STS-derived creds default to 1 hour, configurable up to 12 hours.
identity_granularity.gap: The taxonomy is rich but the two highest-risk types (root, long-lived access keys) remain fully supported with weak defaults — no forced expiry on access keys, and root keys can still be created. The breadth itself is a footgun: each type has different rotation/revocation semantics teams must learn.
identity_granularity.risk: A multi-team org inevitably accumulates a mix; the long-lived-key subset is the dominant AWS breach vector and is the easiest credential to create.

identity_granularity.probe: Long-lived IAM access keys and root credentials: still supported, weak defaults
identity_granularity.finding: IAM user long-lived access keys are fully supported and have NO expiration — they remain valid until manually deactivated/deleted. Rotation is a manual or self-built process; AWS provides no native auto-rotation for IAM user keys (only guidance and a credential report showing key age/last-used). Root user access keys can still technically be created, though AWS strongly advises against them and now blocks/discourages via console. AWS documents these as anti-patterns but the capability and the easy 'Create access key' path persist.
identity_granularity.gap: Versus well-architected best practice, the gap is default posture: keys never expire, rotation is manual, and there is no org-wide enforced max-key-age (must be built with SCPs + automation + Access Analyzer). The secure path (no keys, use roles/Identity Center) is opt-in.
identity_granularity.risk: Leaked static keys (in code, CI, model-training scripts) grant standing access to proprietary IP indefinitely; absent automation, a multi-team org will have stale keys it cannot easily inventory across accounts.

identity_granularity.probe: Short-lived credential mechanisms: STS AssumeRole, web identity, session duration, role chaining
identity_granularity.finding: STS provides AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, and AssumeRoleWithSAML, all returning temporary credentials. Default session is 3600s (1h); MaxSessionDuration is configurable on the role up to 43200s (12h). Crucially, role chaining (assuming a role from already-temporary creds) is hard-capped at 1 hour regardless of MaxSessionDuration — a non-obvious limit. Session policies and policy ARNs can be passed at assume-time to further scope a session (effective permissions = intersection of identity policy ∩ permission boundary ∩ session policy).
identity_granularity.gap: Strong mechanism. The footgun is the 12h max combined with role chaining's silent 1h cap — long-lived sessions and chained sessions behave differently, and operators are surprised when chained sessions can't exceed an hour. 12h sessions also widen the window if a session token leaks.
identity_granularity.risk: For sensitive workloads, 12h sessions reduce rotation friction but extend the exfiltration window on token compromise; the chaining cap can break automation if not anticipated.

identity_granularity.probe: Workload identity for EC2/ECS/Lambda, IRSA, and EKS Pod Identity (late-2023)
identity_granularity.finding: First-class workload identity is broad: EC2 instance profiles, ECS task roles, and Lambda execution roles deliver role creds with no static secrets. For EKS, IRSA (OIDC-provider-based, per-cluster trust-policy edits, subject to per-account OIDC-provider and trust-policy-size limits) is now complemented by EKS Pod Identity (re:Invent 2023), which uses a cluster-agnostic service principal (pods.eks.amazonaws.com) requiring sts:AssumeRole + sts:TagSession and auto-injects session tags (eks-cluster-arn, kubernetes-namespace, kubernetes-service-account, kubernetes-pod-name) usable in ABAC conditions. Pod Identity also supports session policies for per-pod scoping.
identity_granularity.gap: Two coexisting EKS mechanisms (IRSA vs Pod Identity) with different trust models is a discoverability/decision burden; IRSA's auto-injected session tags are absent (Pod Identity added them), so migration is needed to get ABAC-by-pod. EC2 instance-profile creds are delivered via IMDS — IMDSv1 (the SSRF-exploitable version) is still available unless IMDSv2 is enforced (now default on newer launches/AMIs but enforceable org-wide only via configuration).
identity_granularity.risk: Mixed IRSA/Pod Identity estates have inconsistent ABAC capability; IMDSv1 left enabled on GPU/training instances is a classic SSRF-to-credential-theft path toward model weights.

identity_granularity.probe: Off-platform OIDC federation (GitHub Actions) trust-policy claim granularity and the sub-claim footgun
identity_granularity.finding: AWS supports keyless CI via AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity against GitHub's OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com), with an OIDC discovery endpoint and trust policies that condition on the 'sub' and 'aud' claims. Correctly scoped, the sub claim pins repo + ref (repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main). The footgun: the sub claim format CHANGES when a job uses a GitHub environment (becomes repo:org/repo:environment:Name, dropping the branch), and pull_request runs yield sub ...:pull_request. Using StringLike with repo:org/* (or matching only aud=sts.amazonaws.com) is dangerously permissive — any repo/branch/fork in the org can assume the role. AWS's own blog shows a scoped example but does NOT warn about the wildcard/environment pitfalls.
identity_granularity.gap: The trust-policy authoring surface lets you build an over-broad trust unknowingly: conditioning on aud only, or StringLike repo:org/*, passes silently. AWS docs demonstrate the secure form without flagging the insecure forms, and the environment/pull_request sub-claim variance is documented on GitHub's side, not AWS's.
identity_granularity.risk: An over-broad GitHub OIDC trust lets any workflow in the org (including from a forked PR in some configs) assume a deploy role and reach S3 model/source buckets — a direct supply-chain-to-IP path.

identity_granularity.probe: IAM Roles Anywhere: X.509-based credentials for off-platform workloads
identity_granularity.finding: IAM Roles Anywhere lets non-AWS workloads obtain temporary IAM credentials using X.509 client certificates. You register a trust anchor (AWS Private CA or external CA cert) and a profile; the role trust policy must trust rolesanywhere.amazonaws.com. The CreateSession call is signed with an extended SigV4 scheme (AWS4-X509-RSA-SHA256 / AWS4-X509-ECDSA-SHA256); the private key never leaves the host (handled by aws_signing_helper via credential_process), and the returned creds are standard short-lived STS credentials. Certificate attributes can be mapped into session via condition keys.
identity_granularity.gap: Operationally heavy: requires running a CA and managing cert issuance/revocation (CRL upload to Roles Anywhere). Mis-scoped trust anchors or absent CRL handling can leave revoked certs usable until CRL refresh. It is a strong primitive but the security depends entirely on the customer's PKI hygiene.
identity_granularity.risk: For on-prem/hybrid training infrastructure reaching AWS-hosted model weights, this eliminates static keys — a strength — but a weak external CA or missed revocation becomes the new trust root.

identity_granularity.probe: Official AWS MCP servers and self-service read-only tokens
identity_granularity.finding: AWS ships official open-source MCP servers under awslabs/mcp (AWS API MCP Server, IAM MCP Server, Serverless, etc.). These honor the standard boto3 credential chain (AWS profile, env vars, or instance/Pod roles) rather than introducing a new credential type. Read-only operation is supported two ways: the READ_OPERATIONS_ONLY / --readonly server flags, AND — more importantly — scoping the underlying IAM principal (e.g. the AWS-managed ReadOnlyAccess policy or a custom read-only role). AWS documentation explicitly states IAM permissions remain the primary security control, not the server flag. A user with permission to assume a read-only role can self-service inspect resources without admin intervention.
identity_granularity.gap: There is no OAuth/OIDC consent flow purpose-built for MCP tools — the servers ride on whatever ambient AWS credentials are configured, so a misconfigured profile (admin keys in env) gives the MCP server admin power despite a --readonly flag. The managed ReadOnlyAccess policy is broad (grants read across nearly all services incl. data-plane reads like S3 GetObject), so 'read-only' can still mean 'read your model weights'.
identity_granularity.risk: An MCP-driven agent pointed at an over-privileged profile can read proprietary S3/Bedrock/SageMaker artifacts; the --readonly flag is a soft control that does not constrain data-plane reads if the IAM principal allows them.

identity_granularity.probe: Composable scoping primitives: session policies, session tags, source identity, permission boundaries, ABAC
identity_granularity.finding: AWS offers a strong, composable set: (1) session policies passed at AssumeRole-time (session-duration scoping, effective perms = intersection); (2) permission boundaries (an upper bound on what an identity-based policy can grant, distinct from SCPs); (3) session tags + sts:SourceIdentity, where SourceIdentity persists across chained role sessions and is recorded in CloudTrail and enforceable via the aws:SourceIdentity condition key; (4) ABAC via aws:PrincipalTag / aws:RequestTag / aws:ResourceTag conditions. These are the correct standards-based scoping primitives (token exchange + session-time scoping + propagated, logged metadata) rather than a bespoke 'agent identity' type.
identity_granularity.gap: Power comes with sharp edges: effective-permission computation is the intersection of identity policy, permission boundary, session policy, SCP, and resource policy — five layers that interact non-obviously, making 'why is this denied/allowed' hard to reason about. Source identity is opt-in (must be required via trust-policy condition) and is not propagated by default. Permission boundaries are frequently confused with SCPs.
identity_granularity.risk: The richness enables precise, auditable agent/workload scoping (the right model for AI agents touching IP), but the multi-layer evaluation makes misconfiguration likely; teams that don't require sts:SourceIdentity lose attribution of which human/agent acted behind a shared role in CloudTrail.

identity_granularity.probe: Brokered secrets: Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store rotation defaults
identity_granularity.finding: AWS provides two brokers for third-party/registry credentials the platform holds: Secrets Manager (Lambda-based or managed rotation, fine-grained IAM + resource policies, KMS encryption, CloudTrail logging) and SSM Parameter Store (SecureString via KMS, free tier, versioning). Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation but it is NOT enabled by default — you must configure a rotation Lambda (or managed rotation for supported DBs) and a schedule. Parameter Store has NO built-in rotation at all; rotation must be hand-built with Lambda + EventBridge.
identity_granularity.gap: Rotation is opt-in and, for arbitrary third-party/registry creds (Docker registry, model-hub tokens), requires writing custom rotation logic — many teams store such secrets statically and never rotate them. Parameter Store SecureStrings are commonly used for secrets precisely because they're cheaper, but get zero rotation and weaker secret-specific tooling.
identity_granularity.risk: Static, unrotated registry/model-hub credentials brokered by the platform become long-lived liabilities; a compromised role with secretsmanager:GetSecretValue or ssm:GetParameter on '*' can harvest every brokered credential at once.

identity_granularity.probe: Org-wide key governance: visibility, revocation, logged creation, IAM Access Analyzer
identity_granularity.finding: Creation/deletion of access keys and role assumptions are logged as CloudTrail events (CreateAccessKey, DeleteAccessKey, AssumeRole), and per-account credential reports list key age and last-used. IAM Access Analyzer provides external-access findings (free, identifies resources shared outside the org) and unused-access findings (continuously monitors all roles/users across the AWS Organization for unused permissions/keys — but this analyzer is opt-in and PRICED per role/user/month), plus custom policy checks for CI/CD policy validation. An org admin can centralize CloudTrail (org trail) and run Access Analyzer at the org level.
identity_granularity.gap: There is no single default-on console view of 'every key/credential across every account and who can revoke it' — admins assemble it from org CloudTrail + credential reports + Access Analyzer. Unused-access detection (the feature that surfaces stale keys org-wide) is opt-in and costs money per principal, so many orgs don't run it. CloudTrail event history is only 90 days unless an org trail ships to S3.
identity_granularity.risk: In a multi-team org, stale/over-privileged keys accumulate undetected unless the org pays for and configures unused-access analyzers and an org-wide CloudTrail; cross-account credential inventory is a deliberate engineering effort, not a default.

identity_granularity.probe: KMS default key policy delegates all access to IAM ('Enable IAM User Permissions' / kms:* to account root), so a CMK does not by itself isolate data — any sufficiently broad IAM admin in the account can decrypt
identity_granularity.finding: Every KMS key created via the API/CLI/SDK without an explicit policy gets a one-statement default policy: Principal arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:root, Action kms:*, Resource *. AWS docs are explicit that the :root account principal 'doesn't give any IAM principal permission to use the KMS key. Instead, it allows the account to use IAM policies to delegate the permissions.' The console default adds the same 'Enable IAM User Permissions' statement first, then key-admin and key-user statements. Consequence: with the default policy in force, key access is governed by IAM, not the key policy — so any principal whose IAM policy allows kms:Decrypt on Resource:* (e.g. a broad account-wide admin, a PowerUser, or an over-scoped role) can decrypt anything that key protects, independent of who the 'CMK owner' team is. AWS's own least-privilege page concedes you should 'only allow all actions (kms:*) on a KMS key policy if you plan to further restrict permissions with additional IAM policies' and to 'limit who has the ability to create and attach IAM policies.' Also note the lockout safety check (PutKeyPolicy requires the policy to keep the caller able to make a future PutKeyPolicy) can be skipped with BypassPolicyLockoutSafetyCheck=true, which AWS warns 'increases the risk that the KMS key becomes unmanageable' — a self-lockout footgun in the other direction.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-default.html — 'When the principal in a key policy statement is the account principal... it allows the account to use IAM policies to delegate the permissions.' Least-privilege caveat: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/least-privilege.html . Lockout/bypass: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_PutKeyPolicy.html (BypassPolicyLockoutSafetyCheck).
identity_granularity.gap: The default-on behavior inverts the intuition that a customer-managed CMK gives per-team isolation. For a multi-team org under one Organization holding proprietary IP (model weights, source), a CMK with the stock policy provides NO isolation from any broad IAM admin in the same account — isolation only materializes if teams are split across accounts and/or the default 'Enable IAM User Permissions' statement is replaced with explicitly-named principals. AWS documents the mechanic but the well-architected expectation (CMK == isolation boundary) is not what the default delivers; nothing in the create-key flow warns that the default policy means IAM, not the key, is the boundary.
identity_granularity.risk: Team A's CMK protecting Team A's model weights can be decrypted by any over-privileged role/admin in the same account (Team B's CI role with kms:Decrypt on *, a sandbox admin, a compromised broad principal) without ever touching Team A's key policy or appearing as a key-policy change. Per-team data isolation is silently absent unless accounts or explicit-principal key policies are used.

identity_granularity.probe: KMS condition keys (kms:ViaService, kms:CallerAccount, kms:EncryptionContext) and grant constraints can re-establish per-team scoping, but they are opt-in, narrow in applicability, and carry their own ergonomic footguns
identity_granularity.finding: AWS provides the scoping primitives a multi-team org needs: kms:ViaService limits a key to requests proxied through a named service (e.g. sagemaker.us-east-1.amazonaws.com), kms:CallerAccount restricts to a specific account, and kms:EncryptionContext:context-key ties decrypt to the exact context bound at encrypt time. AWS's least-privilege guidance calls EncryptionContext 'the most effective way to implement least privileged permissions.' Grants add fine-grained, temporary delegation with constraints (EncryptionContextEquals/Subset) and a retiring principal. Caveats that blunt these in practice: (1) EncryptionContext conditions only apply to symmetric crypto operations that take an EncryptionContext parameter (Decrypt/GenerateDataKey/CreateGrant) — used on DescribeKey or asymmetric/HMAC keys, the permission is denied or the condition is inert. (2) Using a ForAllValues/ForAnyValue set operator with the single-valued kms:EncryptionContext:context-key is a documented footgun that 'matches requests without the specified encryption context'; AWS now returns an OverlyPermissiveCondition error for ForAllValues, but ForAnyValue silent over-permission remains a hazard. (3) ViaService only constrains service-proxied calls — it does nothing to stop direct KMS API decrypts by an authorized principal. (4) Grants are easy to create cross-account and the default key/console policy hands key users kms:CreateGrant (gated only by kms:GrantIsForAWSResource=true); stale or over-broad grants are a known audit gap and AWS recommends retiring unused grants. (5) The encryption-context value to scope to is service-specific and lives in each service's security docs, so writing a correct EncryptionContext condition requires per-service research.
identity_granularity.evidence: Condition key semantics & ForAllValues warning: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-kms.html . Least-privilege patterns combining ViaService+CallerAccount+EncryptionContext: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/least-privilege.html . Grants and GrantIsForAWSResource: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html .
identity_granularity.gap: The controls exist and are well-documented, but they are entirely opt-in and require deliberate, per-service policy authoring; the defaults give none of them (the stock key-user statement grants broad Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey on Resource:* with no ViaService/EncryptionContext/CallerAccount scoping). ViaService cannot constrain direct-API decrypt, so it is not a substitute for principal scoping. The ForAnyValue/ForAllValues set-operator footgun and the symmetric-only / parameter-only applicability mean an engineer can write a condition that looks restrictive but silently permits unscoped decrypts. Grant sprawl (cross-account CreateGrant in the default key-user statement) is a discoverable but unmanaged-by-default isolation leak.
identity_granularity.risk: A multi-team org can achieve Team-A-only decrypt of model weights via EncryptionContext + named principals + (where applicable) ViaService — but only if every team writes these conditions correctly. A mis-scoped set operator, a forgotten cross-account grant, or reliance on ViaService alone reopens cross-team decrypt. The burden of getting per-team IP isolation right shifts entirely onto policy authors, with several silent footguns that pass review while leaving data effectively unscoped.

identity_granularity.probe: Cross-account confused-deputy protection: external ID requirement in third-party/vendor AssumeRole trust policies
identity_granularity.finding: AWS documents the external ID (sts:ExternalId condition key) as the canonical fix for the cross-account confused-deputy problem when a SaaS vendor or security tool assumes a role across accounts. It is entirely OPT-IN and DEFAULT-OFF: a cross-account role trust policy that names only the third party's account ID as Principal (with no Condition) is fully valid and is the default produced by the create-role flow. Without the ExternalId condition, ANY customer of that vendor who learns your role ARN (AWS explicitly states the ARN 'isn't a secret') can have the vendor assume your role. AWS is explicit that the external ID must be GENERATED BY THE VENDOR, not the customer, and must be unique per customer; the docs warn against guessable values: 'do not use something that can be guessed, like the name or phone number of the third party.' Well-Architected SEC03-BP09 elevates this to a UUID recommendation and lists 'Using the default IAM trust policy without any conditions' and 'Reusing external IDs' as named anti-patterns (Level of risk: Medium). AWS also documents a concrete verification test: when given a role ARN, 'test whether you can assume the role both with and without the correct external ID. If you can assume the role without the correct external ID, don't store the customer's role ARN.'
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/confused-deputy.html ('Presumably the other customer learned or guessed the AWS1:ExampleRole, which isn't a secret.'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_common-scenarios_third-party.html ('do not use something that can be guessed, like the name or phone number of the third party'; 'AWS does not treat the external ID as a secret... can be seen by anyone with permission to view the role'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/framework/sec_permissions_share_securely_third_party.html (anti-patterns: 'Using the default IAM trust policy without any conditions', 'Reusing external IDs'; Level of risk: Medium)
identity_granularity.gap: There is NO default-on or platform-side enforcement that a cross-account/third-party trust policy carry an ExternalId (or aws:PrincipalOrgID) condition. IAM neither warns nor blocks at role-creation time when a trust policy trusts an external account with no condition. IAM Access Analyzer flags the role as externally-accessible but does NOT specifically flag a missing/weak external ID, does NOT distinguish a UUID from a guessable string, and 'does not consider the state of any external accounts.' The documented correctness depends on (a) the vendor choosing to generate a unique UUID, (b) the customer remembering to add the condition, and (c) the customer manually running the with/without assume-role test. For a multi-team org wiring up many SaaS/security vendors across many accounts, a single missing or reused external ID on one role silently exposes proprietary IP (S3 source/model weights, etc.) to every other tenant of that vendor.
identity_granularity.risk: A vendor integration role created without the ExternalId condition (or one where the vendor reuses external IDs across customers) lets any other customer of that vendor coerce the deputy into assuming your role and reading/writing the IP in that account. Because the role ARN is not secret and nothing default-on enforces the condition, the failure mode is silent and per-role, scaling with the number of external integrations the org maintains.

identity_granularity.probe: Cross-service confused-deputy protection: aws:SourceArn / aws:SourceAccount on resource-based policies for AWS services acting on your behalf (S3/SNS/SQS/KMS/CloudTrail, etc.)
identity_granularity.finding: AWS supports aws:SourceArn, aws:SourceAccount (and the newer aws:SourceOrgID / aws:SourceOrgPaths) as the documented defense against the cross-SERVICE confused-deputy problem, where a service principal (e.g., cloudtrail.amazonaws.com, sns.amazonaws.com) is granted access to your S3 bucket / KMS key / SQS queue via a resource-based policy. These conditions are OPT-IN and DEFAULT-OFF: a resource policy that allows a service Principal with no Source* condition will accept requests the service makes on behalf of ANY account, so an unauthorized actor who knows your resource name (e.g., bucket name) can have the service write to it. AWS recommends Source* keys 'wherever an AWS service principal is granted permission to access one of your resources' and notes aws:SourceArn (full ARN) is the strongest. AWS also now documents using Resource Control Policies (RCPs) with aws:SourceOrgID to centrally enforce this across an Organization without editing every resource policy — a genuine strength for a multi-account org.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/confused-deputy.html ('an S3 bucket that trusts the CloudTrail service principal with no conditions could receive CloudTrail logs from... an unauthorized actor in their AWS account, if they know the name of the S3 bucket'; 'We recommend using aws:SourceArn, aws:SourceAccount, aws:SourceOrgID, or aws:SourceOrgPaths... wherever an AWS service principal is granted permission'); per-service pages e.g. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/cross-service-confused-deputy-prevention.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/datasync/latest/userguide/cross-service-confused-deputy-prevention.html
identity_granularity.gap: Two documented footguns. (1) DEFAULT-OFF with no platform enforcement: AWS-generated and console-generated resource policies for service integrations frequently omit Source* conditions, and AWS explicitly states it is the customer's job to add them; per-service support is INCONSISTENT (the docs say 'see the documentation of the services you use... whether aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount... are supported'), so an org cannot assume a uniform control. (2) aws:SourceArn-with-wildcards / aws:SourceAccount-only weakens the control to account granularity, and AWS's own KMS/grant note shows some services rely on separate, service-specific mechanisms (encryption context) rather than Source* — meaning the same condition keys are not a universal answer. For an org with many accounts and many service integrations writing logs/notifications/data into shared S3/KMS, each unguarded resource policy is an independent confused-deputy exposure that no single default closes; the RCP-based central enforcement is itself opt-in and must be authored.
identity_granularity.risk: A shared logging/data S3 bucket or KMS key whose resource policy trusts a service principal with no aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount condition can be coerced by an actor in an unrelated account (who knows the resource name) into having the service write to or use it on their behalf — data-injection into the org's logging/IP stores. The exposure is per-resource-policy, silent, and inconsistently mitigated because service support for the keys varies.

identity_granularity.probe: IAM Access Analyzer external-access analyzer as the primary 'is our IP reachable by an outside principal?' detection control: coverage, defaults, and resource-policy-only scope
identity_granularity.finding: The external access analyzer uses automated (Zelkova-style) logic-based reasoning to flag resources reachable by a principal outside the configured zone of trust (org or account). It covers 15 resource types directly relevant to IP exfiltration: S3 general-purpose AND directory buckets, IAM role trust policies, KMS keys (policies + grants), Lambda functions and layers, SQS, Secrets Manager, SNS, ECR repositories, EFS, EBS volume snapshots, RDS DB and DB-cluster snapshots, and DynamoDB tables and streams. The external analyzer carries NO additional charge, can be scoped to the whole Organization as the zone of trust (so cross-account-within-org sharing is treated as trusted), re-analyzes a changed policy within ~30 minutes, and surfaces a public-vs-cross-account dashboard. HOWEVER three structural limits matter: (1) it is OPT-IN and NOT auto-created — Security Hub control IAM.28 / CIS 1.20 exists precisely because accounts ship with no analyzer; (2) external-access analysis is per-Region — the docs state 'IAM Access Analyzer analyzes only policies applied to resources in the same AWS Region where it's enabled... you must create an external access analyzer in each Region', so a single org analyzer does NOT cover a GPU/model workload spun up in an unmonitored Region; (3) it analyzes ONLY resource-based policies (plus S3 ACLs / snapshot share-ACLs), so exposure via identity-based policies, RAM shares of RDS clusters/clones, or cross-account S3 access-point policies (explicitly 'not analyzed... outside the analyzer account') is invisible.
identity_granularity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-access-analyzer.html — 'For external access, IAM Access Analyzer analyzes only policies applied to resources in the same AWS Region where it's enabled. To monitor all resources in your AWS environment, you must create an external access analyzer to enable IAM Access Analyzer in each Region.' Resource list and exclusions: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer-resources.html (S3 cross-account access points 'not analyzed'; RDS RAM shares not monitored). Opt-in confirmation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/iam-controls.html (IAM.28 fails 'if the account doesn't have an external access analyzer enabled in your currently selected AWS Region').
identity_granularity.gap: For a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, the control that answers 'is any IP reachable externally?' is default-OFF and Region-scoped, so coverage depends on org-wide enforced enablement in every Region (Control Tower / SCP / IaC), not on a default. Its resource-policy-only scope means whole classes of exposure (identity-based cross-account grants, RAM-shared RDS clusters, cross-account S3 access points) are silently out of scope, and a KMS key whose policy doesn't grant the analyzer SLR read access produces an ACCESS_DENIED 'error finding' rather than a confident 'no external access' — a blind spot that reads as clean unless someone inspects error findings.
identity_granularity.risk: A model-weights bucket, a Secrets Manager secret, an ECR image, or an EBS/RDS snapshot shared to an outside account in a Region where no analyzer was created generates no finding at all; and an over-locked KMS key protecting model weights can sit in an unanalyzed ACCESS_DENIED state that is easy to mistake for 'not externally shared'.

identity_granularity.probe: Least-privilege governance at scale: unused-access analyzer (unused roles/keys/permissions) plus custom-policy-checks in CI (CheckNoNewAccess / CheckAccessNotGranted / CheckNoPublicAccess)
identity_granularity.finding: Two distinct least-privilege engines exist and are org-aware. (1) The unused-access analyzer continuously reviews last-accessed data across all IAM roles and users in the org (via a delegated administrator) and flags unused roles, unused IAM-user access keys, unused passwords, and — for active principals — unused services and actions, feeding a findings dashboard. It is paid: $0.20 per IAM role or user analyzed per analyzer per month (service-linked roles excluded), and because it is global a single analyzer covers all Regions in a partition (unlike the external analyzer), but a per-Region analyzer is billed per Region if duplicated. (2) Preventive checks shift left into CI/CD: CheckNoNewAccess (does this policy grant more than a reference baseline?), CheckAccessNotGranted (does it grant a forbidden action such as iam:DeleteRole or a data-exfil action?), and CheckNoPublicAccess (does a resource policy allow public access?), invokable in a PR pipeline to block a widening grant before deploy at $0.0020/API call. Free ValidatePolicy adds grammar + best-practice linting. AWS publishes reference-policy samples (aws-samples/iam-access-analyzer-custom-policy-check-samples) for these checks.
identity_granularity.evidence: Unused access scope + org delegated admin: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-access-analyzer.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer-delegated-administrator.html . Pricing ($0.20/role-or-user/month unused; $0.0020/custom-policy-check API call): https://aws.amazon.com/iam/access-analyzer/pricing/ . Custom policy checks (CheckNoNewAccess, CheckAccessNotGranted, CheckNoPublicAccess) in CI: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer-custom-policy-checks.html and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/introducing-iam-access-analyzer-custom-policy-checks/
identity_granularity.gap: Both engines are opt-in and the strongest ones cost money, so least-privilege at scale is a deliberate spend, not a default: the unused-access analyzer must be created (and is billed per role/user/month), and the preventive CheckNoNewAccess/CheckAccessNotGranted gate only protects pipelines a team chooses to wire it into — it is an API, not an enforced Organization guardrail, so a team that authors policy outside the gated pipeline (console, ClickOps, un-instrumented IaC) bypasses it entirely. Unused-access also covers only IAM users and roles, not resource-policy breadth, and SCPs/RCPs/permission boundaries are governed separately.
identity_granularity.risk: Across many teams, role and access-key sprawl accumulates unless someone funds and acts on the paid unused-access findings; and a developer can widen a role toward the org's source/model-weights stores through any authoring path that skips the CI custom-policy-check, since the check is voluntary tooling rather than an organization-enforced deny.

supply_chain.probe: ECR private registry authentication (IAM-based, no shared/cross-tenant credentials)
supply_chain.finding: ECR uses IAM exclusively for auth. A docker/notation login obtains a base64 authorization token via GetAuthorizationToken whose permission scope exactly equals the calling IAM principal's, valid 12 hours; there are no long-lived registry passwords and no credential shared across accounts/tenants. Cross-account pulls are governed by repository (resource) policies plus IAM, both of which must allow the action. Private repos are private by default. This is the well-architected expectation and it is met.
supply_chain.gap: The 12-hour token is bearer-style: anyone who exfiltrates a node's cached ECR token (or the credential helper output) can pull any repo the node's role can reach for the token lifetime. There is no per-image scoping inside a registry token; scoping is only as fine as the IAM/role boundary, so a broadly-scoped node role flattens repo isolation across a multi-team org.
supply_chain.risk: A compromised CI runner or EKS node with a broad ECR pull role can pull another team's proprietary model/image. Repo isolation depends entirely on disciplined per-repo IAM/resource policies, which are not the default.

supply_chain.probe: ECR image signing AND verification enforcement (sign vs. actually block unsigned images)
supply_chain.finding: ECR supports signing via AWS Signer with two modes: managed signing (auto-sign on push, GA Nov 2025) and manual Notation-CLI signing; signatures are stored as OCI artifacts beside the image. Signing is opt-in (off by default). Verification/enforcement is uneven and largely customer-assembled: neither EKS nor ECS ships a native AWS-managed gate — EKS verification requires customer-installed third-party admission controllers (Gatekeeper+Ratify or Kyverno, each with the AWS Signer plugin) plus a Notation trust store/policy, and ECS requires a custom Lambda wired to task lifecycle/EventBridge; there is also NO native gate for raw EC2 or Lambda container images. Out of the box, an unsigned or tampered image runs fine everywhere.
supply_chain.gap: 'ECR supports image signing' implies supply-chain integrity is on; in reality nothing is signed or verified unless you opt in to signing AND separately stand up enforcement per compute platform. The ECS path is do-it-yourself (Lambda + lifecycle hooks). No registry-level 'reject unsigned pulls' control exists. Compare to a default-deny admission posture: AWS ships none.
supply_chain.risk: In a multi-team org, a poisoned base image or a maliciously re-pushed tag deploys to ECS/EC2 with no signature gate, exfiltrating IP or model weights at runtime. Teams that 'enabled signing' may falsely believe deploys are verified.

supply_chain.probe: ECR vulnerability scanning depth and default (basic vs. enhanced/Inspector)
supply_chain.finding: Basic scanning is on by default per private registry but only covers OS packages via Clair-style CVE matching and (by default) scans on push of changed images, not continuously. Enhanced scanning via Amazon Inspector adds OS + programming-language-package (application dependency) coverage and continuous re-scan as new CVEs land, but it is explicitly opt-in and billed through Inspector. Activation converts all repos in the registry and can only be deactivated by the Inspector delegated admin.
supply_chain.gap: The high-value coverage (language deps like vulnerable PyPI/npm in ML images, continuous re-scan) is the part that is NOT default-on. A team relying on default basic scanning gets shallow, point-in-time OS-only results and may miss a CVE disclosed after push.
