Enabling enhanced scanning is an org-level decision tied to Inspector cost, not a per-team toggle.
supply_chain.risk: A SageMaker/EKS GPU image with a vulnerable Python dependency passes basic scanning and is never re-flagged when the CVE drops, leaving an exploitable path to proprietary model weights.

supply_chain.probe: Base AMIs / GPU Deep Learning AMI patch ownership (guest OS, NVIDIA driver/CUDA)
supply_chain.finding: AWS publishes maintained AMIs (Amazon Linux, DLAMI) and BYO AMI is fully supported. Under the shared responsibility model the customer patches the guest OS. For GPU, the DLAMI ships pinned NVIDIA driver + CUDA; AWS scans and periodically republishes DLAMIs, but a running instance is frozen at its launch-time versions and AWS warns against kernel updates that would break driver compatibility. Kernel Live Patching is available on Amazon Linux to apply kernel CVE fixes without reboot.
supply_chain.gap: DLAMI driver/CUDA patching ownership is ambiguous-by-design: AWS ships and pins the driver, but once launched the customer owns updating it, and AWS guidance actively discourages kernel/driver bumps to preserve compatibility. There is no auto-update of the NVIDIA driver/CUDA stack on a live GPU node; the org must re-bake or re-launch from a newer DLAMI. No default mechanism forces this.
supply_chain.risk: GPU fleets running training on proprietary data drift onto an old NVIDIA driver/CUDA with a known CVE because 'don't touch the kernel' guidance and re-bake friction discourage patching; the vulnerable host sits under sensitive model training.

supply_chain.probe: Container/compute isolation model (Firecracker microVM, Nitro, EKS shared kernel, GPU)
supply_chain.finding: Lambda and Fargate run each task/invocation in a dedicated Firecracker microVM with its own kernel on Nitro bare-metal, giving VM-grade tenant isolation by default (no shared guest kernel across tenants). All modern EC2 sits on the Nitro System (hardware-offloaded, no hypervisor admin shell). EKS standard worker nodes, by contrast, run pods as Linux containers sharing the node kernel; pods on the same node are only namespace/cgroup-isolated unless you add stronger isolation. Firecracker does not do GPU passthrough, so GPU workloads (Fargate has no GPU; EKS/EC2 GPU) fall back to container-level (shared-kernel) isolation.
supply_chain.gap: The strong microVM isolation does NOT extend to GPU or to standard EKS nodes. Multi-tenant GPU sharing (MIG/time-slicing) and bin-packed EKS pods rely on shared-kernel container isolation, a weaker boundary than the Firecracker story implies. AWS does not default EKS to per-pod microVM isolation; that is a customer architecture choice.
supply_chain.risk: Two teams' workloads co-scheduled on one EKS GPU node share a kernel; a container-escape or GPU-driver bug crosses the team boundary, exposing one team's model weights to another. The 'Firecracker isolates everything' mental model overstates the GPU/EKS reality.

supply_chain.probe: Hardware/firmware attestation (Nitro System, NitroTPM, Nitro Enclaves, Secure Boot)
supply_chain.finding: Nitro Enclaves provide isolated execution where even root on the parent EC2 instance cannot read enclave memory, and produce a hypervisor-signed attestation document (PCR measurements) that KMS can natively gate key release on. NitroTPM (TPM 2.0) plus UEFI Secure Boot give measured boot and remote attestation: PCR0/1 measure AWS-controlled early boot, PCR4/7/12 validate the AMI/Secure Boot state. These are real, default-available confidential-computing primitives.
supply_chain.gap: Attestation is opt-in and asymmetric: the customer can attest their own boot/enclave state, but the lower layers (Nitro firmware, the security chip, hypervisor) are attested BY AWS and are opaque to the customer — you trust AWS's signature, you cannot independently inspect Nitro firmware. Nitro is not a customer-verifiable open root of trust; there is no third-party-auditable measurement of the Nitro controller itself exposed to tenants.
supply_chain.risk: For an org whose threat model includes the cloud operator, Nitro attestation reduces but does not eliminate trust-in-AWS: the firmware/hardware root remains AWS-attested and unverifiable by the customer, so 'attested' is bounded by trusting AWS's own chain.

supply_chain.probe: KMS key custody and who can decrypt (AWS-managed vs CMK vs CloudHSM vs XKS)
supply_chain.finding: Standard KMS keys live in AWS FIPS 140-3-validated HSMs; AWS states no one including AWS employees can export plaintext key material, which never leaves HSM volatile memory. Customer-managed keys (CMKs) let you define exactly which IAM principals can encrypt/decrypt and add a key policy as a second authorization layer beyond resource policy. For stronger custody, CloudHSM-backed custom key stores (single-tenant HSM you control) and External Key Store (XKS, keys physically outside AWS, AWS never touches them) are available.
supply_chain.gap: With standard/AWS-managed keys, AWS still operates the HSMs and the KMS service that performs the crypto, so the operator is in the trust boundary for the decrypt operation even though plaintext key bytes are not exportable. Truly removing AWS from key custody requires XKS (with its own availability/operational risk and no KMS SLA) or CloudHSM. AWS-managed keys also give the customer no key-policy control and limited rotation/audit visibility versus a CMK — the weaker default.
supply_chain.risk: Data encrypted under AWS-managed keys is decryptable by the controlling service path under AWS-operated infrastructure; an org that wants provable 'AWS cannot decrypt my IP' must adopt XKS/CloudHSM and accept the operational burden. Relying on default AWS-managed keys leaves custody and audit weaker than CMKs.

supply_chain.probe: S3 secure-by-default posture (encryption, Block Public Access, ACLs disabled)
supply_chain.finding: Strong defaults landed in 2023: SSE-S3 (AES-256) is applied to all new object uploads automatically since Jan 5 2023; S3 Block Public Access is auto-enabled and ACLs are disabled (Object Ownership = BucketOwnerEnforced) for all NEW buckets since April 2023 regardless of creation path (console/CLI/SDK/CloudFormation). S3 access control is via IAM/bucket policies, fully separate from compute credentials, and VPC gateway endpoints allow private-only access. This is a genuine well-architected default-on strength.
supply_chain.gap: These defaults apply only to NEW buckets — existing buckets created before the change retain prior (potentially public, ACL-enabled) settings, and the SSE-S3 default uses AWS-managed keys (no customer key policy / no per-bucket KMS custody unless you opt into SSE-KMS). Block Public Access can still be explicitly turned off per bucket. Default encryption protects at-rest only; it is not a substitute for a deny-by-default bucket policy or VPC endpoint restriction, which remain customer-configured.
supply_chain.risk: Legacy buckets holding source code or documents predating April 2023 may still be ACL/public-exposed; and default SSE-S3 means AWS-managed key custody for sensitive IP unless teams deliberately switch to SSE-KMS CMKs.

supply_chain.probe: Brokered secrets management (Secrets Manager rotation/injection vs Parameter Store)
supply_chain.finding: Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation (native Lambda for RDS/Redshift/DocumentDB, custom for others), fine-grained IAM + resource policies, and CMK encryption. Injection into ECS/Fargate is via task definition secret references resolved by the task EXECUTION role at start (plaintext never in the image/task def); EKS uses the ASCP + Secrets Store CSI driver scoped to specific pods via IAM roles for service accounts. Read-back is the model (you fetch current value), and resource policies allow scoping a secret to specific principals/environments.
supply_chain.gap: SSM Parameter Store (SecureString) is the cheaper, widely-used alternative but has NO native rotation — teams must build custom rotation Lambdas, and there is no rotation-state tracking. Rotation in Secrets Manager is also opt-in per secret, not default. ECS env-var injection surfaces secrets as process environment (readable via /proc or a crash dump to anyone who can exec into the task). IAM scoping a secret to exactly one team/workload is possible but must be hand-written; the default broad-read role footgun applies.
supply_chain.risk: A team standardizing on Parameter Store for model-registry or API credentials silently has no rotation, so a leaked long-lived secret stays valid indefinitely; and env-var injection makes those secrets visible to any code/exec in the container.

supply_chain.probe: AWS Marketplace third-party AMI/container trust and vetting
supply_chain.finding: Marketplace runs a self-service AMI/container scan that sellers must pass before listing (must contain no known CVEs/malware per the scanner), AWS reviews submissions against Marketplace policies, and continuously re-scans listings, contacting sellers when new CVEs affect a product. First-party AWS-published AMIs are distinct from third-party seller products.
supply_chain.gap: Vetting is automated CVE/malware scanning plus policy review, NOT a deep security audit or provenance/signing guarantee of third-party code. The scan is point-in-time at submission; a clean scan does not attest the seller's build pipeline or that the running software matches reviewed source. Third-party Marketplace AMIs/containers are a real supply-chain trust boundary the org inherits, and there is no signed-provenance requirement comparable to signing your own ECR images.
supply_chain.risk: Pulling a third-party GPU/ML AMI or container from Marketplace into accounts holding proprietary IP imports a supply-chain dependency vetted only by automated scanning; a backdoored-but-CVE-clean image could pass and run alongside sensitive workloads.

supply_chain.probe: Bedrock preventive AI controls: mandatory Guardrails enforcement (bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier), model-scoped IAM, and PrivateLink-to-Bedrock endpoint policies
supply_chain.finding: AWS shipped the preventive primitives, but every one is opt-in and several carry documented bypasses. (1) Guardrails (content filters, PII redaction, denied topics, prompt-injection/contextual-grounding) are NEVER applied by default; until 18 March 2025 they were optional per API call and a developer could omit them entirely. The new bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier IAM condition key lets you Deny InvokeModel/InvokeModelWithResponseStream/Converse/ConverseStream when the request doesn't carry a specific guardrail ARN+version. But the docs themselves list bypasses: a caller 'can bypass applying a guardrail in their prompt by using guardrail input tags' (request-side filtering is skipped; only the response is guarded), the key breaks RetrieveAndGenerate/InvokeAgent (those make internal InvokeModel calls without the guardrail, causing AccessDenied), and Guardrails do not support resource-based policies so enforcement is single-account only. (2) Model-scoped IAM works: bedrock:InvokeModel can be pinned to a specific foundation-model ARN (e.g. arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-...) in both identity policies and VPC endpoint policies, which constrains a compromised role from invoking arbitrary/expensive models. (3) PrivateLink interface endpoints (com.amazonaws.<region>.bedrock-runtime) keep prompts/embeddings off the public internet, and the endpoint policy can pin bedrock:InvokeModel to specific model ARNs. However Bedrock is reachable over the public internet by default (PrivateLink is opt-in), and crucially 'The default endpoint policy allows full access to Amazon Bedrock through the interface endpoint' — so a PrivateLink endpoint added for network privacy does NOT restrict actions or models unless a custom endpoint policy is authored.
supply_chain.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails-permissions-id.html (bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier examples + 'Limitations': input-tag bypass, RetrieveAndGenerate/InvokeAgent breakage, no cross-account); announced 2025-03-18 at https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/amazon-bedrock-guardrails-announces-iam-policy-based-enforcement-to-deliver-safe-ai-interactions/ ; default endpoint policy 'allows full access' + model-ARN-scoped example at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/vpc-interface-endpoints.html
supply_chain.gap: Three opt-in gaps stack against a multi-team org protecting proprietary prompts/IP: (a) no account- or org-wide default that forces a guardrail on every invocation — enforcement must be hand-written as paired Allow+Deny IAM statements per account, and even then is bypassable on the request side via input tags and incompatible with Agents/Knowledge-Base flows; (b) guardrail enforcement cannot span accounts (no resource-based policy), so a central security team cannot mandate one org-wide guardrail across many member accounts — it must be recreated and re-referenced in each account; (c) the default VPC endpoint policy is full-access, so the common 'we added PrivateLink' assumption gives network privacy but zero action/model restriction. None of these are SCP-enforceable defaults; they are per-account authoring exercises.
supply_chain.risk: A compromised or careless role can invoke any enabled foundation model with no content/PII/prompt-injection guardrail simply by omitting the guardrail parameter or using input tags, exfiltrating proprietary prompts/embeddings or running up cost on arbitrary models — unless every account independently authors the Deny-on-mismatch policy AND a restrictive endpoint policy. Teams that 'enabled Guardrails' or 'added a Bedrock VPC endpoint' may believe they have enforcement they do not have.

supply_chain.probe: SageMaker network isolation defaults (VPC-only training/endpoints, no direct internet) and Bedrock fine-tuning data isolation (your data never trains the base model)
supply_chain.finding: Two distinct realities. (1) SageMaker network isolation is OPT-IN and the insecure state is the default: AWS states 'SageMaker AI training and deployed inference containers are internet-enabled by default,' explicitly warning this 'could provide an avenue for unauthorized access to your data... a malicious user or code... could access your data and transfer it to a remote host.' Isolation requires setting EnableNetworkIsolation=True on CreateTrainingJob/CreateModel/CreateHyperParameterTuningJob; once set it cannot be changed (per-job, immutable). VPC attachment (VpcConfig) is a separate opt-in, and classic notebook instances default to direct internet access that cannot be disabled after creation (must recreate). So 'VPC-only, no direct internet, no root' is achievable but is a deliberate multi-flag configuration, not a default — Security Hub even ships dedicated controls flagging notebooks/training jobs/models lacking isolation. (2) By contrast, Bedrock fine-tuning data isolation is a genuine, documented default-on strength: 'When you fine-tune an FM on Amazon Bedrock, it is based on a private copy of that model' — your training/validation data 'isn't used to train the base ... models or distributed to third parties,' is not stored after the job, and the resulting custom model is encrypted with your KMS key and accessible only to you. Customer inputs/outputs are not shared with model providers nor used to improve base FMs.
supply_chain.evidence: SageMaker 'internet-enabled by default' + EnableNetworkIsolation opt-in/immutable: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/mkt-algo-model-internet-free.html ; Security Hub SageMaker isolation controls: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/sagemaker-controls.html ; Bedrock private-copy fine-tune + data-not-used-to-train-base FAQ: https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/faqs/
supply_chain.gap: The SageMaker side inverts least-privilege-by-default: the data-exfiltration-resistant posture (EnableNetworkIsolation + VpcConfig + no direct internet + disabled root in Studio/notebooks) is several independent opt-in flags, each immutable post-creation, with no single 'isolate everything' switch and no org-level default — enforcement falls to SCPs/Security Hub detection after the fact rather than a secure default. For a multi-team org running training on proprietary model weights and source data, any team that forgets a flag ships a container that can phone home. The Bedrock fine-tune isolation is the opposite and needs no further hardening.
supply_chain.risk: A training or inference container launched with default settings retains outbound internet egress and AWS credentials in the runtime, so a poisoned dependency or malicious training script can exfiltrate proprietary datasets or model weights to a remote host — the exact threat AWS's own doc describes — unless every job explicitly opts into network isolation, which cannot be retrofitted onto a running job.

org_maturity.probe: Named product security organization and leadership (Amazon CSO, AWS CISO, Security Assurance)
org_maturity.finding: Amazon/AWS has a clearly named, public security leadership chain: Steve Schmidt is Amazon Chief Security Officer (covering AWS, amazon.com, Devices, Kuiper, etc.), Amy Herzog is the current AWS VP & CISO (since June 2025; ex-MITRE principal security engineer, prior CISO for Amazon Devices & Services / Ads), and Chris Betz — AWS CISO from Aug 2023, ex-Capital One CISO — moved to VP of Infrastructure Security at AWS in June 2025, and a dedicated AWS Security Assurance organization (led by a named VP/Director) owns the audit/compliance program. AWS publishes named 'Security Profile' interviews and an Executive Insights security hub. The org is mature, staffed at the C-level, and externally identifiable.
org_maturity.gap: None material for a multi-team org. The security org is one of the most mature and visibly-staffed of any cloud provider. The only nuance is that 'AWS Security Assurance Services' (a paid consulting/QSA arm) is distinct from the internal AWS Security org, and customers should not conflate the two when scoping who is accountable for their workloads.
org_maturity.risk: Low. A customer holding proprietary IP can identify and escalate to a named, accountable security organization. No realistic risk that there is 'no one home.'

org_maturity.probe: Vulnerability Disclosure Program (VDP) and safe-harbor language for AWS services
org_maturity.finding: AWS runs a public Vulnerability Disclosure Program on HackerOne (hackerone.com/aws_vdp) plus aws-security@amazon.com (PGP available). It carries explicit good-faith/safe-harbor language: AWS 'will not bring legal action against you or report you for Good Faith Security Research, including for bypassing technological measures,' and commits to a non-automated receipt acknowledgment within 24 hours and monthly check-ins. AWS is also a CVE Numbering Authority (Amazon CNA) and issues CVEs for its own services.
org_maturity.gap: The VDP requires researchers to report within 72 hours of discovery and to seek pre-engagement guidance — a tighter, more constraining posture than many programs. Safe harbor is conditioned on AWS's good-faith definition, which AWS interprets unilaterally.
org_maturity.risk: Low-to-moderate. The reporting channel and legal safe harbor are real and usable, so externally-found vulns in services hosting your IP have a clear path to AWS. The 72-hour reporting clock mainly constrains the researcher, not AWS's remediation.

org_maturity.probe: Paid public bug bounty covering AWS infrastructure services
org_maturity.finding: AWS's public, monetary bug bounty (the Amazon Vulnerability Research Program, hackerone.com/amazonvrp, paying up to ~$25k) is scoped to Amazon retail/consumer/devices/Alexa assets — NOT core AWS cloud infrastructure (EC2, S3, IAM, SageMaker, Bedrock). For AWS cloud services, the only public channel is the UNPAID VDP; monetary rewards for AWS-service vulns exist only via a private, invite-only AWS Bug Bounty Program that researchers reach by nomination after VDP participation. AWS has been publicly criticized by researchers (e.g., Nick Frichette) for lacking a full public paid bounty on AWS services.
org_maturity.gap: There is no open, financially-incentivized bounty pointed at the exact services (SageMaker, Bedrock, EKS, S3, IAM) that would host this org's model weights and source code. The economic incentive that surfaces deep multi-tenant isolation bugs is gated behind a private invite list, which structurally reduces external research pressure on AWS's most IP-relevant surfaces.
org_maturity.risk: Moderate. Cross-tenant isolation flaws in the managed AI/compute services holding proprietary IP get less paid external scrutiny than a public bounty would generate. You are relying more on AWS internal testing and the unpaid VDP than on a market of incentivized researchers.

org_maturity.probe: Customer-facing incident response: CIRT vs. paid Security Incident Response service
org_maturity.finding: Two distinct things exist and are easy to conflate. (1) The AWS Customer Incident Response Team (CIRT) is a 24/7 team engageable by ANY customer via a support case 'regardless of support plan level' at no separate charge; it triages active security events, does root-cause via AWS service logs, and gives recovery guidance. (2) AWS Security Incident Response (GA Dec 2024) is a PAID subscription product (originally ~$7k/month minimum, repriced Nov 2025 to metered: first 10k findings/month free, then ~$0.000676/finding; bundled free with Enterprise Support / Unified Operations) that adds automated triage, case management, and pre-engaged 24/7 access. CIRT's scope is explicitly limited to AWS infrastructure/control-plane analysis — OS forensics, memory analysis, and app code review are pushed to third-party DFIR partners.
org_maturity.gap: Engagement severity (and thus effective responsiveness) for the free CIRT path is tied to support tier — Basic-plan customers can only open a 'General question' severity case, while Enterprise gets 'Critical business risk.' Meaningful IR SLAs effectively require a paid Support plan or the paid Security Incident Response subscription. CIRT does NOT do deep forensics on your instances/data; that is your responsibility or a third party's.
org_maturity.risk: Moderate. During an active compromise of your IP, an org on a low support tier gets slower, lower-severity triage and no AWS-led forensics on the workload itself. Budgeting Enterprise Support or the IR subscription is effectively required for credible incident response at organizational scale.

org_maturity.probe: SOC 2 Type II scope shape and per-service coverage
org_maturity.finding: AWS produces SOC 1, SOC 2, and SOC 3 (Type II) reports annually with continuous coverage; the Fall 2025 cycle covers 185 services over a 12-month audit period (Oct 1 2024 - Sep 30 2025). Critically, SOC/compliance scope is PER-SERVICE and PER-FEATURE: a service is covered only if listed on 'Services in Scope by Compliance Program,' and individual sub-features can be explicitly excluded (e.g., SageMaker AI is in scope but Studio Lab is excluded; Bedrock is in scope but Bedrock Marketplace is excluded). The major IP-relevant services this org would use — EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, EKS, SageMaker AI, Bedrock — are all currently in SOC 1/2/3 scope. AWS explicitly states a service not being in scope 'does not mean that you cannot use the service.'
org_maturity.gap: 'AWS has SOC 2' is true but materially incomplete: ~185 services are in scope out of 200+ AWS services, and feature-level exclusions are buried in the in-scope table. A team adopting a newer or niche AWS service, or a specific excluded sub-feature, for sensitive IP may be outside the assured boundary without realizing it. Verifying scope is a recurring per-service due-diligence task, not a one-time check.
org_maturity.risk: Moderate. An organization could place proprietary model weights or source code on a service/feature that is outside the current SOC 2 attestation, weakening contractual/audit assurances despite 'AWS is SOC 2 compliant' being technically accurate.

org_maturity.probe: Breadth of compliance attestations and self-service access (AWS Artifact)
org_maturity.finding: AWS Artifact is a free, self-service console portal giving customers on-demand access to AWS's audit artifacts: SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001/27017/27018/9001, PCI DSS AOC, C5, FedRAMP packages, and many others. SOC 1/SOC 2 reports require accepting an AWS NDA via Artifact Agreements before download; SOC 3 is public. HIPAA eligibility is supported via a BAA. The breadth of certifications and the self-service (vs. sales-gated) delivery model is a genuine maturity strength.
org_maturity.gap: Access to Artifact is gated by IAM permissions, so in a multi-account Organization, teams may not have the permission to pull the reports they need; the org must deliberately grant artifact:Get* / artifact:DownloadAgreement actions and manage NDA acceptance centrally. The NDA requirement also means SOC 1/2 reports cannot be freely circulated to all internal stakeholders or downstream auditors without care.
org_maturity.risk: Low. Evidence for the org's own compliance program is readily obtainable; the main friction is IAM/NDA governance, which is a customer-side configuration task rather than an AWS gap.

org_maturity.probe: Security advisory / changelog feed and CVE handling
org_maturity.finding: AWS publishes Security Bulletins at a stable URL with a machine-readable RSS feed (aws.amazon.com/security/security-bulletins/rss/), plus dedicated Amazon Linux security advisory feeds (ALAS). AWS is a CVE Numbering Authority (Amazon CNA) and issues CVEs for valid vulnerabilities in its services (e.g., the 2025 runc container CVEs were published as AWS bulletins). AWS Prescriptive Guidance documents a recommended pattern for ingesting the bulletin feed into a vulnerability-management process.
org_maturity.gap: Security Bulletins skew toward issues with a customer action item (shared-responsibility, OS/container CVEs) and notable industry events. Service-side issues that AWS remediates internally with no customer action are frequently NOT disclosed as bulletins — consistent with AWS's general posture of not publishing internal-only fixes. So the feed is a reliable signal for 'what you must patch,' not a complete ledger of every vulnerability that touched the platform holding your IP.
org_maturity.risk: Low-to-moderate. You get timely, structured notice of actionable vulns, but cannot infer the full internal vulnerability/remediation history of the managed services from the public feed alone.

org_maturity.finding: AWS maintains a public Post-Event Summaries (PES) page and has a track record of publishing detailed root-cause writeups for major events. The October 2025 us-east-1 DynamoDB outage produced a thorough public post-mortem describing the exact mechanism (a latent race condition in the DynamoDB DNS Planner/Enactor automation that produced an empty DNS record for the regional endpoint), the timeline, blast radius across EC2/Lambda/ECS/EKS/STS, and the specific remediations (disabling the DNS automation worldwide pending a fix). This level of technical candor on availability incidents is a maturity strength.
org_maturity.evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/technology/pes/ and the published DynamoDB us-east-1 2025-10 summary (analyzed at https://www.infoq.com/news/2025/11/aws-dynamodb-outage-postmortem/)
org_maturity.gap: Public PES writeups are reliably produced for large-scale AVAILABILITY/outage events. AWS does not publish equivalent detailed post-mortems for SECURITY incidents affecting the platform; security-relevant disclosures, if any, come through bulletins or direct customer notification rather than narrative public summaries. So transparency is strong for outages but asymmetric for confidentiality/integrity events touching customer IP.
org_maturity.risk: Low-to-moderate. For availability events you will get a credible root-cause account; for a hypothetical platform-side security event affecting your data, expect direct/contractual notification rather than a public narrative, which complicates independent verification.

org_maturity.probe: Incorporation, jurisdiction, financial stability, and CLOUD Act exposure
org_maturity.finding: AWS is operated by Amazon.com, Inc., a publicly traded (NASDAQ: AMZN), Delaware-incorporated, Seattle-headquartered US company — financially among the most stable cloud providers in existence. As a US entity, AWS is subject to US law including the CLOUD Act, meaning US legal process can in principle compel production of data regardless of the Region in which it physically resides. AWS publishes this as a fact (not a hidden caveat) and frames its sovereignty offerings as the mitigation.
org_maturity.gap: For an org whose crown-jewel asset is proprietary IP, US jurisdiction over the operating entity is a structural fact to feed into your own risk framework: standard commercial Regions do not place your data beyond US extraterritorial reach. Region selection controls data RESIDENCY but not the JURISDICTION of the controlling entity unless you adopt a sovereign offering.
org_maturity.risk: Context-dependent. For most commercial IP this is acceptable; for IP with foreign-government, export-controlled, or jurisdiction-sensitive characteristics, the default commercial footprint may not satisfy legal-sovereignty requirements without additional controls.

org_maturity.probe: Data-residency and sovereignty controls (GovCloud, European Sovereign Cloud, sovereignty pledge)
org_maturity.finding: AWS offers tiered sovereignty options. AWS GovCloud (US) is a physically/logically isolated US Region with administrative access restricted to US Persons, supporting ITAR, FedRAMP High, DoD SRG IL2/4/5, CJIS, and CMMC — appropriate for export-controlled IP. The AWS European Sovereign Cloud reached general availability on Jan 15 2026 (announced in Potsdam; first Region in Brandenburg, Germany), independently operated by EU-resident personnel under EU legal entities (German-law managing director), with customer data and customer-created metadata kept in the EU and incident response operated within the EU — explicitly engineered to put services under EU jurisdiction and outside CLOUD Act reach. These sit under a public Digital Sovereignty Pledge (since 2022).
org_maturity.gap: The strongest sovereignty guarantees come at a cost: GovCloud and the European Sovereign Cloud are SEPARATE Regions/partitions with their own (initially narrower) service catalogs, separate accounts, and feature lag versus commercial Regions — so a multi-team org standardizing on, say, the newest Bedrock or SageMaker features in commercial us-east-1 cannot transparently inherit those sovereignty properties. Sovereignty is an opt-in architectural decision per workload, not a default, and may not yet support every service holding the org's IP.
org_maturity.risk: Moderate. Achieving true legal sovereignty for sensitive IP requires committing affected workloads to a dedicated partition with potential feature/service trade-offs; mixing sovereign and commercial Regions for one IP corpus reintroduces the jurisdictional exposure the sovereign Region was meant to remove.

org_maturity.finding: AWS Shield Standard provides always-on, automatic, inline DDoS mitigation to all customers at no cost. The Shield Response Team (SRT) — a specialized 24/7 DDoS team — is engageable for active DDoS events but requires BOTH the paid AWS Shield Advanced tier AND a Business or Enterprise Support plan (the AWS Shield FAQ states a Business/Enterprise plan is needed to engage the SRT; customers can also pre-authorize the SRT via an IAM role to act on their behalf during an event). This is a separate response path from the general-security CIRT.
org_maturity.gap: Human SRT engagement and the associated DDoS cost-protection guarantees require Shield Advanced (a paid add-on) AND a Business or Enterprise Support plan. Customers on Shield Standard get automatic mitigation but no dedicated human response team for an escalating attack. For an org exposing public AI/inference or edge endpoints, SRT-grade response is an opt-in paid control, not a default.
org_maturity.risk: Low-to-moderate. Baseline DDoS protection is default-on, but escalated human-led DDoS response for sustained, targeted attacks on customer-facing IP endpoints requires budgeting Shield Advanced.

org_maturity.probe: AWS Config recording enablement: is configuration recording on by default in every account/region, and is org-wide aggregation default-on?
org_maturity.finding: The customer managed AWS Config configuration recorder is OFF by default and is strictly per-account, per-region. To get any configuration history, drift detection, or compliance evaluation, an operator must explicitly call put-configuration-recorder, put-delivery-channel, and start-configuration-recorder (or click 'Turn on' in the console where the default state is literally 'Recording is off') in EACH account and EACH region — enabling us-east-1 does nothing for eu-west-1. Recording incurs per-configuration-item billing, so it is not silently turned on. Org-wide visibility is also opt-in: you must create an AWS Config aggregator (or, better, designate a delegated administrator and create an organization aggregator) in a chosen account/region; member accounts only contribute data if they themselves have recording on. AWS Control Tower changes this picture materially — it auto-enables Config (continuous recording, service-linked role, org-level aggregator) during landing-zone setup, but ONLY in enrolled accounts and Control-Tower-governed regions, and only for accounts actually onboarded. The notable strength: aggregation can now run from a delegated admin (not just the management account), and global IAM resource types are excluded from per-region recording by default to control cost/noise.
org_maturity.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/stop-start-recorder.html ("You are charged service usage fees when AWS Config starts recording... If the customer managed configuration recorder is turned off, it disables the ability of AWS Config to track changes"); per-region setup via put/start-configuration-recorder: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/gs-cli-subscribe.html ; org aggregator / delegated admin: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregate-data.html and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/mt/org-aggregator-delegated-admin/ ; Control Tower auto-enablement scope (enrolled accounts + governed regions only): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/controltower/latest/userguide/config.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/controltower/latest/userguide/config-costs.html
org_maturity.gap: There is no organization-wide, default-on continuous configuration recording. In a multi-team org NOT fully governed by Control Tower (sandbox/dev accounts, accounts onboarded by org SCPs but never enrolled, opt-in regions like ap-southeast-* that hold GPU/SageMaker capacity, or regions outside the Control Tower governed set), Config recording is silently OFF unless someone deployed it via StackSets/Quick Setup. Coverage is a per-account-per-region matrix that an operator must affirmatively complete and continuously verify; the recorder can also be stopped or have its scope narrowed, and drift in the recorder itself must be separately watched (AWS::Config::ConfigurationRecorder CI). 'We use AWS Organizations' does not imply 'Config records everywhere.'
org_maturity.risk: Resource-configuration blind spots in exactly the places proprietary IP tends to land off the paved road: a team's GPU/EKS account spun up in an opt-in region with Config never enabled produces NO configuration history and NO compliance signal — an unencrypted EBS volume holding model weights, a public S3 bucket with source code, or an open security group is never recorded and never flagged until a manual audit or an incident. Because billing scales with configuration items, cost pressure also pushes teams to narrow recording scope, creating quieter blind spots.

org_maturity.probe: Continuous compliance / drift detection backbone: do conformance packs and Control Tower detective guardrails actually evaluate resources, and what is their hidden dependency?
org_maturity.finding: AWS provides strong building blocks for continuous compliance: managed Config rules, conformance packs (single-deploy bundles of rules + optional auto-remediation), prebuilt sample templates mapped to CIS AWS Foundations, PCI-DSS, NIST 800-53 rev 5, NIST-CSF, FedRAMP, and others, and organization conformance packs deployable across all member accounts from a delegated admin. Control Tower 'detective guardrails' are implemented under the hood AS AWS Config rules (plus Lambda) and are even published as a standalone 'AWS Control Tower Detective Guardrails' conformance pack. The critical, easily-missed dependency: ALL of this is downstream of Config recording. The conformance-pack prerequisites page states plainly: 'Before you deploy your conformance pack, turn on AWS Config recording' and instructs the user to change the console state from 'Recording is off' to on. Config rules and detective guardrails evaluate the configuration items the recorder produces; with recording off (the default), there are no CIs to evaluate, so the rules report nothing — not 'non-compliant', but effectively silent/empty. None of these packs are deployed by default; each is an explicit opt-in deployment.
org_maturity.evidence: Prerequisite is recording on: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/cpack-prerequisites.html ("Before you deploy your conformance pack, turn on AWS Config recording... under 'Recording is off', choose 'Turn on'"); detective guardrails are Config rules: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aws-control-tower-detective-guardrails.html and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/mt/aws-control-tower-detective-guardrails-as-an-aws-config-conformance-pack/ ; sample packs (CIS/PCI/NIST): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/conformancepack-sample-templates.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/operational-best-practices-for-nist-800-53_rev_5.html ; org conformance packs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/conformance-pack-organization-apis.html
org_maturity.gap: The detective half of governance is only as good as Config coverage, and the dependency is a Discoverable footgun: 'guardrail' and 'conformance pack' are reassuring vocabulary that imply active enforcement, but in an account/region where Config recording was never started they silently evaluate nothing. Control Tower hides this by auto-enabling Config in enrolled accounts/governed regions, but for accounts merely attached to the Organization (not enrolled) or resources in non-governed/opt-in regions, deploying a CIS or NIST pack without first turning on recording yields an empty, falsely-reassuring compliance dashboard. Conformance packs are also region-scoped and must be re-deployed per governed region; remediation requires pre-provisioning automation roles in every member account before deployment.
org_maturity.risk: False assurance for an org holding proprietary IP: a security team can stand up a NIST 800-53 / CIS conformance pack and a green Control Tower dashboard while a team's unenrolled GPU/SageMaker account in an opt-in region — with recording off — shows no findings simply because nothing is being recorded. Public buckets, unencrypted volumes, and open security groups protecting model weights and source code go undetected, and the gap looks like 'compliant' rather than 'unmonitored' to anyone reading the dashboard.

transparency.probe: Explicit statement of what AWS does NOT secure (Shared Responsibility Model)
transparency.finding: AWS publishes a canonical Shared Responsibility Model that cleanly partitions 'security OF the cloud' (AWS: hardware, software, networking, facilities running AWS services) from 'security IN the cloud' (customer: data, IAM config, OS patching on EC2, customer-controlled encryption, network/security-group config). It explicitly states responsibility shifts by service tier: for IaaS like EC2 the customer owns guest-OS patching, application software, and the security-group firewall; for abstracted services like S3/DynamoDB AWS operates the OS/platform. This is genuinely strong, repeated transparency about what AWS will NOT do for you.
transparency.evidence: https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model/ and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/security-pillar/shared-responsibility.html ('AWS is responsible for protecting the infrastructure... Customer responsibility will be determined by the AWS Cloud services that a customer selects.')
transparency.gap: The model is high-level and per-service boundaries are scattered across individual 'Security' chapters rather than a single machine-readable matrix; a multi-team org must read each service's security page to know exactly where its line falls (e.g., who patches the SageMaker training container vs the model code).
transparency.risk: Teams shipping proprietary models on EC2-GPU/EKS may assume AWS patches the guest OS or container base image when it does not, leaving CVE-exposed hosts holding model weights.

transparency.probe: 'Scoped' IAM permissions: resource-level and condition-key support varies by action
transparency.finding: The Service Authorization Reference documents per-action capability: 'The Resource types column indicates whether the action supports resource-level permissions. If the column is empty, then the action does not support resource-level permissions and you must specify all resources ("*") in your policy.' Condition keys likewise 'might be supported with an action, or with an action and a specific resource.' So 'least privilege' is only writable to the granularity each individual action/service exposes — many actions (e.g. List* operations) force Resource: '*' and accept no narrowing condition key.
transparency.gap: AWS truthfully documents the variance per-action, but there is no aggregate, queryable view of 'which actions in my policy are forced to wildcard'; an author must check each of hundreds of actions individually across hundreds of per-service pages. The footgun is that a policy can LOOK scoped (resource ARNs on some statements) while privilege-bearing actions in the same policy silently resolve to '*'.
transparency.risk: An org trying to write tight cross-account roles for source code / model-weight S3 buckets and Bedrock can be misled into believing a role is scoped when key actions grant account-wide reach, widening blast radius if a CI role is compromised.

transparency.probe: API Gateway 'API keys' explicitly disclaimed as NOT an authorization mechanism
transparency.finding: AWS explicitly warns against treating API keys as a security control: 'Don't use API keys for authentication or authorization to control access to your APIs. If you have multiple APIs in a usage plan, a user with a valid API key for one API in that usage plan can access all APIs in that usage plan. Instead... use an IAM role, a Lambda authorizer, or an Amazon Cognito user pool.' It also notes keys are transmitted in loggable headers and that usage-plan throttling/quotas are best-effort, not hard limits, and should not be relied on for cost control.
transparency.gap: The disclaimer lives in a 'Best practices' subsection of the usage-plans page, not at the top of the API-keys setup flow; a developer following the 'Set up API keys' how-to may wire keys into the request path before encountering the warning. The familiar term 'API key' still implies authorization to many.
transparency.risk: Low for this org given the warning exists and is clear; the residual risk is an internal team exposing a proprietary inference API behind only an API key, believing it is access-controlled.

transparency.probe: Account stated as the strong/hard isolation boundary
transparency.finding: AWS explicitly frames the AWS account as the hard isolation boundary: by default no access is allowed between accounts and cross-account sharing must be explicitly granted. The Well-Architected Security Pillar (SEC01-BP01) and the multi-account whitepaper direct multi-team orgs to separate workloads into separate accounts under one Organization, applying SCPs at the OU level as preventative guardrails. AWS is candid that account separation — not IAM-within-one-account — is the recommended strong boundary.
transparency.gap: The transparency is honest but implies more default safety than exists: SCPs do not restrict the management/payer account, do not by default deny anything (default FullAWSAccess), and the account boundary is porous via resource-based policies, RAM shares, VPC peering, and cross-account role trust that an org must actively constrain.
transparency.risk: A multi-team org may over-rely on 'accounts are a hard boundary' and under-invest in SCP deny guardrails, leaving a team able to share a model-weights bucket cross-account via a bucket policy that the account boundary does not stop.

transparency.probe: Encoded authorization failure messages: informative without leaking
transparency.finding: AWS designs authorization-denial detail to be informative to the authorized while not leaking to the unauthorized. EC2-style operations return Client.UnauthorizedOperation (HTTP 403) with an ENCODED message; 'The message is encoded because the details of the authorization status can contain privileged information that the user who requested the operation should not see.' Only a principal holding sts:DecodeAuthorizationMessage can decode it, revealing whether the denial was explicit-deny vs absence-of-allow, the principal, action, resource, and evaluated condition-key values.
transparency.gap: Coverage is uneven and self-documented as such: 'Only certain AWS operations return an encoded authorization message.' Many services return a plain AccessDenied with no decodable detail, so the rich diagnostic path is not universal. Decoding also requires a separately-granted permission that is easy to omit from break-glass/debug roles.
transparency.risk: Low — this is a genuine strength. Minor operational friction: teams debugging denied access to GPU/SageMaker resources may lack sts:DecodeAuthorizationMessage and burn time on opaque 403s.

transparency.probe: AccessDenied message phrasing distinguishes explicit vs implicit deny and names the blocking policy type
transparency.finding: IAM AccessDenied messages are documented to be diagnostically explicit: an explicit-deny message includes 'with an explicit deny in a <type> policy' and may name the policy ARN; an implicit-deny message includes 'because no <type> policy allows the <action> action.' The <type> token surfaces WHICH policy layer blocked (identity, resource, SCP, permissions boundary, session policy), and AWS documents the full deny/allow evaluation chain including that everything is implicitly denied by default except the root user.
transparency.gap: Message richness is newer and not uniform across all services/SDK paths; some services still return generic AccessDenied without the policy-type token, and SCP-origin denials can be confusing because the SCP lives in a management account the workload team cannot read.
transparency.risk: Low. For a multi-team org the residual issue is cross-account: a team blocked by an org-level SCP sees a deny it cannot self-diagnose without central-team help.

transparency.probe: Honesty that the root user cannot be constrained by IAM policy
transparency.finding: AWS states plainly that the account root user 'has complete access to all AWS services and resources' and that 'You can't use IAM policies to deny the root user access to resources explicitly. You can only use an AWS Organizations service control policy (SCP) to limit the permissions of the root user.' This is candid disclosure of a real limit: identity/permissions-boundary controls do not bind root, and only org-level SCPs (plus newer centralized-root-access features) can.
transparency.gap: Honest, but it means a standalone account (or the Organizations MANAGEMENT account, which SCPs do not restrict) has an uncontainable super-identity. For a multi-team org the management/payer account root is a structural single point of total compromise that no policy can scope down.
transparency.risk: Compromise of management-account root = unconstrained access across the entire Organization holding all teams' IP; mitigations are operational (hardware MFA, lock-away) not policy-enforceable.

transparency.probe: Honesty about security-group limits (no deny rules, stateful, service-traffic gaps)
transparency.finding: AWS documents that security groups support ALLOW rules only and cannot express explicit denies (implicit-deny model), are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed regardless of outbound rules via connection tracking), and 'do not filter traffic destined to and from certain AWS services.' It steers users to stateless Network ACLs for explicit deny rules, while also disclosing NACL limits (e.g., NACLs cannot block DNS to the Route 53 Resolver / VPC+2 address).
transparency.gap: Truthful but a footgun: practitioners frequently assume a security group can 'block' an IP/CIDR (it cannot), and connection-tracking nuances (when flows become tracked vs untracked) are buried. The implicit-deny + allow-only model means you cannot carve a deny exception inside an otherwise-permissive group.
transparency.risk: A team trying to exfiltration-harden a subnet holding model weights cannot do it with security groups alone and may believe an allow-only group denies egress it actually permits.

transparency.finding: AWS publishes public per-service roadmaps on GitHub (notably aws/containers-roadmap for ECS/ECR/Fargate/EKS) and the roadmap repos explicitly route security-vulnerability reports away from public Issues to AWS Security. There is no single unified, public AWS security roadmap; security-feature direction is fragmented across individual service roadmaps, What's New posts, and re:Inforce announcements, and AWS states it 'can't provide specific target dates for features' because 'job zero is security.'
transparency.evidence: https://github.com/aws/containers-roadmap (README routes security issues to AWS Security; no dated commitments) and https://aws.amazon.com/security/security-bulletins/
transparency.gap: A multi-team org cannot plan around upcoming security capabilities (e.g., new condition keys, default-on changes, isolation features) from one source; it must monitor many channels. This is weaker than a consolidated, trackable security roadmap and is not itself documented as a deliberate choice.
transparency.risk: Architecture decisions for protecting IP (e.g., waiting on a service to gain a needed resource-level condition key) are made blind to whether/when the gap closes.

transparency.probe: 'Well-Architected' marketing vs documented scope of the review
transparency.finding: AWS is transparent that a Well-Architected review is 'a constructive conversation about architectural decisions, and is not an audit mechanism,' and the free Well-Architected Tool 'identifies high-risk issues' rather than certifying security. The framework is positioned as guidance/self-assessment, not a guarantee or attestation. This honesty counters the natural reading of the brand term 'Well-Architected' as a security seal.
transparency.gap: The disclaimer is real but easy to miss; leadership often treats 'we ran a Well-Architected review' as equivalent to a security assessment. The Tool reflects only self-reported answers and does not inspect live config, so a 'green' review can coexist with misconfigured production. The brand term implies more assurance than the self-assessment delivers.
transparency.risk: An org may substitute a Well-Architected self-review for an actual control audit of accounts holding proprietary IP, mistaking guidance-conformance for verified posture.

transparency.probe: Transparency about AI/ML data use (Bedrock / foundation models)
transparency.finding: For the AI workloads central to this org, AWS states clearly what it does NOT do with content: 'Prompts... and outputs... are not used to train the underlying Amazon FMs, unless a customer consents,' inputs/outputs 'are not shared with third-party model providers,' and model customization creates 'a private, customized copy of the base FM' with tuning data transferred through the customer VPC and not used to train base models. This is explicit, affirmative transparency about data-use boundaries.
transparency.gap: Statements live in FAQ/marketing-adjacent pages rather than the binding service terms; the contractual ground truth is the AWS Service Terms / AI services opt-out policy, which a reader must cross-reference. The 'unless a customer consents' clause and per-service opt-out mechanics (AI services opt-out policy at the Organization level) are not surfaced in the same place, so the default-vs-opt-in posture for OTHER AI services (not Bedrock FMs) requires separate verification.
transparency.risk: A team using a managed AI service other than Bedrock FMs may not realize whether content is used for service improvement by default, relevant when prompts contain proprietary source code or internal documents.

network_architecture.probe: Default VPC public-by-default footgun: auto-assign public IPv4 + IGW route in default subnets
network_architecture.finding: Every region in a new AWS account ships with a default VPC that has an internet gateway attached, a main route table with a 0.0.0.0/0 route to that IGW, and default subnets whose 'auto-assign public IPv4' attribute is set to true. Docs state plainly: 'Instances that you launch into a default subnet receive both a public IPv4 address and a private IPv4 address.' So an EC2 instance launched with no explicit subnet/SG/IP config lands in a public subnet with a routable public IP. Crucially, this is the DEFAULT-VPC behavior only: a non-default VPC or non-default subnet is private (no IGW route, no public IP) unless you explicitly add an IGW and enable public IP. The footgun is specifically the default VPC/default subnet, not VPCs in general.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/default-subnet.html — 'By default, a default subnet is a public subnet, because the main route table sends the subnet's traffic that is destined for the internet to the internet gateway... Instances that you launch into a default subnet receive both a public IPv4 address and a private IPv4 address.'
network_architecture.gap: For a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, the default VPC means any team that launches an instance via the console quick-start path can expose it directly to the internet without realizing it. Default VPCs exist in every region (including unused ones), widening the attack surface. There is no account-wide 'default subnets are private' setting; mitigation requires deleting default VPCs, SCPs blocking IGW/public-IP, or org-managed networking (Control Tower / shared VPCs). Since 2024, public IPv4 carries an hourly charge, which surfaces the footgun on the bill, but it does not prevent the exposure.
network_architecture.risk: A SageMaker/EC2 GPU box or a service holding model weights launched into a default subnet gets a public IP and is reachable from the internet, gated only by its security group — one over-permissive inbound rule away from exposing proprietary IP.

network_architecture.probe: Default-deny inbound but open-by-default egress on new security groups (exfiltration footgun)
network_architecture.finding: A newly created security group has NO inbound rules (default-deny ingress — a genuine strength) but ONE outbound rule allowing ALL outbound traffic to 0.0.0.0/0 (and ::/0 if IPv6 is enabled). Security groups are stateful. AWS documents the asymmetry explicitly and recommends removing the open egress rule. The result: by default, ingress is locked down but egress is wide open.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security-group-rules.html — 'When you first create a security group, it has no inbound rules... When you first create a security group, it has an outbound rule that allows all outbound traffic from the resource. You can remove the rule and add outbound rules that allow specific outbound traffic only.'
network_architecture.gap: Open-by-default egress is the canonical data-exfiltration footgun. For an org whose crown jewels are source code and model weights, the default posture lets any compromised workload (or malicious dependency / SSRF) initiate outbound connections to arbitrary internet hosts. Least-privilege egress is writable (you can scope to CIDRs, prefix lists, or referenced SGs), but it is opt-in per security group and easy to omit. There is no built-in egress allowlist-by-default.
network_architecture.risk: A compromised training job or build agent can POST model weights or source to an attacker-controlled endpoint over the default open egress rule, with nothing in the SG layer stopping it.

network_architecture.probe: Default security group self-references and is silently inherited by unconfigured resources
network_architecture.finding: Every VPC (default and custom) has an undeletable 'default' security group whose default inbound rule allows ALL traffic from any resource assigned to the same default SG (self-referencing), and whose default outbound rule allows all traffic. AWS warns: 'if you don't specify a security group when you launch an EC2 instance, we associate the instance with the default security group for its VPC,' and recommends creating purpose-specific SGs instead.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/default-security-group.html — inbound rule 'Allows inbound traffic from all resources that are assigned to this security group'; outbound '0.0.0.0/0 All All Allows all outbound IPv4 traffic'; 'We recommend that you create security groups for specific resources... instead of using the default security group.'
network_architecture.gap: The footgun is silent inheritance: resources launched without an explicit SG land in a group where every co-member can reach every other member on all ports/protocols. In a multi-team account this can flatten intended segmentation between unrelated workloads sharing the default SG. The default SG cannot be deleted (only emptied), so it remains a standing trap. CIS/AWS Foundational Security Best Practices flag a default SG with rules as a finding, but that detection is opt-in (Security Hub/Config).
network_architecture.risk: Two unrelated teams' instances that both fall back to the default SG gain unrestricted mutual network access, breaking workload isolation for proprietary data.

network_architecture.probe: Account as the strong isolation boundary; VPCs isolated by default; cross-account requires explicit grants
network_architecture.finding: The AWS account is the hard isolation boundary: by default there is no control-plane or data-plane access between accounts, IAM namespaces are per-account, and VPCs in different accounts are independent and isolated. Cross-account access requires explicit configuration (IAM role trust policies, resource policies, RAM shares, or VPC peering/Transit Gateway with explicit routes). AWS guidance recommends treating the account as the lowest level of workload isolation, which aligns well with a multi-account Organization.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/saas-tenant-isolation-strategies/full-stack-isolation.html and https://aws.amazon.com/solutions/guidance/workload-isolation-on-aws/ — 'AWS accounts are a strong isolation boundary; by default there is no control plane or data plane access from one AWS account to another... The sharing of resources or data between accounts requires explicit policy statements that allow cross-account access.'
network_architecture.gap: This is a genuine strength for a multi-team org. The residual gap is ergonomic/governance, not capability: nothing forces teams onto separate accounts, and within a single shared account VPCs/SGs are the only isolation, which is weaker than the account boundary. Realizing the strength requires deliberate account-per-team/per-workload design (Organizations + Control Tower), which is opt-in.
network_architecture.risk: If teams co-locate proprietary workloads in one account to save overhead, they forfeit the strongest isolation boundary AWS offers and rely on more error-prone VPC/SG/IAM segmentation.

network_architecture.probe: Keyless, portless, audited instance access: SSM Session Manager (no-bastion pattern)
network_architecture.finding: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides shell access to EC2 (and managed on-prem) instances with no inbound ports, no SSH keys, and no bastion host. Connections are outbound from the SSM Agent over the AWS API; access is gated by IAM; and every session is logged to CloudTrail and optionally streamed to S3/CloudWatch Logs (including keystroke logging). EC2 Instance Connect and EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint provide complementary keyless paths (ephemeral IAM-pushed SSH keys; EIC Endpoint reaches private instances with no public IP). SSH can be disabled entirely by removing port-22 ingress and relying solely on Session Manager.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/connect-with-ec2-instance-connect-endpoint.html — Session Manager offers 'no open inbound ports,' IAM-based access, and session logging to S3/CloudWatch.
network_architecture.gap: Strong, mature, and the recommended modern pattern — but entirely opt-in. It requires the SSM Agent (preinstalled on Amazon Linux/Ubuntu AMIs but must be running), an instance profile granting SSM permissions, and network reachability to SSM endpoints (ideally via PrivateLink interface endpoints, themselves opt-in). Nothing disables SSH or enforces Session Manager by default; teams must actively choose it and write IAM/SCPs to forbid port-22 SGs. Default-account posture still permits classic key-pair SSH over a public IP.
network_architecture.risk: Without enforcement, teams default to SSH key pairs and open port 22, reintroducing long-lived keys and an inbound attack surface on hosts holding proprietary IP.

network_architecture.probe: VPC Flow Logs available but OFF by default
network_architecture.finding: VPC Flow Logs capture IP traffic metadata for VPCs, subnets, and ENIs, but they are NOT enabled when a VPC is created — they are entirely opt-in, must specify a destination (CloudWatch Logs / S3 / Kinesis Firehose), incur ingestion/archival charges, and require IAM permissions to create. AWS provides Config rules (vpc-flow-logs-enabled) and SSM automation to detect/remediate, but detection and remediation are themselves opt-in.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/flow-logs.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/vpc-flow-logs-enabled.html — flow logs must be explicitly created with a destination; the Config rule is NON_COMPLIANT 'if flow logs are not enabled for at least one Amazon VPC.'
network_architecture.gap: With flow logs off by default, a new account/VPC has NO network-level forensic record. For an org that needs to detect exfiltration of proprietary IP or investigate lateral movement, the absence of default network telemetry is a meaningful visibility gap that only an org-wide guardrail (Config rule + auto-remediation, or org-level flow logs via Control Tower) closes. Flow logs also do not capture packet contents (metadata only) and miss some traffic (e.g., to Route 53 Resolver, DHCP, Windows license).
network_architecture.risk: An exfiltration event over the open default egress could occur with no network log to reconstruct what left the VPC or where it went.

network_architecture.probe: No egress filtering by default; AWS Network Firewall / DNS Firewall provide domain allowlisting (opt-in)
network_architecture.finding: By default a VPC performs no egress content/domain filtering — egress control is limited to SG/NACL CIDR rules plus a NAT gateway for private subnets. For true egress filtering, AWS Network Firewall offers stateful domain allow/deny lists (HTTP Host header, HTTPS SNI), Suricata-compatible rules, and (as of 2026) URL and domain-category filtering; Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall filters DNS queries by domain. With an ALLOWLIST domain rule group, Network Firewall sends traffic only to specified domains and drops the rest.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/network-firewall/latest/developerguide/stateful-rule-groups-domain-names.html and https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/simplifying-policy-management-with-url-and-domain-category-filtering-on-aws-network-firewall/ — domain allowlists 'send traffic only to specified domains and drop all other requests.'
network_architecture.gap: Capability is strong but the default posture has zero egress inspection. Network Firewall is a separate, billed service that must be deployed, inserted into route tables (typically a dedicated inspection VPC / centralized via Transit Gateway), and have rules authored and maintained per allowed domain — significant opt-in effort. SNI-based filtering can be evaded (domain fronting / ESNI / direct-IP) unless paired with TLS inspection, a documented bypass class. So 'egress filtering' exists but is neither default nor trivially airtight.
network_architecture.risk: Without deploying and tuning Network Firewall, the org relies solely on open-by-default SG egress, leaving model weights and source code free to flow to any internet destination.

network_architecture.probe: PrivateLink for platform services: interface VPC endpoints + gateway endpoints keep API traffic off the public internet
network_architecture.finding: AWS PrivateLink interface endpoints expose AWS service APIs (and SaaS/your-own services) as ENIs with private IPs inside your subnet, and gateway endpoints provide private routing to S3 and DynamoDB with no IGW/NAT and no additional charge. Both keep service-API traffic on the AWS backbone rather than the public internet. Endpoint policies and S3 bucket-policy aws:SourceVpce conditions let you require that data access arrive only via your endpoint. This is a mature, well-documented strength.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/concepts.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/gateway-endpoints.html — gateway endpoints 'provide reliable connectivity to Amazon S3 and DynamoDB without requiring an internet gateway or a NAT device'; interface endpoints use private IPs via AWS PrivateLink.
network_architecture.gap: The capability is excellent but opt-in and easy to under-deploy: by default SDK/CLI calls to S3, SageMaker, Bedrock, STS, ECR, etc. resolve to PUBLIC service endpoints and egress via IGW/NAT unless you create the corresponding interface/gateway endpoints AND constrain access with endpoint/resource policies. Interface endpoints are billed hourly + per-GB, so cost discourages blanket coverage. Gateway endpoints (S3/DynamoDB) don't extend to on-prem/peered/TGW paths, a documented limitation. Realizing 'API traffic off the internet' for all the org's services requires deliberate per-service endpoint build-out plus aws:SourceVpce enforcement.
network_architecture.risk: If endpoints aren't deployed and enforced, calls that move model weights to/from S3 or invoke Bedrock can traverse public endpoints, and S3 buckets remain reachable from outside the VPC unless bucket policies pin them to the endpoint.

network_architecture.probe: Inter-VPC connectivity: VPC peering (non-transitive) and Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, transitive) with explicit routing
network_architecture.finding: VPCs are isolated by default; connecting them is explicit. VPC peering creates a 1:1, NON-transitive connection (A-B and B-C does not give A-C), requiring routes and SG references on both sides — a fail-safe default that prevents accidental transit. Transit Gateway provides a hub-and-spoke model with TRANSITIVE routing across many VPCs, accounts, and regions, with TGW route tables for segmentation; cross-account attachments are shared via AWS RAM and must be explicitly accepted. Both require deliberate route-table entries to take effect.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/vpc-peering.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/tgw/tgw-peering.html — VPCs 'do not support transitive peering'; Transit Gateway connects VPCs/on-prem via a hub-and-spoke model with route-table isolation.
network_architecture.gap: The defaults here are safe (isolation, non-transitivity, explicit routes). The risk inverts with Transit Gateway: its transitive routing makes it easy to create unintended any-to-any reachability across teams if TGW route tables and attachment associations aren't carefully segmented — a configuration footgun rather than a default-on one. SG referencing across peering/TGW also creates 'stale rule' management overhead when a peer SG/VPC is deleted. Multi-account segmentation via TGW is powerful but demands disciplined route-table design.
network_architecture.risk: A flat shared Transit Gateway route table can silently give one team's compromised workload network reach into another team's VPC holding proprietary IP.

network_architecture.probe: Stateful security groups + stateless network ACLs as layered restrict-by-network controls
network_architecture.finding: AWS provides two complementary network controls. Security groups are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), allow-only (no deny rules), instance/ENI-scoped, and default to deny-inbound/allow-all-outbound. Network ACLs are stateless (must allow both directions explicitly), support allow AND deny rules, are subnet-scoped, and the DEFAULT NACL allows all inbound and outbound — while a CUSTOM NACL denies all until you add rules. This gives a defense-in-depth pairing: SGs for fine-grained instance rules, NACLs for coarse subnet-level deny (e.g., blocking a bad CIDR).
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security-group-rules.html — default NACL allows all traffic; custom NACL denies all until rules are added; security groups are stateful with no deny rules.
network_architecture.gap: Two ergonomic footguns: (1) the DEFAULT network ACL associated with every subnet allows all traffic in and out, so unless you author a custom NACL the subnet layer adds no restriction — the protective deny-by-default only applies to NACLs you create yourself. (2) SGs cannot express deny rules, so blocking a specific malicious IP while allowing a range must be done at the NACL (stateless, ephemeral-port-aware) layer, which teams frequently get wrong. Layered control exists and is well-documented, but the default subnet posture is permissive at the NACL layer.
network_architecture.risk: Relying on the default NACL means subnet-level segmentation is effectively off, leaving SGs (with open default egress) as the only enforced control around proprietary workloads.

network_architecture.probe: Private interconnect to corporate networks: Direct Connect, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN
network_architecture.finding: AWS offers multiple private/encrypted paths between corp networks and VPCs: AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated private fiber (optionally with MACsec encryption and Direct Connect gateway for multi-VPC/multi-region), Site-to-Site VPN provides IPsec tunnels over the internet (terminating on a Virtual Private Gateway or Transit Gateway), and AWS Client VPN provides managed OpenVPN-based remote user access with mutual-certificate, Active Directory, or SAML 2.0 federated authentication (IAM Identity Center works via the SAML path; there is no standalone 'IAM' auth mode) and group-based authorization rules. These keep corp-to-cloud traffic off the public internet (Direct Connect) or encrypted (VPN), and integrate with Transit Gateway for hub-and-spoke reach.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/s2svpn/VPC_VPN.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/clientvpn-admin/what-is.html — Direct Connect 'links your internal network to a Direct Connect location'; Site-to-Site VPN creates IPsec tunnels; Client VPN is a managed client-based VPN.
network_architecture.gap: Comprehensive capability set, none of it default (all are deliberately provisioned, and Direct Connect involves physical cross-connects and lead time). Footguns: Site-to-Site VPN over the internet still depends on correct IPsec config and BGP routing; Direct Connect alone is NOT encrypted at layer 2 unless MACsec or a VPN-over-DX is layered on (a commonly missed point); Client VPN authorization rules default to no access until explicitly added, but split-tunnel vs full-tunnel and DNS settings are easy to misconfigure for data-leak prevention. These are provisioning/config concerns, not gaps in the offering.
network_architecture.risk: Misconfigured Direct Connect without MACsec/VPN can carry corp-to-cloud traffic (including proprietary IP) unencrypted across the provider link; a poorly scoped Client VPN can over-grant network reach to remote users.

network_architecture.probe: IMDSv2 org-wide enforcement at launch (Declarative Policy / SCP / account default) and the IMDSv1-reachable-by-default baseline
network_architecture.finding: AWS ships multiple opt-in mechanisms to force IMDSv2 (HttpTokens=required) on NEW launches, and they are ergonomically writable for a multi-account Org: (1) an account-level default per Region via ec2:modify-instance-metadata-defaults (--http-tokens required --http-tokens-enforced enabled), (2) an AWS Organizations Declarative Policy (ec2_attributes.instance_metadata_defaults with http_tokens=required and http_tokens_enforced=enabled) that applies across many accounts/Regions at once and which member accounts cannot override, and (3) an SCP/IAM Deny on ec2:RunInstances using the ec2:MetadataHttpTokens condition key (StringNotEquals required) plus ec2:MetadataHttpPutResponseHopLimit. Newly-released EC2 instance types and AMIs registered with imds-support=v2.0 also default to IMDSv2-only with hop limit 2. CRITICAL footgun: NONE of this is default-on. With no account default and an AMI whose imds-support is null, a new instance launches HttpTokens=optional (IMDSv1 reachable) -- AWS's own precedence table shows 'Launch parameter Not set / Account No preference / AMI null -> Resulting: V1 or V2'. Furthermore, merely setting the account default to 'V2 only' WITHOUT http_tokens_enforced=enabled does not guarantee enforcement: a per-launch HttpTokens=optional (Precedence 1, highest) silently overrides the account default (Example 2). Only http_tokens_enforced=enabled makes an optional launch parameter fail (Example 3). So a team launching with their own launch template can still reopen IMDSv1 unless enforcement is explicitly on.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/configuring-instance-metadata-options.html -- precedence table row 'Not set / No preference / null -> V1 or V2', and Example 2/Example 3; declarative policy schema at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_ec2_syntax.html (Instance Metadata: http_tokens_enforced 'enabled': 'Attempts to launch an IMDSv1 instance or to enable IMDSv1 on existing instances will fail'); SCP condition key ec2:MetadataHttpTokens at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps_examples_ec2.html
network_architecture.gap: The secure-by-default posture is opt-in on three axes at once: (a) the zero-config baseline is IMDSv1-reachable, not IMDSv2-only; (b) setting an account 'default' to V2-only is not the same as enforcement -- without http_tokens_enforced a team's launch template overrides it; (c) Declarative Policy / SCP must be authored and attached at the Org root, which a multi-team org must do deliberately. Best practice (defense against SSRF-to-credential-theft, the Capital One pattern) would be enforcement-by-default; AWS leaves the consequential 'enforced' flag and the org policy as deliberate actions a security team must take and verify in every Region.
network_architecture.risk: If the Org has not attached a Declarative Policy or SCP with http_tokens_enforced/MetadataHttpTokens=required across all accounts and Regions, any team can launch a GPU/EC2/EKS-node instance from a stock or self-built AMI with IMDSv1 reachable. A single app-layer SSRF on that node then reads /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ over IMDSv1 (no token, no PUT) and steals the node's instance-role credentials, enabling lateral movement toward proprietary source, model weights, and S3 data. A misconfigured-but-believed-safe state ('we set the account default to V2') is especially dangerous because it looks enforced but isn't.

network_architecture.probe: IMDSv2 enforcement coverage of the EXISTING heterogeneous fleet (already-running instances with IMDSv1)
network_architecture.finding: Every IMDSv2 enforcement mechanism AWS offers is scoped to NEW launches (or to blocking re-enablement), not to remediating already-running instances. AWS states explicitly: 'Setting the account-level default does not reset existing instances ... any existing instances that are set to IMDSv1 are not affected.' For account-level enforcement: 'This setting does not change the IMDS version of existing instances, but blocks enabling IMDSv1 on existing instances that currently have IMDSv1 disabled.' The Declarative Policy http_tokens_enforced='enabled' likewise only blocks future launches and ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions toward IMDSv1; it does not flip live instances from optional to required. The SCP (ec2:RunInstances / ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions condition keys) is a launch/modify-time deny -- it cannot retroactively close IMDSv1 on instances that are already optional and long-running. Closing IMDSv1 on an existing instance requires an explicit, per-instance ec2:ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions call (HttpTokens=required), iterated across the whole fleet -- there is no single org-level 'enforce on existing instances now' switch.
network_architecture.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/configuring-IMDS-new-instances.html -- 'Setting the account-level default does not reset existing instances ... existing instances that are set to IMDSv1 are not affected' and 'This setting does not change the IMDS version of existing instances, but blocks enabling IMDSv1 on existing instances that currently have IMDSv1 disabled'; remediation path is per-instance modify-instance-metadata-options at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/configuring-IMDS-existing-instances.html
network_architecture.gap: There is no default-on or org-wide control that retroactively converts a heterogeneous, long-lived fleet (the realistic state of a multi-team org with persistent GPU/EC2 boxes and EKS node groups) to IMDSv2-only.
