organizational_substrate.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_rcps.html ('List of AWS services that support RCPs' — enumerated list excludes Bedrock, SageMaker, EC2 instances, Lambda, RDS, EKS; 'RCPs do not impact the effective permissions of any service-linked role'; 'RCPs do not affect resources in the management account'); https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_data-perimeters.html ('To enforce data perimeter controls on resources that are currently not supported by RCPs, you can use resource-based policies that are attached to resources directly'); https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2026/02/aws-expands-resource-control-policies-amazon/ (DynamoDB added Feb 2026, confirming list is still incrementally expanding)
organizational_substrate.gap: The resource-side perimeter is non-uniform across exactly the services that matter most to an AI/IP org. Bedrock (custom/fine-tuned model access, model invocation), SageMaker (training jobs, notebook/Studio access, model registry), and the core compute/database tier (EC2/EBS snapshots, RDS, Lambda, EKS) cannot be wrapped in an RCP that says 'only my org's principals, only from my network.' The documented mitigation — SCP + VPC endpoint policy — protects the identity-and-network legs but does NOT replicate the resource-side guarantee that a resource-based policy (e.g., a Bedrock or SageMaker resource granting an external account) cannot open a hole; SCPs constrain YOUR identities, not what an outside principal can do against your resource via a too-permissive resource policy. The service-linked-role and management-account carve-outs further mean SLR-mediated paths and anything in the management account sit entirely outside the RCP perimeter.
organizational_substrate.risk: An org can correctly enable RCPs org-wide and believe it has closed 'data can't be copied to an attacker's account,' yet a Bedrock custom-model share, a SageMaker model-registry or notebook resource policy, an EBS/RDS snapshot shared cross-account, or an EKS-fronted workload remains outside RCP enforcement. For proprietary model weights (SageMaker/Bedrock) and source code (S3/ECR — these two ARE covered), the perimeter is strongest exactly where the artifacts are version-controlled and weakest exactly where the trained models live and run. The footgun is that 'RCPs are enabled' reads as a closed perimeter, while the highest-value AI assets fall through the service-coverage gap and must be backstopped by SCP deny-on-sharing-actions plus detective controls (Config/EventBridge remediation) that AWS documents precisely because the preventive RCP leg is absent for them.

organizational_substrate.probe: EBS encryption-by-default: account/region setting, default state, and CMK binding for proprietary volumes/snapshots holding model weights and training data
organizational_substrate.finding: Amazon EBS encryption-by-default is OFF by default and is a per-Region, per-account setting (`ec2:EnableEbsEncryptionByDefault` / console EC2 > Settings > Data protection and security). AWS docs state plainly: 'Encryption by default is a Region-specific setting. If you enable it for a Region, you cannot disable it for individual volumes or snapshots in that Region.' Until explicitly enabled in each Region, newly created EBS volumes and instance root/data volumes are created UNENCRYPTED. When enabled you can bind it to a chosen symmetric customer-managed KMS key, otherwise it falls back to the AWS managed key `aws/ebs`. Two critical caveats: (1) 'Encryption by default has no effect on existing EBS volumes or snapshots' — flipping it on does not retroactively encrypt anything; (2) it does NOT block someone from explicitly creating an unencrypted volume in a Region where the setting is off, and there is no org-wide single switch — it must be set in every Region of every account independently. This is the genuine GPU/IP gap: SageMaker, EKS, and EC2 GPU instances persist model weights, checkpoints, and training data on EBS volumes and snapshots, and those snapshots are the share/exfil surface (an unencrypted snapshot can even be made PUBLIC: 'You can share only unencrypted snapshots publicly. The encrypted snapshot cannot be made public.').
organizational_substrate.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/encryption-by-default.html (quote: 'Encryption by default is a Region-specific setting... If you enable it for a Region, you cannot disable it for individual volumes or snapshots' and 'Encryption by default has no effect on existing EBS volumes or snapshots'); public-share constraint: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-modifying-snapshot-permissions.html
organizational_substrate.gap: There is no Organization-level, default-on enforcement of EBS encryption. The control is opt-in, per-Region, and per-account, so a new account or a newly enabled Region (e.g., a team spinning up GPU capacity in a region nobody hardened) silently produces unencrypted volumes and snapshots. AWS provides no native 'encrypt all EBS everywhere' org policy — closing the gap requires either an SCP denying ec2:CreateVolume/RunInstances/CopySnapshot when ec2:Encrypted=false (Discoverable, must be hand-written), a Config rule + remediation, or a per-Region/per-account bootstrap that calls enable-ebs-encryption-by-default in all ~30 regions. The retroactive blind spot (existing volumes/snapshots stay unencrypted) means turning it on does not remediate the back catalog of checkpoints already on disk.
organizational_substrate.risk: A multi-team org with proprietary model weights faces a direct IP-leak path: a team in an un-hardened region launches a GPU instance, the root/data volume is unencrypted, a snapshot is taken, and that unencrypted snapshot can be shared cross-account or made public by mistake — leaking weights and training data in the clear. Because the setting is per-region/per-account and off by default, coverage gaps are the rule, not the exception, until an org explicitly builds enforcement.

organizational_substrate.probe: Tag governance as the substrate for multi-team ABAC/isolation: do Tag Policies actually force team/cost-center/data-classification tags to EXIST at resource creation, or must SCPs do the real enforcing?
organizational_substrate.finding: AWS Organizations Tag Policies do NOT prevent untagged resource creation. The AWS docs are explicit: 'Basic compliance rules do not enforce tag compliance on resources that are created without tags. This capability does not enforce missing tag keys. You cannot use this capability to ensure that required or mandatory tag keys are configured at resource creation.' Tag Policy enforcement mode only validates the VALUE and CAPITALIZATION of a tag that is actually supplied (e.g., block CostCenter unless value is in {Legal,HR}); it is silent when a tag key is absent, and 'Account administrators can still create untagged resources.' To actually require tags to exist at creation, AWS directs you to SCPs: 'Use SCPs to prevent IAM users and roles in target accounts from creating certain resource types if the request doesn't include the specified tags' — i.e., hand-written deny policies using `"Null": {"aws:RequestTag/team": "true"}` plus `aws:TagKeys`, per service and per action. ABAC itself is fully supported (aws:PrincipalTag / aws:ResourceTag condition keys), but the IAM/ABAC layer presumes tags are present and consistent — which Tag Policies alone do not guarantee. Enforcement is also limited to 'supported resource types' only.
organizational_substrate.evidence: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-enforcement.html (quote: 'Basic compliance rules do not enforce tag compliance on resources that are created without tags... You cannot use this capability to ensure that required or mandatory tag keys are configured at resource creation. ... Use SCPs to prevent IAM users and roles in target accounts from creating certain resource types if the request doesn't include the specified tags'); best-practices confirmation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-best-practices.html (quote: 'Resources that have never had tags attached to them don't show as noncompliant in reports. Account administrators can still create untagged resources.'); SCP examples: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps_examples_tagging.html
organizational_substrate.gap: The substrate that makes per-team isolation, ABAC, and cost attribution enforceable — guaranteed-present, consistent team/cost-center/data-classification tags — is NOT delivered by the feature whose name implies it ('Tag Policies'). Real creation-time enforcement requires a separate, per-service, per-action SCP body (every Create* call across EC2, EBS, S3, SageMaker, Bedrock, EKS, RDS, Lambda must be individually denied-unless-tagged), and SCP tag conditions only bite on services/actions that support the aws:RequestTag/aws:TagKeys keys — coverage is uneven and must be audited service by service. Tag Policies cover only 'supported resource types', and untagged resources are invisible in compliance reports (they don't even show as noncompliant). This is a classic 'Documented but Misleading' familiar-term footgun: an org that adopts Tag Policies expecting mandatory tagging will discover teams can still launch untagged, cross-team-unattributable GPU resources.
organizational_substrate.risk: In a multi-team org, ABAC-based isolation (e.g., aws:ResourceTag/team must match aws:PrincipalTag/team to access model-weight buckets/volumes) silently fails open for any resource a team created without the team tag, because nothing forced the tag to exist. Untagged or mis-cased tags break per-team access boundaries, defeat data-classification-based controls, and make IP-bearing resources unattributable for cost and incident response. Closing this requires bespoke, comprehensively-maintained SCPs — and any service/action gap in those SCPs is a hole in tenant isolation.

corrections.claim: eventVersion is currently 1.11 and follows documented major/minor compatibility rules.
corrections.probe: Is the current CloudTrail eventVersion 1.11 as the draft states?

corrections.claim: sourceIPAddress and userIdentity are marked Optional:False (always present); the listed top-level fields exist with documented Since versions.
corrections.probe: Are sourceIPAddress and userIdentity marked Optional:False (always present)?

corrections.claim: userIdentity exhaustively documents type, principalId, arn, accountId, accessKeyId, sessionContext (sessionIssuer, mfaAuthenticated, sourceIdentity), invokedBy, onBehalfOf/credentialId for IAM Identity Center, inScopeOf for service-to-service, and a new invokedByDelegate field flagging actions by product providers via delegated access.
corrections.correction: All fields confirmed. invokedByDelegate exists and contains the product-provider accountId of a party using temporary delegated access. inScopeOf carries sourceArn/sourceAccount/issuerType/credentialsIssuedTo (e.g. AWS::Lambda::Function), confirming the 'originating resource' framing.
corrections.probe: Does the userIdentity element document invokedByDelegate, inScopeOf, onBehalfOf, credentialId, sessionContext/sessionIssuer/mfaAuthenticated/sourceIdentity, invokedBy, and tlsDetails as the draft claims?

corrections.claim: Event history is on automatically per Region, free, no setup, a 90-day rolling record of MANAGEMENT events only (no data events ever), viewable via console or LookupEvents.
corrections.probe: Is CloudTrail Event history 90 days, management events only, free?

corrections.claim: Management events are on by default for trails/event data stores; data events (S3/Lambda/DynamoDB/AI-ML) and network activity events (VPC-endpoint API calls) are NOT logged by default and incur additional charges.
corrections.probe: Are management events on by default while data events and network activity events are NOT logged by default and billed separately?

corrections.claim: Data events ... and notably AI/ML resource access such as Bedrock InvokeModel-class operations ... are NOT logged by default and incur additional per-event charges.
corrections.correction: Amazon Bedrock logs InvokeModel, InvokeModelWithResponseStream, Converse, and ConverseStream as MANAGEMENT events (the documented example shows "managementEvent": true and "eventCategory": "Management"), so they ARE captured by default at no extra data-event charge. Only certain runtime ops are data events: InvokeModelWithBidirectionalStream, GetAsyncInvoke, StartAsyncInvoke, plus all Agents-for-Bedrock runtime ops (InvokeAgent, InvokeInlineAgent), Retrieve/RetrieveAndGenerate, InvokeFlow, RenderPrompt. The draft's premise that 'by default AWS records who changed configuration but NOT who invoked a model' is therefore wrong for the common Bedrock invocation APIs - the fact of the invocation (with principal, source IP, modelId) IS in the default management-event stream; what is NOT captured by CloudTrail is the prompt/response content (that requires Bedrock model invocation logging). Note SageMaker InvokeEndpoint remains a data event, so the SageMaker side of the claim still holds.
corrections.probe: Are Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel-class operations data events that are NOT logged by default, as the 'Management vs DATA events' finding asserts?

corrections.claim: CloudTrail records the Bedrock API call (management/data event) but not the prompt/response content.
corrections.probe: Does CloudTrail capture Bedrock prompt/response content, or only the API call?

corrections.claim: Bedrock model invocation logging captures full request/response data and metadata (input/output JSON bodies up to 100 KB) to CloudWatch Logs and/or S3, but is DISABLED by default and must be explicitly enabled per Region.
corrections.probe: Is Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging disabled by default, with full prompt/response capture up to 100 KB to CloudWatch Logs and/or S3?

corrections.claim: CloudWatch Logs retention is configurable 1 day to 10 years (3653 days) or Never Expire; default for new log groups (including auto-created Lambda groups) is Never Expire (missing retentionInDays); must be set explicitly per log group.
corrections.probe: Is CloudWatch Logs per-log-group retention configurable 1 day to 3653 days (10 years) with default 'Never Expire', including auto-created Lambda log groups?

corrections.claim: CloudTrail Lake event data stores retain up to 3653 days (~10 years) under EXTENDABLE_RETENTION_PRICING or up to 2557 days (~7 years) under FIXED_RETENTION_PRICING.
corrections.probe: Do CloudTrail Lake event data stores support up to 3653 days under EXTENDABLE_RETENTION_PRICING and up to 2557 days under FIXED_RETENTION_PRICING?

corrections.claim: CloudTrail log file integrity validation creates hourly digest files using SHA-256 hashing and SHA-256-with-RSA signing, each digest chaining the previous one.
corrections.probe: Does CloudTrail log file integrity validation use SHA-256 hashing and SHA-256-with-RSA signing with hourly digest files that chain to the previous digest?

corrections.claim: An organization trail created from the management/delegated-admin account is visible to member accounts but cannot be modified, disabled, have its event selectors changed, or deleted by them.
corrections.probe: Can member accounts modify/disable/delete an organization trail or change its event selectors?

corrections.claim: Security Lake centralizes CloudTrail management events, S3/Lambda data events, VPC Flow Logs, and more, automatically normalizing them to the open OCSF schema in the customer's S3 (Parquet), with subscriber access for third-party SIEMs.
corrections.probe: Does Amazon Security Lake centralize and normalize CloudTrail management events plus S3/Lambda data events, VPC Flow Logs and more into OCSF with subscriber access?

corrections.claim: Default session is 3600s (1h); MaxSessionDuration configurable on the role up to 43200s (12h); role chaining is hard-capped at 1 hour regardless of MaxSessionDuration.
corrections.probe: Is STS role chaining hard-capped at 1 hour regardless of MaxSessionDuration, and is default AssumeRole session 3600s with MaxSessionDuration configurable up to 43200s (12h)?

corrections.claim: EKS Pod Identity uses cluster-agnostic service principal pods.eks.amazonaws.com requiring sts:AssumeRole + sts:TagSession and auto-injects session tags (eks-cluster-arn, kubernetes-namespace, kubernetes-service-account, kubernetes-pod-name) usable in ABAC conditions; also supports session policies.
corrections.correction: Accurate, but the auto-injected tag set is slightly larger than the draft lists. The current reference set is six tags: eks-cluster-arn, eks-cluster-name, kubernetes-namespace, kubernetes-service-account, kubernetes-pod-name, and kubernetes-pod-uid. The draft's four are a correct subset; consider noting eks-cluster-name and kubernetes-pod-uid for completeness. Session policy support for Pod Identity is also confirmed (later GA feature).
corrections.probe: Does EKS Pod Identity (re:Invent 2023) use service principal pods.eks.amazonaws.com, require sts:AssumeRole + sts:TagSession, and auto-inject session tags eks-cluster-arn, kubernetes-namespace, kubernetes-service-account, kubernetes-pod-name?

corrections.claim: Access Analyzer external-access findings are free; unused-access findings are opt-in and PRICED per role/user/month.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Exact figures: external access analysis is free; unused access analysis is $0.20 per IAM role or user per month (one analyzer per account/partition covers all regions, since roles/users are global); custom policy checks are $0.0020 per API call.
corrections.probe: Is IAM Access Analyzer external-access analysis free while unused-access analysis is opt-in and priced per role/user/month?

corrections.claim: The sub claim format changes when a job uses a GitHub environment (becomes repo:org/repo:environment:Name, dropping the branch); pull_request runs yield sub ...:pull_request; StringLike repo:org/* is dangerously permissive.
corrections.probe: Does the GitHub Actions OIDC sub claim change to repo:org/repo:environment:Name (dropping branch) when a job uses a GitHub environment, and ...:pull_request for PR runs?

corrections.claim: Root user access keys can still technically be created though AWS strongly advises against them and now blocks/discourages via console; long-lived IAM user keys have no expiry and no native auto-rotation.
corrections.correction: Confirmed that root access keys remain creatable and are discouraged. Worth adding the current organizational control the draft does not mention: AWS launched Centralized Root Access Management for AWS Organizations on 2024-11-15, which lets a management account remove root credentials from member accounts entirely and perform short-term, task-scoped privileged root sessions (AssumeRoot) instead. This materially strengthens the org-wide root governance story.
corrections.probe: Can root user access keys still be created (CLI/API/console) while AWS discourages them, and did AWS launch centralized root access management?

corrections.claim: CreateSession is signed with extended SigV4 (AWS4-X509-RSA-SHA256 / AWS4-X509-ECDSA-SHA256); private key never leaves host (aws_signing_helper via credential_process); returns standard short-lived STS creds; trust anchor is AWS Private CA or external CA.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Minor currency note: AWS has since added a post-quantum signing variant (AWS4-X509-MLDSA) alongside the RSA/ECDSA schemes, so the scheme list is now three, not two. Not an error in the draft, just an addition.
corrections.probe: Does IAM Roles Anywhere use the extended SigV4 schemes AWS4-X509-RSA-SHA256 / AWS4-X509-ECDSA-SHA256 via CreateSession, with the private key handled by aws_signing_helper as a credential_process and a trust anchor (Private CA or external CA)?

corrections.claim: awslabs/mcp servers honor the standard boto3 credential chain; read-only via READ_OPERATIONS_ONLY / --readonly flags AND via scoping the IAM principal; AWS docs state IAM permissions remain the primary security control, not the server flag.
corrections.probe: Do awslabs/mcp servers honor the boto3 credential chain and support read-only via READ_OPERATIONS_ONLY / --readonly flags, with IAM permissions as the primary control?

corrections.claim: Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation but it is NOT enabled by default; Parameter Store has NO built-in rotation at all.
corrections.probe: Is Secrets Manager automatic rotation off by default (requires configured rotation Lambda/managed rotation + schedule), and does Parameter Store have no built-in rotation?

corrections.claim: ec2:RunInstances requires permissions across up to eight distinct resource types: instance, image, subnet, network-interface, security-group, key-pair, volume, and spot-instances-request.
corrections.correction: The core point (RunInstances is one action spanning many resource types, the multi-resource-type footgun) is correct, but the enumeration is stale. The current Service Authorization Reference lists TWELVE resource types for ec2:RunInstances, not eight: image, instance, network-interface, security-group, subnet, and volume are marked required (*), plus key-pair, elastic-gpu, capacity-reservation, placement-group, launch-template, and license-configuration as optional. 'spot-instances-request' is NOT among the resource types for RunInstances in the current reference (it is associated with other Spot actions). Recommend changing 'up to eight (... spot-instances-request)' to 'up to twelve' and dropping spot-instances-request from the RunInstances list (or replacing it with elastic-gpu / capacity-reservation / placement-group / launch-template / license-configuration).
corrections.probe: Does ec2:RunInstances in the current Service Authorization Reference require permissions across exactly the eight resource types the draft enumerates (instance, image, subnet, network-interface, security-group, key-pair, volume, spot-instances-request)?

corrections.claim: All data-plane API calls are authenticated with SigV4-signed request headers (not URL query params for normal use), so credentials are not exposed in URLs; STS-derived creds default to 1 hour, configurable up to 12 hours.
corrections.correction: Correct as qualified. For normal signed requests the session token rides in the X-Amz-Security-Token header (not the URL). The draft's 'for normal use' caveat is important and accurate: AWS does also support presigned/query-string SigV4 (used by S3 presigned URLs and STS GetCallerIdentity presign for IAM auth) where the token and signature appear as query parameters by design - so the claim holds for standard SDK/CLI calls but is not absolute across all SigV4 usage.
corrections.probe: Are data-plane API calls authenticated with SigV4-signed request headers (session token in X-Amz-Security-Token header) rather than URL query params for normal use?

corrections.claim: IRSA (OIDC-provider-based, per-cluster trust-policy edits) is now complemented by EKS Pod Identity (re:Invent 2023); IRSA is subject to per-account OIDC-provider and trust-policy-size limits.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. AWS explicitly states the two coexist and that it will continue investing in and supporting IRSA; Pod Identity is the recommended path for in-cloud EKS while IRSA remains required for EKS Anywhere / ROSA / self-managed clusters. The 'complemented' framing is accurate - Pod Identity does not deprecate IRSA.
corrections.probe: Are both IRSA and EKS Pod Identity current/supported, with Pod Identity complementing rather than replacing IRSA?

corrections.claim: ECR uses IAM exclusively; a docker/notation login obtains a base64 authorization token via GetAuthorizationToken whose permission scope exactly equals the calling IAM principal's, valid 12 hours; no long-lived registry passwords, no cross-tenant shared credential.
corrections.probe: Does ECR's GetAuthorizationToken really produce a token valid 12 hours whose permission scope equals the calling IAM principal, with no long-lived/shared registry passwords?

corrections.claim: ECR supports managed signing (auto-sign on push, GA Nov 2025) plus manual Notation-CLI signing; signing is opt-in (off by default); signatures stored as OCI artifacts beside the image.
corrections.probe: Did ECR managed signing (auto-sign on push via AWS Signer) actually reach GA in November 2025, and is signing opt-in/off by default?

corrections.claim: Verification/enforcement is uneven: EKS has a native managed verification path, ECS requires you to build a Lambda admission controller; no native gate for raw EC2 or Lambda container images.
corrections.correction: EKS has NO native/AWS-managed signature-verification gate. AWS's own EKS guide says verifying AWS Signer signatures at deployment requires customer-installed third-party admission controllers (Gatekeeper+Ratify with the AWS Signer plugin, or Kyverno with the AWS Signer plugin), plus a Notation trust store/policy. So enforcement is customer-assembled on BOTH EKS and ECS (EKS via an open-source admission controller webhook, ECS via a Lambda wired to task lifecycle/EventBridge). The draft's framing that EKS is 'native managed' while ECS is hand-built is inaccurate; neither is a turnkey native gate. The rest of the finding (off-by-default signing, no native gate for raw EC2/Lambda container images, unsigned images run by default) is correct.
corrections.probe: Does Amazon EKS provide a NATIVE/managed signature-verification gate that blocks unsigned images, distinct from ECS which needs a custom Lambda?

corrections.claim: ECS requires you to build a Lambda admission controller wired to service lifecycle hooks (to verify AWS Signer signatures).
corrections.correction: Confirmed in substance: ECS verification is a customer-built pattern using a Lambda that runs Notation CLI + AWS Signer, triggered via EventBridge task-state-change events and/or ECS service lifecycle hooks. There is no native ECS gate.
corrections.probe: Is the ECS verification path really a customer-built Lambda admission controller tied to service lifecycle hooks?

corrections.claim: Basic scanning is on by default per private registry, covers only OS packages via Clair-style CVE matching, scans on push of changed images not continuously; enhanced (Inspector) adds OS + language-package coverage and continuous re-scan, is opt-in, billed through Inspector, converts all repos, deactivated only by the Inspector delegated admin.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Minor freshness note (not a defect): AWS now also offers an 'improved basic scanning' option powered by Amazon Inspector as an alternative to the legacy Clair-based basic scanner; the legacy Clair-based default the draft describes is still correct.
corrections.probe: Is ECR basic scanning on by default (OS packages, Clair CVE DB, scan-on-push not continuous), with enhanced/Inspector scanning opt-in, registry-wide, and Inspector-billed?

corrections.claim: Lambda and Fargate run each task/invocation in a dedicated Firecracker microVM with its own kernel on Nitro bare-metal (no shared guest kernel across tenants); EKS standard worker nodes run pods as containers sharing the node kernel.
corrections.probe: Are Lambda and Fargate each run in a dedicated Firecracker microVM with its own kernel, giving VM-grade tenant isolation by default, while EKS standard nodes share the node kernel across pods?

corrections.claim: NitroTPM (TPM 2.0) + UEFI Secure Boot give measured boot; PCR0/1 measure AWS-controlled early boot, PCR4/7/12 validate the AMI/Secure Boot state; KMS can gate key release on these.
corrections.correction: Confirmed and current: PCR0/1 measure AWS-controlled early boot code; PCR4 (boot binary) + PCR12 (command line) validate standard boot of an Attestable AMI, and PCR7 validates UEFI Secure Boot state. KMS exposes kms:RecipientAttestation:NitroTPMPCR4/PCR7/PCR12 condition keys to gate key use.
corrections.probe: Are NitroTPM PCR0/1 AWS-controlled early boot and PCR4/7/12 used to validate the AMI / Secure Boot state, with KMS attestation condition keys?

corrections.claim: Nitro Enclaves give isolated execution where even root on the parent EC2 instance cannot read enclave memory, and produce a hypervisor-signed attestation document (PCR measurements) that KMS can natively gate key release on.
corrections.probe: Do Nitro Enclaves produce a signed attestation document with PCRs that KMS can natively gate Decrypt/GenerateDataKey on, and can parent-instance root not read enclave memory?

corrections.claim: Standard KMS keys live in AWS FIPS 140-3-validated HSMs; no one including AWS employees can export plaintext key material; CMKs add a key-policy authorization layer; CloudHSM-backed custom key stores and External Key Store (XKS) are available.
corrections.correction: Confirmed and current: AWS KMS HSMs received FIPS 140-3 Security Level 3 validation from NIST in Feb 2025 (an upgrade from the prior FIPS 140-2 Level 3), so 'FIPS 140-3-validated' is accurate.
corrections.probe: Are standard KMS keys in FIPS 140-3 validated HSMs (key material never leaving HSM, no AWS employee export), with CMK key policies and CloudHSM custom key stores / XKS available?

corrections.claim: SSE-S3 (AES-256) is applied to all new object uploads automatically since Jan 5 2023; S3 Block Public Access auto-enabled and ACLs disabled (Object Ownership = BucketOwnerEnforced) for all NEW buckets since April 2023 regardless of creation path (console/CLI/SDK/CloudFormation).
corrections.probe: Is SSE-S3 (AES-256) applied to all new object uploads since Jan 5 2023, and are Block Public Access auto-enabled + ACLs disabled (Object Ownership = BucketOwnerEnforced) for all new buckets since April 2023 across all creation paths?

corrections.claim: Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation (native Lambda for RDS/Redshift/DocumentDB, custom for others); ECS/Fargate injection via task-definition secret references resolved by the task execution role; EKS via ASCP + Secrets Store CSI driver scoped to pods via IRSA.
corrections.correction: Accurate, with a freshness addition: in addition to the Lambda-based rotation templates the draft describes (still available for RDS/Aurora/Redshift/DocumentDB), AWS now also offers fully MANAGED rotation for RDS/Aurora/Redshift/DocumentDB master/admin credentials where you 'never touch Lambda at all'. So rotation is no longer Lambda-only for those engines.
corrections.probe: Does Secrets Manager support automatic rotation with native Lambda for RDS/Redshift/DocumentDB and custom for others, and ECS/EKS injection via execution role / ASCP+CSI scoped by IAM?

corrections.claim: Marketplace runs a self-service AMI/container scan sellers must pass before listing (no known CVEs/malware), AWS reviews submissions against Marketplace policies, and continuously re-scans listings, contacting sellers when new CVEs affect a product.
corrections.probe: Does AWS Marketplace run a self-service AMI/container scan sellers must pass (no known CVEs/malware), review submissions against policy, and continuously re-scan listings, contacting sellers on new CVEs?

corrections.claim: Chris Betz is AWS CISO (since Aug 2023, ex-Capital One CISO).
corrections.correction: Chris Betz was AWS CISO from August 2023 but stepped out of the role in June 2025. Amy Herzog (former MITRE principal security engineer; previously CISO for Amazon's Devices & Services / Media & Entertainment / Advertising businesses) is the current AWS VP & CISO — she keynoted re:Inforce in June 2025 as AWS CISO and gave interviews as CISO in Aug 2025. Chris Betz moved to VP, Infrastructure Security at AWS. The 'since Aug 2023' / 'ex-Capital One' history for Betz is accurate, but he is no longer the sitting AWS CISO.
corrections.probe: Is Chris Betz still the AWS CISO as the draft claims?

corrections.claim: Steve Schmidt is Amazon Chief Security Officer (covering AWS, amazon.com, Devices, Kuiper, etc.).
corrections.correction: Confirmed current. Steve (Stephen) Schmidt remains Amazon CSO as of Dec 2025 (Fortune, Dec 17 2025; The Record 2025), formerly AWS CISO before being elevated to the Amazon-wide CSO role.
corrections.probe: Is Steve Schmidt still Amazon Chief Security Officer?

corrections.claim: The AWS European Sovereign Cloud launched Jan 14 2026 (Brandenburg, Germany).
corrections.correction: The AWS European Sovereign Cloud reached general availability on January 15, 2026 (announced in Potsdam, Germany), not January 14. First Region is in Brandenburg, Germany. It is operated exclusively by EU residents under a new EU parent company and three German GmbH subsidiaries led by EU citizens, with no critical dependencies on non-EU infrastructure — consistent with the draft's jurisdiction framing. Only the date (Jan 14 vs Jan 15) is wrong.
corrections.probe: Exact GA/launch date of the AWS European Sovereign Cloud.

corrections.claim: AWS Security Incident Response (GA Dec 2024) was originally ~$7k/month minimum, repriced Nov 2025 to metered (first 10k findings/month free, then ~$0.000676/finding; free with Enterprise Support / Unified Operations).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. GA at re:Invent Dec 2024 with a $7,000/month minimum tiered on AWS spend. On Nov 21, 2025 AWS switched to metered pricing: first 10,000 findings/month free, then $0.000676/finding with tiered volume discounts; included at no additional cost (and not billed even above the free tier) for Enterprise Support and Unified Operations customers.
corrections.probe: AWS Security Incident Response pricing: original ~$7k/month minimum and Nov 2025 repricing to metered/per-finding.

corrections.claim: The Fall 2025 cycle covers 185 services over a 12-month audit period (Oct 1 2024 - Sep 30 2025).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. AWS Fall 2025 SOC 1/2/3 reports cover 185 services over the audit period October 1, 2024 - September 30, 2025 (up from 184 in Spring 2025).
corrections.probe: AWS SOC Fall 2025 cycle: service count and audit period.

corrections.claim: SOC scope is per-service/per-feature; SageMaker AI in scope but Studio Lab excluded; Bedrock in scope but Bedrock Marketplace excluded; EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, EKS, SageMaker AI, Bedrock all currently in scope.
corrections.correction: Confirmed against the live 'Services in Scope by Compliance Program (SOC)' page. SageMaker AI is in scope but explicitly 'excludes Studio Lab, Public, Workforce and Vendor Workforce'; Bedrock is in scope but 'excludes Amazon Bedrock Marketplace'; EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, EKS are in scope with no exclusions listed.
corrections.probe: Per-service/per-feature SOC scope, including the SageMaker Studio Lab and Bedrock Marketplace exclusions and EC2/S3/Lambda/RDS/EKS inclusion.

corrections.claim: AWS public VDP on hackerone.com/aws_vdp with explicit good-faith/safe-harbor language ('will not bring legal action...including for bypassing technological measures'), non-automated receipt within 24h and monthly check-ins; aws-security@amazon.com with PGP; AWS is a CVE Numbering Authority.
corrections.correction: Confirmed on the AWS Vulnerability Reporting page. URL hackerone.com/aws_vdp; verbatim safe-harbor text including 'bypassing technological measures'; commits to a non-automated receipt confirmation within 24 hours, timely updates, and monthly check-ins; aws-security@amazon.com with a published PGP key; AWS operates the Amazon CNA and issues CVEs.
corrections.probe: Does the AWS public VDP carry safe-harbor language, a 24h non-automated acknowledgment, monthly check-ins, the HackerOne aws_vdp handle, aws-security@amazon.com with PGP, and Amazon CNA status?

corrections.claim: The Amazon Vulnerability Research Program (hackerone.com/amazonvrp, up to ~$25k) is scoped to Amazon retail/consumer/devices/Alexa, NOT core AWS cloud services; AWS-service vulns only have the unpaid public VDP plus a private invite-only bounty.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The AmazonVRP HackerOne policy states testing against AWS and AWS customer assets is strictly out of scope and against the AWS AUP, directing AWS-service findings to the separate AWS Vulnerability Reporting (VDP) page. The public paid channel does not cover core AWS cloud infrastructure.
corrections.probe: Is Amazon's public paid bug bounty (amazonvrp) scoped to consumer/retail/devices and NOT core AWS cloud infrastructure?

corrections.claim: AWS CIRT is engageable by ANY customer via a support case 'regardless of support plan level' at no separate charge; scope limited to AWS infrastructure/control-plane, with OS forensics, memory analysis, and app code review pushed to third-party DFIR partners.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The CIRT announcement states 'Any AWS customer can engage the AWS CIRT through an AWS support case, regardless of support plan level,' and recommends complementing CIRT with a specialized AWS DFIR Partner for host/OS forensics, memory analysis, and application code review. Note: current AWS Security Incident Response user-guide docs now label this team 'Security Incident Response engineers' rather than 'CIRT,' but the engagement-via-support-case-for-all-customers model is unchanged.
corrections.probe: Is the AWS CIRT engageable by ANY customer regardless of support plan at no separate charge, with scope limited to AWS-side analysis and OS/memory/app forensics referred to DFIR partners?

corrections.claim: The Oct 2025 us-east-1 DynamoDB outage post-mortem describes a latent race condition in the DynamoDB DNS Planner/Enactor automation producing an empty DNS record for the regional endpoint, with blast radius across EC2/Lambda/ECS/EKS/STS and remediation disabling the DNS automation worldwide pending a fix.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. AWS's post-event summary attributes the outage to a latent race condition in the DynamoDB DNS management automation (DNS Planner / DNS Enactor) that left an empty/incorrect DNS record for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com and could not self-repair; impacts cascaded to new EC2 launches, Lambda, Fargate/ECS/EKS, etc.; AWS disabled the DynamoDB DNS Planner and Enactor automation worldwide pending a race-condition fix and added safeguards.
corrections.probe: Is the October 2025 us-east-1 DynamoDB outage post-mortem accurately described (DNS Planner/Enactor race condition, empty regional DNS record, blast radius, worldwide disablement of the automation)?

corrections.claim: The Shield Response Team (SRT) is engageable for active DDoS events but is a benefit of the paid AWS Shield Advanced tier (with pre-authorization possible).
corrections.correction: Partially correct but incomplete. SRT does require Shield Advanced AND a Business or Enterprise Support plan — the AWS Shield FAQ states 'you need a Business or Enterprise support plan in order to escalate to or engage the AWS Shield Response Team (SRT).' Framing SRT as solely a Shield Advanced benefit understates the support-plan prerequisite. Pre-authorization of the SRT via an IAM role (AWSShieldDRTAccessPolicy) is confirmed. Shield Standard being always-on, automatic, inline, and free to all customers is also confirmed.
corrections.probe: Is the Shield Response Team (SRT) purely a Shield Advanced benefit, or does it also require a Business/Enterprise Support plan?

corrections.claim: AWS GovCloud (US) is a US-isolated Region with admin access restricted to US Persons, supporting ITAR, FedRAMP High, DoD SRG IL2/4/5, CJIS, and CMMC.
corrections.correction: Confirmed on AWS GovCloud (US) compliance docs: physical/logical access restricted to vetted US Persons/US citizens; supports ITAR, EAR, FedRAMP High, DoD SRG Impact Levels 2/4/5, CJIS, FIPS 140-3, IRS-1075. (CMMC is supported via FedRAMP-aligned controls; the broader IL/ITAR/FedRAMP-High claims are directly attested.)
corrections.probe: Does GovCloud (US) restrict admin access to US Persons and support ITAR/FedRAMP High/DoD SRG IL2/4/5/CJIS/CMMC?

corrections.claim: SOC 1/SOC 2 reports require accepting an AWS NDA via Artifact Agreements before download; SOC 3 is public.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. AWS states an NDA is required to review the SOC 1 and SOC 2 reports (downloaded via AWS Artifact, sharing restricted to company/regulators/auditors), while the SOC 3 report is a publicly available summary requiring no NDA.
corrections.probe: Does AWS Artifact require an NDA for SOC 1/SOC 2 while SOC 3 is public?

corrections.claim: AWS states you can't use IAM policies to deny the root user access; only an Organizations SCP can limit root permissions, with newer centralized-root-access features as an additional lever.
corrections.correction: Accurate and current. IAM docs still state IAM policies cannot deny the root user; SCPs can limit root. The 'newer centralized-root-access features' are real: Centralized Root Access Management launched Nov 2024 (AWS What's New 2024/11), letting org admins remove root credentials from member accounts and perform tightly-scoped privileged tasks via short-term (max 15-min) task-scoped root sessions. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Is it still true that IAM policies cannot deny the root user, and that only Organizations SCPs (plus 'newer centralized-root-access features') can constrain root? Verify the centralized root access feature actually launched and does what the draft implies.

corrections.claim: AWS docs say 'Don't use API keys for authentication or authorization to control access to your APIs,' warn a key valid for one API in a usage plan can access all APIs in that plan, note keys are transmitted in loggable headers, and recommend IAM/Lambda authorizer/Cognito instead.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Current REST API usage-plans doc carries the exact disclaimer, the cross-API access warning, the loggable-header caveat, and the IAM-role/Lambda-authorizer/Cognito recommendation. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Does API Gateway documentation currently and explicitly disclaim API keys as an auth mechanism, with the specific cross-API-access warning and header-logging caveat?

corrections.claim: EC2-style operations return an encoded UnauthorizedOperation message, encoded because authorization detail 'can contain privileged information that the user who requested the operation should not see,' decodable only by a principal with sts:DecodeAuthorizationMessage.
corrections.correction: Accurate. STS DecodeAuthorizationMessage docs and the EC2 re:Post guidance confirm the encoded message, the 'privileged information' rationale verbatim, and the sts:DecodeAuthorizationMessage permission gate. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Is the EC2 encoded-authorization-message behavior (Client.UnauthorizedOperation, encoded message, sts:DecodeAuthorizationMessage gate) still documented with the quoted 'privileged information' rationale?

corrections.claim: IAM AccessDenied messages distinguish explicit deny ('with an explicit deny in a <type> policy', may name policy ARN) from implicit deny ('because no <type> policy allows the <action> action'), with <type> surfacing which policy layer (identity, resource, SCP, permissions boundary, session, RCP) blocked.
corrections.correction: Accurate and current. The IAM troubleshooting doc and re:Post explicit-deny guidance confirm both message templates and the policy-type token. The policy-layer list is current (RCPs/resource control policies are now part of the evaluation chain). No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Do current IAM docs document the explicit-vs-implicit deny AccessDenied message phrasing ('with an explicit deny in a <type> policy' vs 'because no <type> policy allows the <action> action') naming the blocking policy layer?

corrections.claim: AWS documents security groups as ALLOW-only and stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), states they do not filter traffic to/from certain AWS services, steers to NACLs for explicit deny, and discloses NACLs cannot block DNS to the Route 53 Resolver / VPC+2 address.
corrections.correction: Accurate. The current VPC NACL doc explicitly lists 'Network ACLs can't block DNS requests to or from the Route 53 Resolver (also known as the VPC+2 IP address or AmazonProvidedDNS)', the IMDS limitation, and the list of AWS service traffic (DNS, DHCP, EC2 metadata, ECS task metadata, Windows license activation, Time Sync, reserved router IPs) NACLs do not filter. Security-group stateful/allow-only behavior is documented on the same page. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Do current VPC docs still state security groups are allow-only/stateful, that they 'do not filter traffic destined to and from certain AWS services,' and that NACLs cannot block DNS to the Route 53 Resolver (VPC+2)?

corrections.claim: AWS describes a Well-Architected review as 'a constructive conversation about architectural decisions, and is not an audit mechanism,' and the free Well-Architected Tool identifies high-risk issues rather than certifying security.
corrections.correction: Accurate. The current review-process doc carries the exact 'constructive conversation... is not an audit mechanism' language; the WA Tool is documented as a no-cost console tool that identifies high-risk issues (HRIs) and records improvements, not an attestation. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Does AWS still position a Well-Architected review as 'not an audit' and the Well-Architected Tool as identifying high-risk issues rather than certifying?

corrections.claim: AWS states Bedrock prompts/outputs are not used to train the underlying Amazon FMs (absent consent), are not shared with third-party model providers, and model customization creates a private customized copy of the base FM with tuning data through the customer VPC, not used to train base models.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Current Bedrock security page states inputs/outputs are 'never shared with model providers or used to train base models' and fine-tuning creates 'a private copy specifically for your use.' The specific 'tuning data transferred through the customer VPC' detail lives in the Bedrock FAQ rather than this page but is current and consistent. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Are the Amazon Bedrock data-use boundary statements (prompts/outputs not used to train base FMs, not shared with third-party model providers, fine-tuning creates a private copy) current?

corrections.claim: The Service Authorization Reference documents per-action capability: an empty Resource types column means the action does not support resource-level permissions (must specify '*'), and condition keys 'might be supported with an action, or with an action and a specific resource.'
corrections.correction: Accurate. The reference doc's overview confirms the Resource-types-column semantics (empty => specify '*') and that condition keys may apply per-action or per-action-and-resource. The draft's broader inference (List* operations commonly force Resource '*') is consistent with how the per-service tables read. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Does the Service Authorization Reference still use the documented wording about the Resource types column (empty => must use '*') and condition keys varying by action/resource?

corrections.claim: AWS publishes public per-service roadmaps (aws/containers-roadmap) that route security-vulnerability reports away from public Issues to AWS Security, and states it can't provide specific target dates because 'job zero is security.'
corrections.correction: Accurate. The containers-roadmap repo routes security issues to AWS's vulnerability disclosure channel (not public Issues) and states it cannot give specific target dates because job zero is security/operational stability. The 'no single unified public AWS security roadmap' [absent] characterization remains correct. No correction needed.
corrections.probe: Does the aws/containers-roadmap repo still route security vulnerabilities away from public Issues to AWS Security and use the 'job zero is security / no specific target dates' rationale?

corrections.claim: Default subnets have 'auto-assign public IPv4' set to true; instances launched into a default subnet get both a public and private IPv4; nondefault subnets default to private (attribute false).
corrections.probe: Is the auto-assign public IPv4 attribute actually true for default subnets (and false for nondefault subnets), making default-VPC launches public-by-default?

corrections.claim: A newly created security group has NO inbound rules and ONE outbound rule allowing all outbound traffic to 0.0.0.0/0 (::/0 if IPv6); AWS documents the asymmetry and recommends removing the open egress rule.
corrections.probe: Does a newly created (custom) security group really have no inbound rules but one outbound rule allowing all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0, and does AWS recommend removing it?

corrections.claim: Every VPC has an undeletable 'default' security group whose inbound rule allows all traffic from resources in the same SG (self-reference) and whose outbound rule allows all traffic; instances launched without a specified SG are associated with it.
corrections.correction: Fully confirmed (the draft flags this as [partial], but the claim as written is accurate). Default SG: one inbound rule allowing all traffic with the security group itself as source (self-reference), one outbound rule allowing all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0; the default SG cannot be deleted (only its rules can be removed); an instance launched without a specified SG is associated with the VPC's default SG.
corrections.probe: Does the default security group self-reference on inbound and allow all outbound, is it undeletable, and is it auto-associated with instances launched without a specified SG?

corrections.claim: AWS Network Firewall offers stateful domain allow/deny lists (HTTP Host, HTTPS SNI), Suricata-compatible rules, and — as of 2026 — URL and domain-category filtering.
corrections.correction: Confirmed and correctly dated. Web/URL/Domain category filtering launched January 27, 2026. aws_domain_category evaluates domain via TLS SNI or HTTP Host with no TLS inspection required; aws_url_category evaluates full URL paths and requires TLS inspection for HTTPS traffic. ~55 AWS-managed categories (e.g., Malicious, Social Networking, AI/ML), auto-maintained. The older stateful domain allow/deny (SNI/Host) and Suricata-compatible capabilities are long-standing.
corrections.probe: Does AWS Network Firewall actually provide URL and domain-category filtering 'as of 2026', and is the SNI-vs-TLS-inspection distinction correct?

corrections.claim: Gateway endpoints provide private routing to S3 and DynamoDB with no IGW/NAT and no additional charge; described under the 'AWS PrivateLink' heading alongside interface endpoints.
corrections.correction: Pricing claim confirmed: gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB carry no additional charge (no hourly/data-processing fee). Minor framing nuance: AWS documents that gateway endpoints do NOT use AWS PrivateLink technology (only interface endpoints do), so grouping gateway endpoints under 'PrivateLink' is slightly imprecise even though the functional description (private ENIs/private routing, traffic on the AWS backbone) is correct.
corrections.probe: Are gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB really free, and is calling them 'PrivateLink' accurate?

corrections.claim: AWS Client VPN provides managed OpenVPN-based remote access with IAM/SAML/AD authentication and authorization rules.
corrections.correction: Client VPN is OpenVPN-based (confirmed) and supports exactly three authentication types: mutual (client certificate) authentication, Active Directory authentication (via AWS Directory Service), and SAML 2.0 federated authentication. There is no standalone 'IAM' authentication mode — IAM Identity Center / SSO works through the SAML federated path. The draft should say 'mutual certificate / Active Directory / SAML federated (incl. IAM Identity Center via SAML)', and authorization rules (per-network, group-based) are correctly described.
corrections.probe: Does AWS Client VPN have an 'IAM' authentication mode, or is the draft's 'IAM/SAML/AD' list imprecise?

corrections.claim: SSM Session Manager gives shell access with no inbound ports, no SSH keys, no bastion; outbound from the SSM Agent over the AWS API; IAM-gated; every session logged to CloudTrail and optionally streamed to S3/CloudWatch Logs including keystroke logging.
corrections.probe: Does SSM Session Manager provide IAM-gated, portless, keyless access with CloudTrail logging and optional S3/CloudWatch session (including keystroke) logging?

corrections.claim: Direct Connect provides dedicated private fiber, optionally with MACsec encryption and a Direct Connect gateway for multi-VPC/multi-region.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE, 256-bit, GCM-AES-256/GCM-AES-XPN-256) is supported on dedicated 10/100/400 Gbps Direct Connect connections at select locations; as of July 2025 MACsec was also extended to supported partner interconnects. Direct Connect gateway for multi-VPC/multi-region is standard.
corrections.probe: Does AWS Direct Connect support MACsec encryption on dedicated connections (and a Direct Connect gateway for multi-VPC/region)?

corrections.claim: From Oct 2025 new function URLs require BOTH lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl and lambda:InvokeFunction; choosing AuthType NONE via console/SAM auto-creates the public FunctionURLAllowPublicAccess (Principal '*') resource policy; AuthType is mandatory with no implicit default.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Current docs carry the note 'Starting in October 2025, new function URLs will require both lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl and lambda:InvokeFunction permissions.' For NONE, 'When you create a function URL with auth type NONE using the console or AWS SAM, Lambda automatically creates the resource-based policy for you' (the FunctionURLAllowPublicAccess statement with Principal '*'). AuthType must be specified (AWS_IAM or NONE) at creation. Note: AWS has granted a temporary exception with a Nov 1, 2026 deadline for existing URLs to add the second permission, so the practical enforcement window extends into 2026 — but the requirement for new URLs began Oct 2025 as stated.
corrections.probe: Verify whether new Lambda Function URLs require BOTH lambda:InvokeFunctionUrl and lambda:InvokeFunction as of Oct 2025, and whether choosing NONE auto-creates the FunctionURLAllowPublicAccess public resource policy.

corrections.claim: API Gateway docs state plainly: don't use API keys for authentication/authorization; a user with a valid API key for one API in a usage plan can access ALL APIs in that usage plan; key value is 20-128 chars, transmitted in headers that can be logged.
corrections.correction: Accurate and matches current docs verbatim, including 'Don't use API keys for authentication or authorization...a user with a valid API key for one API in that usage plan can access all APIs in that usage plan' and the 20-128 character value range and 'headers that can be logged.'
corrections.probe: Confirm the exact wording and current presence of the API Gateway docs statement that API keys are not for auth and that a key for one API in a usage plan grants access to all APIs in that plan.

corrections.claim: AWS explicitly says: 'Usage plan throttling and quotas are not hard limits, and are applied on a best-effort basis. In some cases, clients can exceed the quotas... Don't rely on usage plan quotas or throttling to control costs or block access to an API.' and redirects to AWS Budgets and AWS WAF.
corrections.correction: Accurate; the quoted disclaimer and the redirect to AWS Budgets (monitoring) and AWS WAF appear in current docs verbatim.
corrections.probe: Confirm the current API Gateway usage-plan disclaimer that throttling/quotas are best-effort, not hard limits, and not to be relied on for cost control.

corrections.claim: IAM and Lambda authorizers reached HTTP APIs in 2020.
corrections.correction: Accurate. AWS announced IAM and Lambda authorizers for HTTP APIs on September 25, 2020.
corrections.probe: Verify that IAM authorization and Lambda authorizers reached API Gateway HTTP APIs in 2020.

corrections.claim: ALB OIDC/Cognito passes claims in x-amzn-oidc-data JWT; AWS best-practices guidance says the target must verify the JWT signature against the regional public key AND confirm signer == expected ALB ARN, else attackers can fabricate x-amzn-oidc-* headers; and targets must be network-isolated or clients reach the backend directly and bypass ALB auth.
corrections.correction: Accurate, and now reinforced by the 2024 'ALBeast' disclosures (CVE-2024-8901 / AWS-2024-011 et al.): AWS updated guidance to require validating the JWT signature and confirming the 'signer' header equals the expected ALB ARN, plus network-isolating targets (no public IPs; SG accepts ALB-only). The draft's 'partial' rating is reasonable; the underlying claims are correct.
corrections.probe: Confirm the ALB authenticate-oidc/authenticate-cognito header-spoofing footgun: target must verify JWT signature against the regional public key AND confirm the signer equals the expected ALB ARN, and targets must be network-isolated.

corrections.claim: CloudFront Functions are sub-ms/JS-restricted and CANNOT reach KMS or Secrets Manager; Lambda@Edge viewer-request allows up to 5s and has full runtime and secret access for JWT crypto validation.
corrections.correction: Accurate. CloudFront Functions cannot call other AWS services (no KMS/Secrets Manager); Lambda@Edge viewer-request functions have a 5-second max timeout (128 MB memory) and can assume an execution role to reach Secrets Manager/KMS.
corrections.probe: Verify CloudFront Functions cannot reach KMS/Secrets Manager and that Lambda@Edge viewer-request timeout is 5s with full runtime/secret access.

corrections.claim: On a WAF internal error, 'CloudFront typically allows the request, while Regional services typically deny the request' (CloudFront fails open; regional fails closed). A Web ACL protects nothing until explicitly associated; no account-wide default WAF.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Current AWS WAF docs state that when WAF can't evaluate a request due to an internal error, CloudFront typically allows/serves the request while Regional services typically deny it. Web ACLs must be explicitly associated per resource; there is no account-wide default.
corrections.probe: Confirm AWS WAF's documented fail-open on CloudFront vs fail-closed on Regional services on internal error.

corrections.claim: Since May 2025 the WAF rate-based minimum limit is 10 requests (was 100); since Mar 2024 the evaluation window is selectable 60/120/300/600s (default 300s).
corrections.correction: The minimum-limit-of-10 feature is real and current, but the DATE is wrong. AWS announced lowering the minimum rate limit from 100 to 10 on August 30, 2024 — not May 2025. (Some third-party blogs mislabel it 'May 2025'; the authoritative AWS What's New page is dated Aug 30, 2024.) Correct the draft to: 'Since August 2024 the minimum limit is 10 requests (was 100).' The selectable evaluation-window claim (60/120/300/600s, default 300s, announced March 2024) is confirmed correct.
corrections.probe: Verify the AWS WAF rate-based-rule minimum-limit-of-10 change date (draft says 'Since May 2025') and the selectable 60/120/300/600s evaluation window with 300s default (draft says Mar 2024).

corrections.claim: Docs state the mechanism 'is not intended for precise request-rate limiting,' that it estimates rate with a recency-weighted algorithm, that 'it's possible for requests to be coming in at too high a rate for up to several minutes before AWS WAF detects and rate limits them,' and that changing rate settings resets counters and can pause limiting for up to a minute.
corrections.correction: Accurate; all three caveats (not precise / recency-weighted estimate, up-to-several-minutes detection lag, and 'change... resets the rule's rate limiting counts... can pause... for up to a minute') appear in current docs verbatim.
corrections.probe: Confirm WAF rate-based-rule caveats: not for precise rate limiting; possible multi-minute detection lag; changing rate settings resets counters and can pause limiting up to a minute.

corrections.claim: Shield Standard is default-on and free for all AWS customers (L3/L4 at CloudFront/Route 53/Global Accelerator/ELB, no enablement). Shield Advanced is opt-in paid: $3,000/month, 1-year commitment, plus data-transfer fees, adding L7, DRT, cost protection, tighter WAF integration.
corrections.correction: Accurate. Shield Standard is automatic and free for all customers. Shield Advanced is $3,000/month per organization with a 1-year subscription commitment plus usage (data-transfer-out) fees, and adds L7 detection/mitigation, the DDoS Response Team, cost-protection credits, and included WAF for protected resources.
corrections.probe: Verify Shield Standard is default-on and free for all customers (edge/L3-L4), and Shield Advanced is opt-in paid at $3,000/month with a 1-year commitment plus data-transfer fees.

corrections.claim: There is a hard, non-raisable 10 MB REST-API payload limit; body validation is opt-in (defining a model does NOT enforce it; you must attach a validator to the method) and 'if no matching content type is found, request validation is not performed' (silent skip).
corrections.correction: Accurate. The 10 MB REST-API payload quota cannot be increased. Validation requires creating a request validator and assigning it to the method; current docs state 'If no matching content type is found, request validation is not performed' (the documented silent-skip), with $default available to apply one model regardless of content type.
corrections.probe: Confirm the API Gateway REST API 10 MB payload limit is hard/non-raisable, and that body validation requires attaching a validator (defining a model alone does not enforce) with silent-skip when no matching content type is found.

corrections.claim: AWS CodeCommit is closed to new customers; onboarding ceased ~July 25, 2024; only accounts with a pre-existing repository can create new repositories; AWS commits to no new features. A fresh org cannot use a first-party IAM-native Git host and must use external SCM via CodeConnections or self-host.
corrections.correction: REVERSED. AWS CodeCommit returned to FULL General Availability on November 24, 2025. New customer sign-ups are open again: 'New customer sign-ups are open as of today... you can do so right now through the AWS Console, CLI, or APIs.' AWS also reversed its no-new-features stance and is now investing in the service, including Git LFS support (announced for Q1 2026) and regional expansion to eu-south-2 and ca-west-1 (starting Q3 2026). The 2024 closure was real (onboarding ceased ~July 25, 2024, existing customers retained) but is no longer the current state. The draft's downstream conclusion ('a multi-team org standing up fresh accounts... cannot use a first-party, IAM-native Git host and must integrate an external SCM') is now FALSE: new accounts can create CodeCommit repositories directly again. This is the single most significant correction for this signal.
corrections.probe: Is AWS CodeCommit still closed to new customers (the draft's central [absent] finding)?

corrections.claim: Security Hub control CodeBuild.1 flags source repository URLs that embed a PAT or username/password as Critical, steering users off PATs and onto the connection model (draft frames it broadly across providers).
corrections.correction: Severity is confirmed Critical. One precision: the current control title and description are scoped specifically to Bitbucket source repository URLs ('[CodeBuild.1] CodeBuild Bitbucket source repository URLs should not contain sensitive credentials'). The draft's later [partial] CodeBuild finding correctly notes the Bitbucket scope, but the lead [present] finding generalizes it to all 'source repository URLs.' Mechanism (steers off PATs toward OAuth/connections) is accurate.
corrections.probe: Is Security Hub control CodeBuild.1 actually Critical severity, and is it scoped to Bitbucket or all source providers?

corrections.claim: CodeBuild.2 fails if a project defines plaintext AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY env vars (Critical-severity).
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Current docs list CodeBuild.2 severity as Critical; it checks for clear-text AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY environment variables.
corrections.probe: Is Security Hub control CodeBuild.2 (plaintext AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY env vars) Critical severity?

corrections.claim: CodeBuild.4 requires at least one log destination (CloudWatch/S3) and CodeBuild.3 wants those S3 logs encrypted.
corrections.correction: Both controls exist with the described checks. Note severities for context: CodeBuild.4 is Medium and CodeBuild.3 is Low (the draft did not assert their severity, so no factual error, but these are not Critical). The draft's claim that these detective controls are opt-in (require Security Hub + the standard enabled) is accurate.
corrections.probe: Are CodeBuild.4 (logging config) and CodeBuild.3 (S3 logs encrypted) severities as implied, and do they exist?

corrections.claim: Privileged mode is OFF by default ('By default, Docker containers do not allow access to any devices'); when enabled it is effectively root-on-the-build-host.
corrections.correction: The default-off behavior and the quoted text are confirmed in current docs. Caveat for the broader assessment: the Security Hub control that enforced this (CodeBuild.5, 'CodeBuild project environments should not have privileged mode enabled') was RETIRED in April 2024. So while privileged mode is still off by default, there is no longer a Security Hub control flagging projects that enable it. The draft's privileged-mode finding does not cite CodeBuild.5, so it is not directly wrong, but any reliance on a detective control for privileged mode would be stale.
corrections.probe: Does the draft's privileged-mode claim rely on a still-active Security Hub control, and is privileged mode off by default?

corrections.claim: CodeConnections CloudTrail examples are all eventCategory Management (managementEvent: true); doc surfaces no data-event for GetConnectionToken nor any record of which repository content was cloned/fetched. UseConnection is among the logged calls.
corrections.correction: Confirmed with one nuance. The CloudTrail logging doc's worked examples are CreateConnection, DeleteConnection, GetConnection, and CreateHost, all with managementEvent: true / eventCategory Management. There is NO GetConnectionToken or UseConnection example in the doc, and no data-event logging for credential vending or git clone/fetch of repo content (those git operations hit GitHub, not an AWS API). The core finding holds. Minor: the draft lists 'UseConnection, etc.' among logged calls; UseConnection is not shown as an example in the CloudTrail doc (it is an IAM action / appears in EventBridge and IAM contexts), so that specific item is unverified by the cited doc.
corrections.probe: Does CloudTrail log CodeConnections only as management events, with no data event for token-vend (GetConnectionToken) or git fetch?

corrections.claim: The AWS Connector for GitHub requests broad read AND write (code, commit statuses, pull requests, repo administration, repo/org webhooks). GitHub permits only ONE installation per org; every CodeConnection targeting that org shares the one install and its permission set; no AWS-side facility to scope a connection to a repo subset or read-only; a connection can be shared across AWS accounts.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The app requests read+write to administration, code, commit statuses, pull requests, and repository hooks, plus read+write to organization hooks (and org self-hosted runners). GitHub enforces one app installation per organization, so all CodeConnections to that org share the single installation and its full permission set. Cross-account sharing via AWS RAM is supported, widening blast radius. The only scoping lever is the GitHub-side repository allowlist at install time (and GitLab/Bitbucket lack even that); there is no AWS-side per-connection repo-subset or read-only scoping.
corrections.probe: Does the AWS Connector for GitHub App request broad read/write permissions, with one-install-per-org and cross-account sharing?

corrections.claim: The CodePipeline console explicitly states 'you can select specific repositories under Repository Access. Any repositories that are not selected will not be accessible or visible by CodePipeline.'
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim. Current CodePipeline GitHub connections doc states: 'In these steps, you can select specific repositories under Repository Access. Any repositories that are not selected will not be accessible or visible by CodePipeline.'
corrections.probe: Does the CodePipeline console doc state that unselected repos under Repository Access are not accessible/visible?

corrections.claim: AWS's Security blog shows only a correctly-scoped single sub example (e.g. repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/branch) and does NOT warn against the wildcard/omitted-sub failure mode; GitHub later added immutable claims like repository_id.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. The AWS Security blog 'Use IAM roles to connect GitHub Actions to actions in AWS' shows a correctly-scoped sub condition (repo:<org/EXAMPLEREPO>:ref:refs/heads/<ExampleBranch>) but does NOT warn against wildcard subs (repo:org/*) or an omitted sub condition, and does not mention the confused-deputy risk. The caution lives in third-party writeups and GitHub's docs. GitHub did add immutable condition keys including repository_id (numeric, survives renames) recommended for production trust policies.
corrections.probe: Does the AWS Security blog on GitHub Actions OIDC scope the sub claim but omit any warning about wildcard/omitted-sub (confused deputy)?

corrections.claim: CodeBuild on-demand fleets provision compute per build and DESTROY the machine when the build finishes (ephemeral, no reuse by default); reserved-capacity fleets (opt-in) deliberately persist hosts to cut latency.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Current docs: with on-demand fleets, CodeBuild provides compute and the machines are destroyed when the build finishes. Reserved-capacity fleets maintain a set of dedicated, persistent EC2-backed instances that remain available (and incur cost) to cut startup latency. The isolation-vs-speed tradeoff framing is accurate.
corrections.probe: Are CodeBuild on-demand fleets ephemeral (destroyed after build) while reserved-capacity fleets persist hosts?

corrections.claim: AWS Organizations default account quota is 10, raisable to 50,000.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Quota table: 'Maximum number of accounts | 10 ... Limit increases can be granted up to 50,000 accounts based on customer qualifications.' Only the management account can submit the increase. (Note: newly created accounts/orgs may start below 10.)
corrections.probe: Default cap is 10 accounts per org, adjustable via Service Quotas up to 50,000.

corrections.claim: Max 2000 OUs per org; OUs nestable up to 5 levels deep under a root.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. 'Number of OUs in an organization: 2000' and 'OU maximum nesting in a root: Five levels of OUs deep under a root.'
corrections.probe: OUs: max 2000 OUs, nestable up to 5 levels deep under a root.

corrections.claim: Min 4-day age before removal of a created account; max 5 member accounts created concurrently.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. 'Minimum age for removal of created accounts: 4' days, and 'Number of member accounts you can create concurrently: 5.'
corrections.probe: Created accounts must exist 4 days before removal; only 5 accounts can be created concurrently.

corrections.claim: Every entity gets a default FullAWSAccess SCP; remove it and all member-account actions fail.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. 'You should not remove the FullAWSAccess policy unless you modify or replace it ... otherwise all AWS actions from member accounts will fail.' SCP minimum-per-entity is 1 (you can't remove the last SCP when SCPs are enabled).
corrections.probe: SCP default FullAWSAccess on every root/OU/account; removing it blocks all actions.

corrections.claim: SCPs do not affect management-account principals, service-linked roles, and specified root-user tasks.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim in 'Tasks and entities not restricted by SCPs': management-account actions, service-linked-role actions, Enterprise support signup as root, CloudFront trusted signer for private content, and reverse DNS for Lightsail email server / EC2 instance as root (plus Alexa Top Sites, Alexa Web Information Service, Mechanical Turk, Product Marketing API).
corrections.probe: SCP exemptions: management account, service-linked roles, and root-user tasks (Enterprise Support signup, CloudFront trusted signer, Lightsail/EC2 reverse DNS).

corrections.claim: SCPs only constrain in-org principals; they don't affect resource-based policies directly or external-account principals.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim: 'SCPs don't affect resource-based policies directly. They also don't affect users or roles from accounts outside the organization' — illustrated with the cross-account S3 bucket-policy example the draft uses.
corrections.probe: SCPs don't affect resource-based policies directly and don't affect users/roles from accounts outside the org (external-principal blind spot).

corrections.claim: RCPs reached GA on Nov 13, 2024.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. What's New announcement dated Nov 13, 2024. At launch RCPs covered only 5 services (S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager); the list has since expanded.
corrections.probe: RCPs launched/GA Nov 13, 2024 as the resource-side complement to SCPs.

corrections.claim: RCPs currently cover ~24 services including DynamoDB, ECR, CloudWatch Logs, Cognito.
corrections.correction: Confirmed as current (June 2026): the RCP-supported-services list now contains exactly 24 services, including all four named (Cognito, CloudWatch Logs, DynamoDB, ECR) plus the original five and others (AppConfig, AppStream, EC2 Auto Scaling, CodeBuild, CodeCommit, Comprehend, Comprehend Medical, DAX, Health, Kinesis Video Streams, OpenSearch Serverless, Support, Textract, Transcribe, Translate). Note this grew from 5 at launch, so '~24' is accurate today but is the kind of count that will keep moving.
corrections.probe: RCPs apply to a subset of ~24 services -- notably S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager, plus DynamoDB, ECR, CloudWatch Logs, Cognito, etc.

corrections.claim: RCPFullAWSAccess is mandatory, non-detachable, and counts toward a 5-per-entity RCP limit.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Quota table: RCP minimum-per-entity is 1 because 'The RCPFullAWSAccess policy is automatically attached ... You cannot detach this policy and it counts towards the 5 policies quota.' Max RCPs per root/OU/account = 5.
corrections.probe: RCPFullAWSAccess auto-attached to root/every OU/every account, cannot be detached, counts toward a 5-RCP-per-entity quota; RCPs opt-in to author.

corrections.claim: RCP carve-outs: management account, service-linked roles, AWS-managed KMS keys; RCPs bind external/root principals.
corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim under 'Resources and entities not restricted by RCPs': no effect on management-account resources, no effect on service-linked roles, and 'RCPs do not apply to AWS managed keys for AWS KMS.' Docs also confirm RCPs apply 'regardless of whether the principals belong to the same organizations or not. This includes root users.'
corrections.probe: RCPs do NOT affect the management account, do NOT affect service-linked roles, and do NOT apply to AWS-managed KMS keys; enforce even on external-principal access.

corrections.claim: Declarative policies (Dec 2024) cover EC2/EBS/VPC with the listed attributes plus an account status report.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. What's New (Dec 2024): declarative policies support EC2, VPC, EBS; attributes include Instance Metadata Defaults (IMDSv2), Image Block Public Access (AMI BPA), Snapshot Block Public Access, VPC Block Public Access, Allowed Image (AMI) Settings, and Serial Console Access. The 'account status report' is included.
corrections.probe: Declarative policies announced Dec 2024; at launch cover EC2, EBS, VPC only; enforce IMDSv2, EBS encryption-by-default, EBS-snapshot/AMI block public access, allowed-AMI, VPC BPA, serial-console; ship an account status report.

corrections.claim: Declarative-policy quotas: 10 per entity, 10,000-char document, 1000 per org.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Quota tables: Declarative policies max attached per root/OU/account = 10; document max size = 10,000 characters; max declarative policies in an org = 1000.
corrections.probe: Declarative policies: up to 10 per entity, 10,000-char limit, 1000 per org.

corrections.claim: Centralized root access management has two opt-in capabilities and uses sts:AssumeRoot for privileged actions.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Two features: 'root credentials management' and 'privileged root actions in member accounts.' Enablement is via the EnableOrganizationsRootCredentialsManagement / EnableOrganizationsRootSessions APIs (requires IAM trusted access in Organizations). Privileged root tasks use the STS AssumeRoot API with a task-scoped policy and max 15-minute session. Opt-in, not default.
corrections.probe: Centralized root access mgmt (Nov 2024): two independently selectable capabilities (Root credentials management; Privileged root actions), opt-in via IAM trusted access + EnableOrganizationsRootCredentialsManagement / EnableOrganizationsRootSessions; uses sts:AssumeRoot.

corrections.claim: Control Tower control mechanisms and the Nov 2024 RCP-based managed controls.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Preventive = SCPs, detective = AWS Config rules (+Lambda), proactive = CloudFormation Hooks; landing zone creates Log Archive and Audit accounts. Nov 2024: Control Tower launched configurable AWS-managed preventive controls implemented via RCPs (initially for S3, STS, KMS, SQS, Secrets Manager). Minor completeness note for the draft: in Dec 2024 Control Tower also added managed controls implemented via declarative policies, not just RCPs.
corrections.probe: Control Tower controls: preventive=SCPs, detective=Config rules, proactive=CloudFormation hooks; creates Log Archive + Audit accounts; as of Nov 2024 also offers configurable managed controls implemented via RCPs.

corrections.claim: SCIM does not expand nested groups; Entra ID does not recursively unpack indirectly-assigned members.
corrections.correction: Confirmed. Microsoft documents that the Entra provisioning service 'cannot read or provision users in nested groups. Only users that are immediate members of an explicitly assigned group can be read and provisioned' and it 'doesn't recursively unpack the group memberships of indirectly assigned users or groups.' This is an IdP-side limitation that surfaces in IAM Identity Center.
corrections.probe: IAM Identity Center SCIM: nested groups not supported; with Entra ID, indirectly-assigned (nested) members are not unpacked -- only direct members of an explicitly assigned group sync.

corrections.claim: Multivalue attributes are not provisioned via SCIM and attempts fail.
