corrections.correction: Confirmed verbatim: 'Currently, multivalue attributes (such as multiple emails or phone numbers for a given user) are not provisioned with SCIM. Attempts to synchronize multivalue attributes into IAM Identity Center with SCIM will fail.'
corrections.probe: IAM Identity Center SCIM: multivalue attributes (multiple emails/phones) are not provisioned and will fail.

corrections.claim: The IAM Identity Center account quota is 3000 and labeled an 'account-instance' quota.
corrections.correction: The NUMBER is right (3000, raisable) but the LABEL is wrong. The 3000 quota is 'Total number of AWS accounts or applications that can be configured' for an IAM Identity Center instance -- a COMBINED accounts-plus-applications quota (e.g., 2750 accounts + 250 apps = 3000). It is NOT an 'account-instance quota': an 'account instance' is a separate IAM Identity Center concept (limited to 1 per AWS account and notably does not support permission sets). Reword to: 'The combined accounts-or-applications quota per organization instance is 3000 (raisable).'
corrections.probe: IAM Identity Center 'Account-instance quota is 3000 (raisable).'

corrections.claim: Org-wide security telemetry is opt-in: delegated admin must enable each service and set auto-enable; not default-on.
corrections.correction: Confirmed in substance. The Organizations 'services that work with Organizations' set includes all the named services as delegated-administration-capable, and centralized root access is a delegable IAM capability (confirmed separately). GuardDuty/Security Hub support an auto-enable-for-new-accounts preference, and none of these services protects accounts until the delegated admin explicitly enables it and sets the auto-enable preference -- i.e., security telemetry is opt-in, not default-on. (Note: GuardDuty/Security Hub auto-enable now favor an explicit account-config/'auto-enable' model over the older legacy ALL setting; the draft's ALL/NEW framing is directionally correct.)
corrections.probe: Delegated administration is supported for the listed security services (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config, IAM Access Analyzer, Macie, Detective, Inspector, Audit Manager, Firewall Manager, CloudTrail, centralized root access); GuardDuty/Security Hub auto-enable; none enabled by default at org level.

recommendations.title: Durable, immutable, org-wide CloudTrail including S3 object-level data events on IP stores
recommendations.rationale: Default CloudTrail is a 90-day rolling buffer of management events only; S3 object-level reads (the crown-jewel exfil signal for source/model weights) are data events that are OFF by default. Without this, mass download of IP produces no durable trace.

audit.how_to_check: aws cloudtrail list-trails; for the org trail: aws cloudtrail get-trail --name <t> (expect IsOrganizationTrail=true, IsMultiRegionTrail=true, LogFileValidationEnabled=true); aws cloudtrail get-event-selectors --trail-name <t> (expect AdvancedEventSelectors capturing AWS::S3::Object for IP buckets); confirm the destination bucket is in a dedicated log-archive account with S3 Object Lock enabled and a delete-deny bucket policy.
audit.pass_criterion: Org-wide multi-region trail delivering to a dedicated log-archive account; log file validation ON; S3 Object Lock (compliance mode) + delete-deny on the destination; advanced event selectors capture S3 object-level reads on every bucket holding source/model weights.
audit.remediation: Create an organization trail from the management or delegated-admin account; enable multi-region + log file validation; add advanced event selectors for AWS::S3::Object on sensitive buckets; lock the destination bucket with Object Lock and an org-scoped delete-deny policy.

recommendations.title: Org-wide GuardDuty with the relevant protection plans, delegated admin, and auto-enable for new accounts
recommendations.rationale: GuardDuty's 'default-on' covers only the base feeders; the protection plans that matter for IP (S3 Protection, Malware, EKS/Runtime, RDS, Lambda) are per-plan opt-in. A vanilla enablement leaves the most relevant detections off, and new vended accounts can land uncovered.

audit.how_to_check: aws guardduty list-detectors; aws guardduty get-detector --detector-id <id> (Status=ENABLED); aws guardduty describe-organization-configuration --detector-id <id> (AutoEnableOrganizationMembers=ALL; each desired Feature AutoEnable=NEW/ALL); confirm a delegated administrator account is set at the Organizations level.
audit.pass_criterion: GuardDuty enabled in every active account/region via a delegated security account, with S3/Malware/EKS-Runtime/RDS/Lambda plans auto-enabled org-wide AND auto-enabled for new accounts.
audit.remediation: Designate a delegated GuardDuty admin; enable auto-enable=ALL for members and for each protection plan; backfill existing accounts; add a Config/Security Hub control alerting when a new account or region is uncovered.

recommendations.title: Build the data perimeter: identity perimeter (SCP), resource perimeter (RCP), and VPC endpoint policies
recommendations.rationale: For a multi-team org whose threat is IP exfiltration, the highest-leverage control is a coordinated perimeter: credentials can't be used from outside your networks (SCP + aws:SourceVpc/aws:PrincipalOrgID), and your data can't be copied to an attacker-controlled account (RCP + aws:PrincipalOrgID on the resource side). RCPs (Nov 2024) close the resource-policy blind spot SCPs never covered.

audit.how_to_check: aws organizations list-policies --filter SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY and --filter RESOURCE_CONTROL_POLICY; confirm an RCP enforcing aws:PrincipalOrgID is attached at the root/OU and covers S3, STS, SQS, KMS (and Bedrock/SageMaker/RDS where supported); confirm VPC endpoint policies on S3/Bedrock/STS endpoints restrict to the org; confirm SCPs deny credential use without aws:SourceVpc/aws:ViaAWSService where applicable.
audit.pass_criterion: An RCP enforcing aws:PrincipalOrgID is attached org-wide and covers the services holding IP; SCPs constrain credential origin; VPC endpoint policies scope access to the organization — composed as documented in the AWS data-perimeter guidance.
audit.remediation: Adopt the AWS data-perimeter reference policies; roll out RCPs in audit/log mode first, then enforce; add aws:PrincipalOrgID resource-perimeter conditions per service; verify RCP service coverage for Bedrock/SageMaker/EC2-EBS/Lambda/RDS/EKS and compensate with resource policies where a service isn't yet RCP-supported.

recommendations.title: Eliminate standing credentials: enforce IMDSv2 fleet-wide, retire IAM user access keys, and centralize/remove root
recommendations.rationale: Long-lived IAM access keys are a top breach vector and IMDSv1 enables SSRF credential theft; neither is closed by default. Existing long-lived instances remain IMDSv1-reachable, and member-account root credentials are a latent blast radius.

audit.how_to_check: aws accessanalyzer (unused-access findings) or IAM credential report for active user access keys >90 days; aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].MetadataOptions.HttpTokens' (expect 'required'); confirm a Declarative Policy or SCP enforces IMDSv2 at launch; in Organizations confirm centralized root access management is enabled (member-account root credentials removed).
audit.pass_criterion: No long-lived IAM user access keys for humans (SSO/role-based instead); HttpTokens=required across the running fleet and enforced at launch org-wide; member-account root credentials centralized/removed.
audit.remediation: Move humans to IAM Identity Center; rotate/retire IAM user keys; set a Declarative Policy enforcing IMDSv2 at launch and run a campaign to flip existing instances to HttpTokens=required; enable Organizations centralized root access management.

recommendations.title: Security Hub org-wide aggregation with CIS/FSBP standards and auto-enable
recommendations.rationale: Security Hub is the cross-account posture brain (CSPM scores, ASFF/OCSF normalization) but is opt-in per account/region and must be configured to aggregate and auto-enable; absent it, posture drift across many accounts is invisible.

audit.how_to_check: aws securityhub describe-hub; aws securityhub get-enabled-standards (expect AWS FSBP + CIS); aws securityhub list-organization-admin-accounts; confirm a finding aggregator (cross-region) and auto-enable for new org accounts.
audit.pass_criterion: Security Hub enabled in all accounts/regions via a delegated admin, with FSBP + CIS standards on, cross-region aggregation configured, and auto-enable for new accounts.
audit.remediation: Delegate Security Hub to the security account; enable central configuration with a policy that turns on standards and auto-enables new accounts; wire findings to your SIEM/ticketing.

recommendations.title: AWS Config org-wide recording plus conformance packs for encryption and public-access drift
recommendations.rationale: Config recording underpins Control Tower detective guardrails and conformance packs, but it is not on by default in every account/region; without it, drift detection (public S3, unencrypted EBS, open SGs) silently does not run.

audit.how_to_check: aws configservice describe-configuration-recorders and describe-configuration-recorder-status (recording=true) per account/region; aws configservice describe-conformance-packs (expect encryption/public-access packs); confirm org-wide aggregation.
audit.pass_criterion: Configuration recording is ON in every active account/region, aggregated to the security account, with conformance packs evaluating encryption-at-rest and public-exposure rules.
audit.remediation: Enable Config recording org-wide (Control Tower or a StackSet); deploy encryption + public-access conformance packs; alert on non-compliant resources holding IP.

recommendations.title: Confused-deputy hygiene: external IDs, aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount, and OIDC sub-claim conditions
recommendations.rationale: Cross-account vendor roles without an external ID, service resource policies without aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount, and GitHub Actions OIDC trust policies with a missing/wildcard sub claim each silently expose IP to an outside principal — all opt-in conditions AWS does not add for you.

audit.how_to_check: Review third-party AssumeRole trust policies for sts:ExternalId conditions; review S3/SNS/SQS/KMS/CloudTrail resource policies for aws:SourceArn/aws:SourceAccount; review every GitHub OIDC trust policy for an exact token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub condition (repo:ORG/REPO:* scoped, no wildcard org). IAM Access Analyzer external-access findings surface unintended cross-account/public reach.
audit.pass_criterion: Every vendor role requires a unique external ID; service resource policies pin aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount; every OIDC trust policy has an exact, non-wildcard sub-claim condition; Access Analyzer external-access analyzer is enabled org-wide with zero unexpected findings.
audit.remediation: Add the missing conditions; enable IAM Access Analyzer (external + unused) with a delegated admin; add CI custom-policy checks (CheckNoPublicAccess, CheckAccessNotGranted) to block regressions.

recommendations.title: Encryption-at-rest defaults: EBS encryption-by-default and per-team KMS CMKs with scoped key policies
recommendations.rationale: EBS encryption-by-default is opt-in per account/region (a new GPU account/region can create unencrypted volumes/snapshots of training data), and the stock KMS key policy delegates kms:* to the account root, so a CMK alone does NOT isolate data from any broad IAM admin in the same account.

audit.how_to_check: aws ec2 get-ebs-encryption-by-default per account/region (expect true); inspect CMK key policies for the stock 'Enable IAM User Permissions' kms:* to root without scoping; confirm per-team CMKs and that cross-team decrypt is constrained by key policy + grants, ideally with account separation.
audit.pass_criterion: EBS encryption-by-default = true in every account/region bound to a CMK; per-team CMKs whose key policies do not grant blanket kms:* to root, so decrypt authority maps to the owning team.
audit.remediation: Enable EBS encryption-by-default org-wide (Declarative Policy/automation) bound to a CMK; replace stock key policies with scoped ones; separate the most sensitive IP into dedicated accounts so the account boundary backs the KMS boundary.

recommendations.title: Supply-chain integrity: ECR enhanced scanning, image signing, and verification enforcement
recommendations.rationale: ECR signing (AWS Signer) is off by default and there is NO native AWS-managed gate that blocks unsigned/tampered images on EKS, ECS, EC2, or Lambda — out of the box an unsigned image runs everywhere. Basic ECR scanning is shallow vs Inspector enhanced.

audit.how_to_check: aws ecr describe-registry / get-registry-scanning-configuration (expect ENHANCED/Inspector); confirm AWS Signer signing profiles and signatures on images; on EKS confirm a verification admission controller (Gatekeeper+Ratify or Kyverno with the AWS Signer plugin) is installed and enforcing; on ECS confirm a verification Lambda gate.
audit.pass_criterion: ECR enhanced scanning on; production images signed via AWS Signer; a deploy-time verification gate rejects unsigned/untrusted images on each compute platform that runs them.
audit.remediation: Turn on ECR enhanced scanning; sign images in CI with AWS Signer; install an admission controller on EKS and a lifecycle Lambda gate on ECS; document that raw EC2/Lambda container images need a CI-side verification step.

recommendations.title: Edge hardening for public AI/inference endpoints: WAF, rate-based rules, request validation, logging, and no unauthenticated Function URLs
recommendations.rationale: WAF is opt-in and must be explicitly associated; rate-based rules have a documented multi-minute detection lag (a real cost-abuse hole for GPU-backed inference); API Gateway access/exec logging and WAF logging are off by default; Lambda Function URL AuthType NONE is a one-toggle public endpoint.

audit.how_to_check: Confirm WAF web ACLs are associated with each public CloudFront/API Gateway/ALB; confirm rate-based rules (min limit as low as 10 since Aug 2024) and request validation; aws apigateway get-stage (access/exec logging on); confirm WAF logging destination; aws lambda get-function-url-config across functions (AuthType != NONE) and an SCP/guardrail blocking AuthType=NONE.
audit.pass_criterion: Every public endpoint has an associated WAF with rate-based + managed rules, request validation and access/WAF logging enabled, and no Lambda Function URL uses AuthType=NONE.
audit.remediation: Associate WAF with all public entry points; add rate-based rules sized to per-request GPU cost; enable API Gateway and WAF logging; add a guardrail denying Lambda Function URL AuthType=NONE; treat WAF rate limiting as one layer, not a hard ceiling.

recommendations.title: Bedrock/SageMaker AI controls: mandatory Guardrails, model-scoped IAM, PrivateLink, and VPC-only training
recommendations.rationale: Bedrock Guardrails are not enforced unless an IAM condition (bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier) requires them; model access and SageMaker network isolation are opt-in. For proprietary-IP workloads, prompt/response content is only captured if Bedrock model-invocation logging is enabled.

audit.how_to_check: Check IAM policies for a bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier condition forcing a guardrail on InvokeModel; confirm model-scoped IAM (specific model ARNs, not bedrock:*); confirm PrivateLink (interface endpoints) to Bedrock with endpoint policies; aws sagemaker describe-* for VPC-only/no-direct-internet training and endpoints; confirm Bedrock model-invocation logging to a controlled, access-restricted bucket where content forensics is required.
audit.pass_criterion: Bedrock access requires a guardrail via IAM condition and is scoped to specific models over PrivateLink; SageMaker training/endpoints are VPC-only with no direct internet; model-invocation logging is enabled where content-level forensics is needed.
audit.remediation: Add the bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier condition and model-scoped ARNs; deploy Bedrock interface endpoints with org-scoped endpoint policies; enforce SageMaker VPC config; enable model-invocation logging into a locked-down bucket.

recommendations.title: Tag governance and ABAC enforced by SCP, not Tag Policies alone
recommendations.rationale: Tag Policies standardize tag values but do NOT force required tags to exist at resource creation; without SCP enforcement, team/cost-center/data-classification tags that ABAC and cost/isolation rely on are silently absent.

audit.how_to_check: aws organizations describe-effective-policy --policy-type TAG_POLICY; confirm SCPs deny resource creation (e.g. ec2:RunInstances, s3 bucket creation) lacking aws:RequestTag/team & data-classification; sample resources for missing mandatory tags via Config.
audit.pass_criterion: Mandatory team and data-classification tags are enforced at creation by SCP conditions (aws:RequestTag/aws:TagKeys), and ABAC policies key off those tags; Config reports zero untagged IP-bearing resources.
audit.remediation: Pair Tag Policies (value hygiene) with SCPs that deny creation without the required tags; build ABAC permission sets on the enforced tags; add a Config rule flagging untagged resources.
